linkToCourtOrder.text: Montana - State Supreme Court|Montana - Supreme Court of Montana – (Chief Justice) Cory (James) Swanson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In State v. Stroup, 2026 Mont. LEXIS 650 (Mont. May 8, 2026), the defendant’s attorney filed a motion to dismiss that the defendant claimed mistakenly contained “appended research and work-product materials,” which the court determined were “implie[dly] . . . prepared with the use of [GenAI].” The filing lacked the necessary GenAI disclosure required by Local Rule 3(g). After counsel filed a corrected motion with a proper certification, the trial court vacated the defendant’s trial date sua sponte, rejected all defense filings, and characterized the conduct as reflecting not only the “inept use of GenAI but also an unacceptable level of practice before this [c]ourt.” The trial court also “took judicial notice that [defense] counsel had allegedly filed documents in two other cases before him that similarly failed to properly disclose her use of GenAI in their preparation.” The defendant then petitioned the Montana Supreme Court to disqualify the trial judge, arguing that the judge’s response to the GenAI disclosure violation evinced personal bias. The Supreme Court denied the petition, holding that the trial court’s decision to “impose sanctions of a kind permitted under Local Rule 3(g), when counsel admittedly violated Rule 3(g) on numerous occasions involving at least three separate cases,” did not demonstrate personal bias or prejudice sufficient to warrant disqualification.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Colo. – Judge Nina Y. Wang

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary:
In Coomer v. Lindell, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 128372 (D. Colo. July 7, 2025), counsel for the defendants included AI-generated citations in their opposition brief to the plaintiff’s omnibus motion in limine. During the final pre-trial conference, defense counsel admitted that they “ran [the brief] through AI” but “did not [cite] check” the result. Emphasizing that “Rule 11 applies to the use of artificial intelligence,” the court expressed skepticism that the incident was “simply an inadvertent error,” held that defense counsel violated Rule 11, and imposed joint and several sanctions of $3,000 on one attorney and his law firm, and individual sanctions of $3,000 on another attorney. Judge Wang declined to sanction the defendants themselves, however, as counsel “confirm[ed] that [he] did not advise” his clients that he used AI tools in his practice.

Then, in Coomer v. Lindell, Civil Action No. 22-cv-01129-NYW-SBP, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 101691 (D. Colo. May 7, 2026), the court observed that the defendants included in their post-trial motions a citation that listed the incorrect court and did not support the proposition for which it was cited. The court again ordered counsel to show cause. In response, the attorney maintained that he did not use GenAI and that “the error was minor,” And that the “circumstances at issue here are not . . . the same type of AI generated error . . . that was the subject of that earlier sanction.” The court disagreed that his error was not serious, attributing its pattern to a “form of hallucination that has already occurred in this case,” and reasoned that a greater sanction than previously was needed to deter this misconduct. The court imposed a $5,000 sanction.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Federal District Court|Arizona - D. Ariz. – (Judge) Krissa (M.) Lanham

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Burris v. Cty. of Pinal, No. CV-26-01316-PHX-KML, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 101155 (D. Ariz. May 7, 2026), the court reminded the pro se plaintiff to “comply with [the court’s] requirements for litigants using artificial intelligence.” The plaintiff continued to file documents which led the court to believe that he “continue[d] to ignore the court's requirements regarding the use of [GenAI]” because he did not file a declaration identifying “which portions of the filing incorporate [GenAI] outputs.” Judge Lanham warned that future violations may result in filings “summarily stricken with no additional warning.”  The court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss and granted the plaintiff’s motion to amend on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Georgia - Federal District Court (Middle District)|Georgia - Middle District of Georgia – (Judge) Marc (T.) Treadwell

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Kelly v. City of Cochran, No. 5:26-cv-49 (M.D. Ga. May 7, 2026), the court generally reminded the pro se plaintiff of his Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 obligations and issued rules on GenAI use. Specifically, Judge Kelly ordered the plaintiff to “disclose the use of AI in all pleadings filed with the Court.” For the complaint and all future pleadings, the court required that the plaintiff file “a signed statement disclosing whether Plaintiff used AI to prepare or draft the complaint,” and must certify that he “has verified the accuracy of all facts and all legal authorities cited in the complaint” if he uses AI. Judge Treadwell warned that failure to comply would result in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: New Mexico - Federal District Court|New Mexico - D.N.M. – (Judge) Judith (C.) Herrera

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Neri v. Bd. of Educ. for Albuquerque Pub. Sch., No. 1:19-cv-00008-JCH-SCY, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 101246 (D.N.M. May 7, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited a nonexistent case. The court reminded the plaintiff that to the extent that the citation was the result of GenAI misuse, courts “courts ordinarily do not coddle a plaintiff who cites to fake, nonexistent, or misleading authorities,” cautioned her against using GenAI to draft her pleadings, and warned it may impose sanctions for future misconduct. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion for reconsideration on other grounds.

court: North Dakota - Supreme Court of North Dakota

linkToCourtOrder.text: North Dakota - State Supreme Court|North Dakota - Supreme Court of North Dakota – (Chief Justice) Lisa (Fair) McEvers|(Justice) Jerod (E.) Tufte|(Justice) Jon (J.) Jensen|(Justice) Douglas (Alan) Bahr|(Justice) Mark (Allan) Friese

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Stokka v. Stokka, 2026 ND 94 (N.D. May 7, 2026), the pro se defendant-appellant filed an appellate brief that lacked references to the evidentiary record and contained legal authorities that were “either irrelevant, fictitious, or nonexistent.” Citing its own recent precedent explaining that “fake or nonexistent legal citations typically are the result of [GenAI] hallucinations,” the court found that the brief failed to comply with the “minimum requirements” of N.D.R.App.P. 28 (governing citation to authority in appellate briefs and other procedural appellate brief requirements) and summarily affirmed the judgment under N.D.R.App.P. 35.1(a)(8) (governing the affirmance of a lower court ruling by summary opinion). The court awarded the appellee “double costs and attorney’s fees in the amount of $1,000.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: Oklahoma - Federal District Court (Western District)|Oklahoma - W.D. Okla. – (Judge) David () Russell

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Crist v. West, No. CIV-25-1446-R, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 101284 (W.D. Okla. May 7, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel included “nonexistent cases and citation to authority that does not stand for the proposition indicated” in her response brief. Counsel apologized for using a “trusted legal vendor’s AI tool” to draft without verifying the citations. The court determined that counsel violated Fed. Civ. R. 11 and issued the order as a “public reprimand,” ordered her to notify her client of the order, and imposed a $250 sanction.

linkToCourtOrder.text: California - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|California - E.D. Cal. – (Magistrate Judge) Chi (Soo) Kim

results.summary: In Tracy Dirt Pit, LLC v. Cnty. of San Joaquin, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 100543 (E.D. Cal. May 6, 2026), Magistrate Judge Chi Soo Kim issued a pretrial scheduling order that includes a provision requiring each party to “disclose any exhibit that was generated or created with the use of artificial intelligence (AI)” in their exhibit lists filed with the joint pretrial statement.

linkToCourtOrder.text: California - Federal District Court (Northern District)|California - Northern District of California – (Magistrate Judge) Thomas (S.) Hixson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Gregoire v. S.F. Bay Area Rapid Transit Dist., No. 25-cv-02150-TSH, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 100834 (N.D. Cal. May 6, 2026), the court was “unable to locate three of the four cases cited” in the plaintiff’s opposition to the defendant’s motion for terminating sanctions. The court suspected GenAI misuse, and personally sanctioned the plaintiff’s counsel $1,000. Magistrate Judge Hixson ordered counsel to give her client the order and to participate in a CLE course related to ethical GenAI use. The court also ordered the clerk to serve the order on the State Bar of California.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Eastern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: New York - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|New York - Eastern District of New York – (Judge) Hector () Gonzalez

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Norris v. JPMorgan Chase Bank N.A., No. 25-CV-03231 (HG), 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 100628 (E.D.N.Y. May 6, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited nonexistent cases and included nonexistent quotations in his opposition. The court declined to impose any penalties, but warned that further citations to nonexistent cases may result in sanctions. Judge Gonzalez granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.

court: North Carolina - State Intermediate Appeals Court
North Carolina - Court of Appeals of North Carolina

linkToCourtOrder.text: North Carolina - State Intermediate Appeals Court|North Carolina - Court of Appeals of North Carolina – (Judge) Donna () Stroud|(Judge) John () Arrowood|(Judge) April () Wood

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Robinson v. Davidson Cty., No. COA 25-971, 2026 N.C. App. LEXIS 350 (Ct. App. May 6, 2026), the court “note[d] many concerns with Petitioner's brief that raise the question of whether this brief was generated, at least in part, by artificial intelligence,” such as the inclusion of nonexistent quotations. The court warned that “counsel is responsible for the ultimate content of their work” and that failure to comply with the Rules of Appellate Procedure and Court rules may result in sanctions. The court affirmed the trial court’s judgment on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Georgia - State Supreme Court|Georgia - Supreme Court of Georgia – (Justice) Benjamin (Arthur) Land

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Payne v. State, No. S26A0459, 2026 Ga. LEXIS 136 (May 5, 2026), the assistant district attorney included nine citations that do not exist or “do not stand for the proposition asserted” in her response to the defendant’s motion for a new trial. The order denying the defendant’s motion (which was “largely prepared by [the ADA]”) and the ADA’s response to the defendant’s appeal also included citation errors. The ADA admitted to using GenAI in her briefs and proposed order without verification. She also “withdrew reliance” on nine citations in her appellate brief. The court relied on its inherent authority and Georgia Supreme Court Rule 7 to issue sanctions. The court admonished the ADA and the DA’s Office, suspended the ADA from practice in the Supreme Court of Georgia for six months, and required that the ADA complete 12 additional hours of CLE on GenAI in order to be reinstated. The court vacated the trial court’s order and remanded the case to the trial court for a new order that “shall not contain any fictitious or misattributed case citations” and “shall not be prepared by counsel for either party.” The court also clarified its prior order and required counsel for the State to produce “all communications it had with the trial court concerning the proposed order denying [the defendant’s] motion for a new trial.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: Hawaii - Federal District Court|Hawaii - D. Haw. –  (Judge) Michael () Seabright

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Moore v. MC Architects Inc., No. 24-00367 JMS-KJM, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 98836 (D. Haw. May 5, 2026) the court observed that plaintiff’s opposition filings contained “a number of record-and case-citation issues.” During the show cause hearing, counsel admitted that his client “had taken a lead role in preparing the oppositions and had used AI tools in the process of doing so,” And accepted “full responsibility” for the errors. The court therefore imposed $1,000 in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Maine - Federal District Court|Maine - D. Me. – (Judge) Stacey (D.) Neumann

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Fuller v. Hyde Sch., No. 2:25-cv-00354-SDN, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 98656 (D. Me. May 5, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel “submit[ed] two filings to the Court which used [GenAI] to generate erroneous legal citations.” In response to the order to show cause, counsel admitted to using GenAI for drafting without verifying each citation. Counsel informed the court that she had since apologized to her client, retained new local counsel, and implemented “additional firm oversight” and “new policies on AI use and disclosure” at her firm. The court considered counsel’s acceptance of responsibility, lack of prior disciplinary violations, and willingness to implement safeguards, and determined the appropriate sanction should be “focused on education, process improvement, and public admonition, rather than a severe monetary or disciplinary penalty.” The court admonished counsel and the Bar more broadly, and ordered counsel to attend CLE courses on GenAI, create “clear procedures” at her firm to ensure no repeat violations and provide the order to her client. Judge Neumann also struck the plaintiff’s response in opposition to the motion to dismiss and ordered counsel to file an amended response.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Nevada - Federal District Court|Nevada - D. Nev. – (Judge) Jennifer (A.) Dorsey

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Seeto v. Kendall, No. 2:25-cv-00038-JAD-EJY, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 98557 (D. Nev. May 5, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s brief included “quotations or propositions that the cited authorities do not appear to support.” The court warned that whether the errors were the result of “carelessness, reliance on a secondary source without checking the original authority, or unverified reliance on an AI research or drafting tool,” repeated failure to verify accuracy of citations may result in sanctions. The court ultimately declined to impose sanctions at this time.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Eastern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: New York - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|New York - Eastern District of New York – (Magistrate Judge) Steven (I.) Locke

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Nevels v. TransUnion, LLC, No. 25-CV-3446(GRB)(SIL), 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 99537 (E.D.N.Y. May 5, 2026), the pro se plaintiff relied on nonexistent authority in her opposition to the defendant’s motion to dismiss. Though the court suspected GenAI misuse, it declined to sanction the plaintiff because the citations “d[id] not impact the Court’s analysis.” However, the court warned that future fabricated citations will result in sanctions. Magistrate Judge Locke recommended that the defendant’s motion to dismiss be granted in part and denied in part on other grounds.

court: Texas - Court of Appeals of Texas, Seventh District

linkToCourtOrder.text: State Intermediate Appeals Court|Texas - Court of Appeals of Texas, Seventh District –  (Judge) Alex () Yarbrough

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Adams v. Allen Butler Constr., Inc., No. 07-25-00191-CV, 2026 Tex. App. LEXIS 4236 (Tex. App. May 5, 2026), the court found that the pro se appellant included “several citations [] that appear to be non-existent,” “fictitious quotations [] attributed to real cases,” and “inaccurate descriptions of case holdings and mischaracterizations of the clerk’s and reporter’s records.” The court warned that “further blatant misrepresentations of case law” may result in sanctions, and that GenAI use is not an excuse for such errors. The court also found that the appellant waived her claims because she failed to present existing caselaw in support, thus meaning that the appellant’s arguments “present[] nothing for our review,” resulting in waiver. The court affirmed the trial court’s judgment.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Texas - Federal District Court (Southern District)|Texas - Southern District of Texas – (Magistrate Judge) Dena (Hanovice) Palermo

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Phillips v. Equifax Info. Servs., LLC, No. 4:25-CV-04227, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 98712 (S.D. Tex. May 5, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited an authority to support a proposition for which it did not stand. The court warned that Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 applies to “all who present writings to the Court,” that it obligates parties—by filing—to certify their arguments are warranted by existing law, and that violation of the Rule could result in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 11th Circuit - Federal Court of Appeals|11th Circuit - 11th Cir. – (Chief Judge) William (Holcombe) Pryor|(Judge) Kevin (Christopher) Newsom|(Judge) Andrew (Lynn) Brasher| (Magistrate Judge) Patricia (D.) Barksdale

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Williamson v. TransUnion LLC, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 12852 (11th Cir. May 4, 2026), the Eleventh Circuit affirmed the dismissal with prejudice of the pro se plaintiff’s amended complaint as a sanction for his repeated misconduct. In front of the district court, the plaintiff cited nonexistent cases found through GenAI, misquoted and misrepresented actual cases, and continuously violated court rules and orders despite multiple warnings and show-cause hearings. At the district court’s second show-cause hearing, the plaintiff admitted that “some of the cases he cited did not exist, and that he had found them using [GenAI].” The magistrate judge found that the plaintiff’s errors were “‘some of the most egregious things’” she had seen from a pro se litigant and that she “had given him many chances to comply with the Local Rules and court orders.” The magistrate judge issued a report and recommendation finding that the plaintiff’s “repeated citation to nonexistent cases and misquotation and misrepresentation of cases and holdings, intentional misrepresentation of facts, and continuous violation of rules and court orders” warranted dismissal with prejudice, as “no lesser sanction would suffice.” Notably, in a post-judgment order, Williamson v. TransUnion LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 101962 (M.D. Fla. May 8, 2026), the magistrate judge observed that the plaintiff’s continued filings contained no citations to authority, suggesting he “continues to use [GenAI] to prepare the papers he files in this court, but now avoids citing non-existent cases by citing no cases at all.” The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the magistrate judge’s recommendation for dismissal, holding that the district court properly exercised its discretion under its “inherent power to manage its docket” and that the plaintiff’s misconduct was willful.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Utah - Federal District Court|Utah - D. Utah – (Magistrate Judge) Cecilia (M.) Romero

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Wilkes v. Canyons Sch. Dist., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 99436 (D. Utah May 4, 2026), the attorneys for the plaintiffs cited nonexistent cases in their amended complaint. At the order to show cause hearing, counsel admitted that GenAI was used to “polish/refine” their writing and claimed that the software “must have, for reasons unknown to counsel, distorted the cases.” The court was “skeptical” of this explanation, noting that the holdings of the “correct” cases counsel identified did not match the holdings cited in the complaint. The court found that counsel violated their “professional responsibilities under Rule 11(b) by failing to reasonably verify the existence and accuracy of the non-existent cases.” The court also noted that this was not the first time the same counsel had been admonished for improper GenAI use in this district.  As a sanction, the court ordered counsel to pay $7,000 to reimburse the defendants’ legal expenses, finding that the monetary sanction, combined with an outstanding CLE requirement from the prior matter, was “sufficient to deter repetition of the conduct or comparable conduct by others similarly situated.” The court granted partial dismissal of the amended complaint on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Florida - Federal District Court (Middle District)|Florida - M.D. Fla. – (Judge) Kathryn (Kimball) Mizelle

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Smith v. Verra Mobility Corp., No. 8:25-cv-2300-KKM-NHA, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 96040 (M.D. Fla. May 1, 2026), the counsel for one of the defendants “included several misrepresentations of caselaw and citations to non-existent cases” in his motion to dismiss. In response to the order to show cause, the attorney admitted to using GenAI, “including ChatGPT,” without verifying each citation. The attorney sought to withdraw the citations, and assured the court that going forward he would “ensure that every cited authority is personally reviewed, accurately described, and independently verified before filing.” In determining sanctions, Judge Mizelle weighed the violation of the attorney’s Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 duties against the attorney’s “immediate and unqualified acceptance of responsibility” and his “plan to implement corrective measures.” The court therefore imposed a “minimal sanction[],” ordering only that the attorney inform his client of the show cause order.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Kentucky - State Intermediate Appeals Court|Kentucky - Court of Appeals (Chief Judge) Larry (E.) Thompson|(Judge) Susanne (Mowery) Cetrulo|(Judge) Annete () Karem

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Ali v. Idiokitas, 2026 Ky. App. Unpub. LEXIS 276 (Ky. Ct. App. May 1, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed an appellate brief that contained two hallucinated case citations. The court found it “obvious” that the plaintiff “used [GenAI] to write her brief,” as an “exhaustive search of caselaw, both published and unpublished, yielded no results” for either citation. The court noted that had “the brief . . . been filed by a licensed attorney” it “would not hesitate to impose monetary sanctions,” and emphasized that a “litigant’s pro se status does not make them immune to sanctions by the court.” Nevertheless, the court chose to review the merits but warned the plaintiff that “any future non-compliant filings with this Court, or any other level of court, may be met with severe penalties.” The court affirmed the denial of the plaintiff’s brief on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: New Mexico - Federal District Court|New Mexico - D.N.M. – (Magistrate Judge) Jerry (H.) Ritter

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Carter v. Uzglobal LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 96883 (D.N.M. May 1, 2026), the attorney for the plaintiffs filed a response brief and two motions to compel that collectively contained sixteen hallucinated cases. The court found that an associate attorney at counsel’s firm had drafted the filings using GenAI, and that the signing attorney “reviewed” the briefs for “writing style, argument, etc.” but “did not verify the cases cited.” The court noted that opposing counsel had notified plaintiffs that they had cited an “unlocatable case” in April 2025, yet the signing attorney “proceeded to file the two compromised motions to compel in June” without reviewing her briefs until “alerted a second time by another attorney.” The court found that the signing attorney violated Rule 11, holding that “submitting documents with AI-hallucinated cases is objectively unreasonable” and that an attorney “cannot delegate her obligations under Rule 11.” The court imposed sanctions, but noted that the attorney “did not attempt to avoid responsibility,” “self-reported to the New Mexico state bar,” took a CLE course, and “offered to pay reasonable attorney fees.” As sanctions, the court ordered the attorney to self-report to the Texas state bar and disciplinary boards, to update her New Mexico state bar reporting with a copy of this order, and pay a $1,000 fine to the Clerk of Court, submit proof of attendance at an AI-focused CLE course. The court also permitted the defendants to move for an award of attorney fees and costs.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Maryland - Federal District Court|Maryland - D. Md. – (Judge) Julie (R.) Rubin (Apr. 30, 2026) GenAI | Filings/Drafting | Warning

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Johnson v. Nationstar Mortg., LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 94898 (D. Md. Apr. 30, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a motion that cited a nonexistent case. The court noted that the citation “yields no results” when entered into legal databases and that it “appears the citation may be a ‘hallucinated’ case citation derived from the use of a [GenAI] tool.” Citing its own recent precedent addressing GenAI use by pro se parties, the court observed that “[a]lthough the use of [Gen]AI tools for legal research or drafting is not inherently improper,” parties “bear responsibility for the filings they sign, regardless of actual authorship,” including “the duty to include accurate factual and legal citations.” The court declined to issue a Rule 11 show cause order in this instance but “strongly cautioned” the plaintiff that similar conduct in the future may prompt the court to issue such a show cause order. The court denied the preliminary injunction on other grounds.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Eastern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: New York - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|New York - E.D.N.Y. – (Magistrate Judge) James (M.) Wicks

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Superb Motors Inc. v. Deo, No. 23-CV-6188 (JMW), 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 95581 (E.D.N.Y. Apr. 30, 2026), a pro se defendant, who is New York barred attorney, included non-existent citations in his opposition to the plaintiffs’ motion to vacate the preliminary injunction. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the defendant denied using GenAI, instead blaming “computer ‘viruses and malware’ for the fake citations” and asserting that “he ‘was completely fooled by whoever infiltrated [his] computer’ and he will now ‘be vigilant in trying to avoid a repeat going forward.’” The court noted that the defendant was bound by both Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and the New York Rules of Professional Conduct, and that “lawyers by now should know better than submit briefs without cite-checking authority.” Magistrate Judge Wicks ultimately found that the defendant’s conduct amounted to bad faith under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 because he clearly “did not check his citations or brief entirely” before filing, and because courts in the Second Circuit have “repeatedly found that presenting AI-generated hallucinations as valid caselaw constitutes subjective bad faith,” allowing for the court to issue sanctions sua sponte. The court considered that the defendant “acknowledge[d] the severity” of his conduct and promised to be more careful moving forward and imposed a “lower” sanction of $1,500.

Mindful of the plaintiff’s pro se status, the court declined to impose sanctions but warned that “any further misconduct in the future will not be tolerated.” The court granted the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Butler v. Fid. Mgmt. & Rsch. Co. LLC (FMR), 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 95184 (S.D.N.Y. Apr. 30, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a letter requesting an administrative correction to the docket that contained “five citations to cases that clearly do not support the proposition for which they are cited; quotations from three cases that do not appear in the cases cited; and one citation to a nonexistent judicial opinion.” The court stated that it “believe[d] that these issues are due to Plaintiff's use of [Gen]AI tools,” and emphasized that “presentation of false citations, quotations, and holdings by a party . . . to the Court is sanctionable conduct.” However, because the plaintiff was proceeding pro se, the court declined to pursue sanctions. Nevertheless, the court cautioned the plaintiff that future filing of false citations, quotations, or holdings may result in sanctions, and reminded her that pro se litigants “are obligated to review the cases cited in their filings to confirm that they exist, stand for the proposition for which they are cited, and contain any quotations attributed to them.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: Pennsylvania - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|Pennsylvania - E.D. Pa. – (Judge) Harvey () Bartle III

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Berry v. Early Warning Servs., LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 94915 (E.D. Pa. Apr. 30, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed an opposition to the defendant’s motion to dismiss that contained nonexistent case citations, fabricated quotations from real cases, and unsupported legal arguments “attributed to real cases.” The court stated that the plaintiff “undoubtedly used artificial intelligence to craft his opposition” and submitted “numerous false citations,” including cases that do not exist. Noting that pro se litigants “cannot flout procedural rules” but “must abide by the same rules that apply to all other litigants,” the court “strongly caution[ed] [the plaintiff] that any use of fictitious caselaw in the future may lead to severe sanctions under Rule 11.” The court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Arizona - Federal District Court|Arizona - D. Ariz. – (Judge) Diane (J.) Humetewa

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Adams v. Kiewit Infrastructure W. Co., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 93911 (D. Ariz. Apr. 29, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a motion which contained multiple procedural deficiencies, including failure to comply with local rules and failure to meet and confer with the defendants. The filing also contained hallucinated case citations, which “allow[ed] the Court to infer that Plaintiff prepared her filing with the use of [GenAI].” The court noted that while GenAI use “is not necessarily problematic, the failure to confirm the accuracy of the filing is,” and that Rule 11 requires parties to verify accuracy regardless of pro se status. The court warned that the plaintiff is “now on notice that the use of hallucinated cases or the filing of documents with related inaccuracies will result in sanctions.” The court struck the motion in part because of the GenAI hallucinations.

linkToCourtOrder.text: California - Federal District Court (Northern District)|California - N.D. Cal. – (Magistrate Judge) Joseph (C.) Spero

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

Apr. 29, 2026), the attorney for the defendant filed an opposition motion that misstated the court's Local Rules, claiming a meet-and-confer requirement that does not exist under the cited rule. The court warned that “[w]hether counsel’s misrepresentation is the result of hallucination by [GenAI] or lack of diligence, further such misstatements of the law will likely result in the imposition of sanctions by the Court.” The court granted the plaintiff’s motion on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Kansas - Federal District Court|Kansas - D. Kan. – (Chief Judge) John (W.) Broomes

Apr. 29, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel included in the complaint three quotes that were “not present in the cited cases,” leading the court to “strongly suspect[]” GenAI misuse. The court granted the defendants’ motion to dismiss in part and denied the plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction and to amend his complaint on other grounds.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)|New York - S.D.N.Y. – (Magistrate Judge) Gabriel (W.) Gorenstein

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Jimenez-Fogarty v. Fogarty, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 94006 (S.D.N.Y. Apr. 29, 2026), the attorney for the plaintiff filed two memoranda of law opposing motions to dismiss that contained numerous fabricated case citations. The court ordered the attorney to show cause and to provide “a complete and detailed description of the process of the drafting of the two memoranda of law” and the role of “any individual or computer system” involved. However, the court observed that the attorney’s response “offers only airy generalities and conclusory statements” and “contains no coherent explanation for how the two memoranda of law came to contain the fabricated citations,” variously attributing them to “typographical errors, misreading of secondary sources, or limitations in the search functionality of legal databases,” after only later suggesting that LexisNexis’s “[Gen]AI-driven features . . . may very well have” generated them. The court concluded that the attorney “used a[] [GenAI] program that generated the fabricated citations” and “failed to check that these citations were genuine,” in violation of Rule 11’s requirements. The court found, by clear and convincing evidence, that the attorney “acted in bad faith,” citing the attorney’s “obdurate refusal to explain how the citations came to be included in her briefs,” her “complete lack of acceptance of responsibility,” and the fact that she filed additional briefs in other courts containing fabricated citations even after the court had issued its orders to show cause, including one in the Second Circuit that resulted in a referral to the court’s Grievance Panel. As sanctions, the court imposed a $2,500 fine under Rule 11, ordered the attorney to supply a copy of the opinion to her client, and directed her to alert the presiding judge in each pending case where she appears as attorney of record.

linkToCourtOrder.text: California - Federal District Court (Northern District)|California - N.D. Cal. – (Magistrate Judge) Peter (H.) Kang

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hill v. Workday, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 93600 (N.D. Cal. Apr. 28, 2026), the attorneys for the plaintiff filed a joint discovery letter brief that cited at least one nonexistent case, caused in part by use of a GenAI legal research tool. In the first order, signed on September 5, 2025 , the court sanctioned the drafting attorney, who had used a GenAI tool (Westlaw’s CoCounsel) and delegated citation review to a paralegal without personally verifying the citations. The court admonished the attorney, ordered her to take four hours of CLE courses, and ordered a firm-wide circulation of the order. The court also admonished the supervising attorney—the firm’s namesake and principal—for failing to appear at the show cause hearing or file any response and ordered him to submit a declaration addressing his supervisory responsibilities. In a second order, signed on April 28, 2026, the court sanctioned the supervising attorney after he attested that he “did not conduct the research nor contribute to the drafting” of the brief and therefore did not believe the original order to show cause “was directed to” him, failed to appear at the first hearing, and failed to respond to subsequent orders until a further order to show cause was issued. The court held that “managerial and senior lawyers have professional and ethical duties to the Court, their clients, and their employees to appropriately supervise the work performed” and that the duty to check filings for accuracy “is nondelegable to other attorneys or to technology.” As sanctions, the court ordered the supervising attorney to circulate all show cause documents to firm staff, four hours of live, in-person CLE (two hours on supervision of junior attorneys, two hours on ethical use of AI), distribute the CLE materials firmwide, and to pay $1,001 in personal monetary sanctions to the court.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Montana – Federal District Court|Montana - D. Mont. – (Judge) Donald (W.) Molloy

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Thornton v. Brandon (In re Thorco, Inc.), No. CV 25-219-M-DWM, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 92207 (D. Mont. Apr. 27, 2026), the plaintiffs-appellants appealed orders imposing sanctions for an “unrelenting campaign of vexatious litigation” in a bankruptcy proceeding. The vexatious litigation tactics included numerous filings that the bankruptcy court noted “appear to be prepared a least in part using [GenAI] and that contained inappropriate ‘hallucinated’ citations.” The court granted the appellees’ motion to dismiss on jurisdictional and justiciability grounds. The court declined to award attorney’s fees and costs because it determined that the appeal was not frivolous.

court: North Carolina - Federal District Court (Eastern District)
judge: Magistrate Judge Robert T. Numbers, II

linkToCourtOrder.text: North Carolina - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|North Carolina - E.D.N.C. – (Magistrate Judge) Robert (T.) Numbers, II

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Fivehouse v. United States DOD, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 93026 (E.D.N.C. Apr. 27, 2026), the Assistant U.S. Attorney filed a response brief that “contained fabricated quotations and misstatements of case holdings” generated by GenAI. The DOJ attorney initially attributed the errors to the “inadvertent filing of an unfinalized draft document” and made no mention of GenAI in his surreply. Only after the court issued an order to show cause and directly questioned him about each fabrication at the hearing did the AUSA admit that he “used artificial intelligence to catch . . . back up” after overwriting a prior draft, but “did not go back and verify the accuracy of the quotations.” The court found by clear and convincing evidence that the AUSA “knowingly and intentionally submitted a document to the Court that contained fabricated quotations and misstatements of case holdings,” constituting “an abuse of the judicial process that is utterly inconsistent with the orderly administration of justice.” The court emphasized that Renfer’s “posthoc acknowledgment . . . [was] inadequate and demonstrate[d] that his conduct was knowing and deliberate,” and noted that his misdeeds were “particularly odious” because he committed them while serving in “a position of immense trust and power” as an AUSA. Exercising its inherent authority, the court publicly reprimanded the attorney, finding this sanction sufficient in light of the professional consequences he had already suffered, including the loss of his position, national press coverage, and an investigation from the Department of Justice’s Office of Professional Responsibility, but cautioned that courts should begin “moving beyond admonitions and reprimands into more punitive sanctions” to deter similar conduct. The attorney is no longer serving as an AUSA.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Pennsylvania - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|Pennsylvania - E.D. Pa. – (Judge) Joseph (F.) Leeson Jr.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In BMO Bank, N.A. v. DePano III et al., No. 5:26-cv-2753 (E.D. Pa. Apr. 27, 2026), Judge Leeson issued an Initial Procedural Order requiring any party, “whether appearing pro se or through counsel,” who utilizes any GenAI tool “in the preparation of any document” filed before the court to include a Certificate of Use of Generative AI (i) identifying the specific AI tool used, (ii) disclosing “the portions of the filing prepared by the AI program,” and (iii) a certification that “a person has checked the accuracy of any portion of the document generated by AI, including all citations and legal authority.” The use of the phrase “preparation” (as opposed to “filing” or “drafting”) could indicate that disclosure and verification are required when AI tools are used in any part of the preparatory process, including research. The order also directs parties to review the “Joint Formal Opinion of the Pennsylvania Bar Association and Philadelphia Bar Association regarding the use of Artificial Intelligence” and to be mindful of their ethical and professional obligations. The order warns that failure to comply may result in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Alabama - State Supreme Court|Alabama - Supreme Court of Alabama – (Justice) Chris () McCool

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Ibach v. Stewart, No. SC-2025-0106, 2026 Ala. LEXIS 45 (Apr. 24, 2026), the attorney for the appellant filed briefs that “contain[ed] an astounding number of invalid, inaccurate, and irrelevant citations to legal authorities.”  In response to the order to show cause, the attorney moved to withdraw from the case.  He admitted to GenAI use, apologized, accepted responsibility, and “conceded that sanctions were appropriate.”  He also self-reported to the Alabama State Bar and reimbursed the appellee’s counsel for “the fees and costs incurred in responding to the fabricated legal authorities.”  In deciding whether to impose sanctions, the court noted that the attorney “appears . . . remorseful,” but ultimately reasoned that “regardless of whether [the attorney] included the AI hallucinations intentionally, recklessly, or negligently, the result is the same -- the wasteful expenditure of precious resources by both the opposing party and the courts.”  The court concluded that the briefs were “wholly inadequate under Rule 28, Ala. R. App. P” because they failed to support their legal arguments.  The court dismissed the appeal and denied the motion to file supplemental briefs, as “the Alabama Rules of Appellate Procedure do not contemplate allowing a party whose attorney has filed a poor brief to simply file a new brief . . . after the briefing period has closed.”  The court additionally concluded that the appeal was frivolous and thus subject to sanctions under Rule 38, Ala. R. App. P.  The court ordered the attorney to pay $17,200 in attorney fees and costs to the appellee’s attorney (including any amount that has already been paid) and double costs to the court.  The court also prohibited the attorney from filing any other documents in the court unless another attorney in good standing signs the filing and referred him to the Alabama State Bar.  The court also granted the attorney’s motion to withdraw.

linkToCourtOrder.text: California - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|California - E.D. Cal. – (Magistrate Judge) Dennis (M.) Cota

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Geddes v. Loancare, LLC, No. 2:25-cv-02955-DMC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 88944 (E.D. Cal. Apr. 22, 2026) the pro se plaintiff “manufactured two significant quotes that favor her position” in her jurisdiction supplemental briefing.  The court noted that the plaintiff is held to a higher standard than “ordinary pro se litigants” because she is a California-barred attorney.  Magistrate Judge Cota found that the plaintiff’s choice to put quotation marks around only some words indicated “deliberate misdirection” and rejected the plaintiff’s contention that the quotations were mistakes and reflected “paraphrased summaries.”  The court expressed concern about potential GenAI use without “the appropriate due diligence and verification” and reminded the plaintiff that she “bears the responsibility” for her choice.  Magistrate Judge Cota also considered that the plaintiff “double[d] down” on the error and failed to provide “any satisfactory explanation after being given the opportunity to respond.”  The court imposed a $1,000 sanction and ordered her to self-report to the California Bar Association.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Michigan - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|Michigan - E.D. Mich. – (Judge) Susan (K.) DeClercq

results.summary: In Jones v. Delta Air Lines, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 88833 (E.D. Mich. Apr. 22, 2026), the pro se plaintiff admitted during her deposition to having ChatGPT open on her device and using it to assist her in knowing how to “proceed legally as a pro se representation.” When asked whether she was feeding information into the platform during the deposition, the plaintiff refused to answer, “citing attorney/client privilege as the reason,” despite confirming she was not represented by counsel. The court held an emergency status conference and instructed the plaintiff that she could not “use ChatGPT or any other [Gen]AI platform, any other person, [or] any other anything to assist [her] in answering questions” during her deposition, and that she could not “refuse to answer questions on the grounds of attorney/client privilege” because no attorney was present on her behalf, and the plaintiff is not herself an attorney. The court ultimately dismissed the case with prejudice because of the plaintiff’s repeated failure to participate in the discovery process.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Oregon - State Intermediate Appeals Court|Oregon - Court of Appeals – (Presiding Judge) James (C.) Egan|(Chief Judge) Erin (C.) Lagesen|(Judge) Anna (M.) Joyce

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Williams v. Honl, 2026 Ore. App. LEXIS 566 (Ore. App. Apr. 22, 2026), the appellant’s attorney filed an opening brief that “contain[ed] fabricated quotations and propositions of law falsely attributed to existing cases.” Six out of the nine cases referenced in the brief were observed as containing either quotations that do not exist, or “propositions of law not plausibly attributable to them.” After receiving the respondent’s answering brief, the attorney moved to file an amended brief but did not disclose the GenAI issue; the court found that the motion “lacked the candor that we expect from lawyers.” In response to the court’s show cause order, the attorney acknowledged that “generative artificial intelligence was used as a research and drafting aid, and quotations and legal propositions were not independently verified against the cited cases before filing.” The court struck the opening brief, permitted the filing of an amended brief with a certification that counsel did not use GenAI to draft the brief and has personally verified all cited authority, and sanctioned the appellant’s attorneys $8,044.25 in attorney fees payable to the respondent for the costs of responding to the fabricated authority and the show cause order. The court declined to impose additional sanctions payable to the court or to dismiss the appeal, finding the attorney fees award sufficient and expressing confidence that the conduct would not recur.

court: Puerto Rico - Tribunal Supremo de Puerto Rico

linkToCourtOrder.text: Puerto Rico - State Supreme Court|Puerto Rico - Tribunal Supremo de Puerto Rico – (Judge) Virgilio (Mainardi) Peralta|(Judge) Luis (M. Ortega) García|(Judge) Alfredo III (López) Garay

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: [Spanish-language decision] In In re Irizarry Centeno, 2026 TSPR 41 (TSPR Apr. 21, 2026), two attorneys for the defendant submitted a motion to dismiss containing multiple nonexistent citations.  After the plaintiff’s attorney pointed out the errors, the defendant’s attorneys acknowledged the errors but maintained that it was not made in bad faith.  They later supplemented the filing with valid citations.  The Tribunal de Primera Instancia (TPI) determined that all attorneys must ensure that their citations are accurate, and imposed $1,000 in sanctions for causing unjustified delays.  The defendant’s attorneys withdrew as counsel and paid their sanction.  TPI also referred the case to the Puerto Rico Supreme Court (Tribunal Supremo de Puerto Rico, or TSPR) for disciplinary proceedings.  TSPR found that the conduct violated PR Code of Professional Ethics Canon 18 (duty of competence); Canon 35 (duty of sincerity and honesty); Canon 38 (honor and dignity of the legal profession) and Rule 9.1 of Civil Procedure.  The court found that reprimand was the appropriate additional sanction, but warned that the attorneys may be subject to more severe sanctions should they engage in further unethical conduct.

court: Texas - Court of Appeals of Texas, Eighth District

linkToCourtOrder.text: Texas - State Intermediate Appeals Court|Texas - Court of Appeals of Texas, Eighth District – (Chief Justice) Salas () Mendoza|(Justice) Gina (M.) Palafox|(Justice) Lisa () Soto

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: 439 Viewmont Road, Germantown, NY

linkToCourtOrder.text: Federal District Court (Eastern District)|Pennsylvania - E.D. Pa. – (Judge) Kai (Niambi) Scott

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Bunce v. Visual Tech. Innovations, Inc., No. 23-1740, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 86427 (E.D. Pa. Apr. 20, 2026), the plaintiffs pointed out that the defendants’ attorney had previously been sanctioned for “his use of made-up cases and other authorities that did not support his propositions,” (ECF 154) and repeated the same conduct in his omnibus motion seeking sanctions and objecting to the plaintiffs’ travel costs.  The court ordered the defendants’ attorney to pay the plaintiffs’ reasonable travel costs, denied his omnibus motion, and ordered him to show cause why he should not be sanctioned for submitting erroneous citations in violation of Rule 11(b) and the Court’s Standing Order Re: Artificial Intelligence.  After a hearing, Judge Scott determined that the defendants’ attorney submitted a document drafted by GenAI, failed to verify, and “when challenged by opposing counsel, deflected by trying to blame opposing counsel, doubled down on his conduct, and argued that the Court's Standing Order did not clearly specify the need for ‘human verification only.’”   The court was unconvinced by the attorney’s explanation that his mistakes were due to “the fact that he does not normally practice law,” but instead “primarily focuses on ‘business stuff.’”  Judge Scott was also unpersuaded by the attorney’s request to impose lower sanctions because he had already suffered from “fees and [] reputational harm.”  The court imposed a penalty of $5,000, double the penalty it had imposed for the attorney’s prior Rule 11 violation for the same conduct.  It also ordered the attorney to complete additional CLE courses related to GenAI.  The court reminded lawyers to “exercise pristine judgment when using [GenAI],” because if they do not, “it will be [their] name and license on the line, not ChatGPT’s.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: California - Federal District Court (Central District)|California - C.D. Cal. – (Judge) Kenly (Kiya) Kato

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Harris v. Elementis Specialties, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 94256 (C.D. Cal. Apr. 17, 2026), the attorney for the plaintiff filed a motion for sanctions against the defendant and its counsel after the defendant’s motion to dismiss, filed shortly before the case was remanded, contained several GenAI-created citation errors, including a “completely hallucinated cite and other cites that do not support the proposition Defendant[] claimed they did.” The defendant confirmed that “its use of [Gen]AI resulted in several citation errors.” While the court found the defendant’s “failure to verify authority it cited in its filing before a federal court is inexcusable,” it could not conclude that the defendant “knowingly or recklessly raise[d] a frivolous argument” sufficient to establish the bad faith required for sanctions under the court’s inherent authority, Section 1927 (prohibiting vexatious litigation), or the Local Rules. The court also noted that defense counsel “should have and could have alerted the Court to the multiple citation misrepresentations in its Motion but failed to do so,” rejecting counsel’s assertion that defendants were unable to file a correction with the court due to the case being closed. The court explicitly warned that it “will note this incident and consider it in future litigation should Defendant or its counsel make similar ‘mistakes’ as a result of the unverified use of AI.” The court denied the motion for sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Colorado - Federal District Court|Colorado - D. Colo. – (Judge) Nina (Y.) Wang

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hessert v. St. Dog Coal., Civil Action No. 25-cv-03807-NYW-NRN, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 85389 (D. Colo. Apr. 17, 2026), the pro se plaintiff asked the court to modify its April 13, 2026 Minute Order and challenged the constitutionality of the court’s “Standing Order Regarding the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (‘AI’) in Court Filings.” In the April 13 order, the court cautioned the pro se plaintiff to review the court’s “Standing Order Regarding the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (‘AI’) in Court Filings,” which requires all litigants to include an “AI Certification” with “‘every filing’ indicating whether [GenAI] was used in drafting the filing.” (ECF 109). Judge Wang advised the plaintiff that his pro se status “does not exempt him from complying with the Standing Order” and warned that future non-compliant filings “may be stricken.” (ECF 109). In its April 17 decision, the court rejected the plaintiff’s argument that the “Standing Order improperly compels speech in violation of the First Amendment, violates his due process and equal protection rights, ‘encroaches upon; work product, and amounts to legislating from the bench.’” The court denied the plaintiff’s request to “‘[v]acate the reference to the standing order’” in the Minute Order.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In United States v. Cline, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 84665 (N.D.N.Y. Apr. 17, 2026), the pro se defendants filed objections to a magistrate judge’s report and recommendation in a civil forfeiture proceeding. The court observed that the defendants’ arguments were “asserted with the misplaced confidence that has become a hallmark of an output of a [GenAI] tool,” and referred to “the [GenAI] tools that they used” in describing the defendants’ selective reading of the magistrate judge’s analysis. While the court did not impose sanctions or formally address the GenAI issue beyond these observations, it rejected all of the defendants’ arguments and adopted the magistrate judge’s report and recommendation in its entirety.

court: Oregon - Court of Appeals of Oregon

linkToCourtOrder.text: State Intermediate Appeals Court|Oregon - Court of Appeals of Oregon  – (Judge) Anna () Joyce

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Boersma v. Davenport, 349 Or. App. 268 (Mar. 17, 2026), the pro se plaintiff included “several citations to fabricated cases and cases that are unrelated to the propositions for which she cites them” in her opening brief. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the plaintiff claimed that she “conducted her research in good faith despite limited access to legal research tools [and] did not intend to submit fabricated authority.” As evidence for her good faith, she pointed the court to another appeal in which she was notified of inaccurate citations, and she had corrected those issues. The court was not impressed by this explanation because the plaintiff “apparently took no steps to ensure that her opening brief in this case did not contain the same issues to which she had been alerted in the separate appeal.” Because the plaintiff “was on notice of the potential for her research and writing process to produce fabricated authority,” and knew how to “alert[] this court to the fact that her brief might contain fabricated authority,” the court imposed a $500 sanction.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Strong v. Zubha POP Foods LLC, 2026 IL App (1st) 242451-U, 2026 Ill. App. Unpub. LEXIS 900 (Apr. 15, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs filed appellate briefs containing “copious citations to hallucinated authority” and references to “Centient AI,” some of which plaintiff “tried to obscure.” The defendant filed a motion for sanctions, to which the plaintiffs chose not to respond. The court initially struck the plaintiffs’ opening and reply briefs and gave the plaintiffs an opportunity to refile. Despite this second chance, the plaintiffs’ refiled briefs continued to rely on “unsupported arguments and illegitimate citations,” including “abundant citations to both hallucinated cases and fictitious holdings and quotations from existent cases.” The defendant filed a second motion for sanctions, which the plaintiffs again declined to address. Citing recent Illinois appellate authority holding that the “[f]lagrant and unprincipled use of [GenAI] without ensuring the accuracy of the generated response ‘is an abuse of the adversary system,’” the court found that the plaintiffs’ “persistent dependence on incoherent arguments, hallucinated authority, and fictitious case holdings has rendered review impossible.” The court again struck the plaintiffs’ briefs, and then dismissed the appeal for failure to comply with Illinois Supreme Court Rule 341, which regulates both brief formatting and reliance on authorities.

court: Illinois - Appellate Court of Illinois, Third District

linkToCourtOrder.text: Illinois - State Intermediate Appeals Court|Illinois - Appellate Court of Illinois, Third District – (Judge) Matthew (G.) Bertani

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hulvat v. Gumina, 2026 IL App (3d) 240628-U, 2026 Ill. App. Unpub. LEXIS 869 (Apr. 9, 2026), the pro se plaintiff included “citations to nine nonexistent cases” in his initial appellate brief. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the plaintiff admitted that the errors were the result of GenAI. Nevertheless, he argued that the court should not impose sanctions because he is self-represented, relied in good faith on “a well-known [GenAI] legal research tool” and corrected his error by filing an amended brief. The court disagreed, reasoning that citing to nonexistent cases constitutes a willful violation of Illinois Supreme Court Rule 375(a) and allows for sanctions. The court also cited Rule 375(b), which permits a reviewing court, in its discretion, to impose punitive sanctions for filing a frivolous appeal, including where a party cites fictitious cases. The court declined to strike the plaintiff’s brief and dismiss his appeal, but found that monetary sanctions were appropriate, ordering the plaintiff to pay the defendants’ appellate attorney fees (calculated as $3,178) incurred in discovering and addressing the errors. The court noted that further sanctions could have been appropriate, but that it found the award of fees sufficient for deterring similar conduct. The court affirmed the circuit court’s judgment on other grounds.

The court granted in part and denied in part the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Louisiana - Federal District Bankruptcy Court (Western District)|Louisiana - Bankr. W.D. La. – (Judge) John () Kolwe

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In In re Wise, No. 25-51132, 2026 Bankr. LEXIS 903 (Bankr. W.D. La. Apr. 9, 2026), the debtor’s counsel included AI-generated citations in her Objection to Motion for Relief from Stay and Supplemental Response. In response to the order to show cause, the attorney admitted to GenAI use. The court found that therefore there was “no question” that it should sanction the attorney under Fed. R. Bankr. P. 9011(c) for violating Rule 9011(b)(2), which requires attorneys to verify the authorities relied upon in filings. Judge Kolwe imposed both monetary and non-monetary sanctions for such a “flagrant abdication of [the attorney’s] basic professional responsibilities,” but imposed a “lesser” monetary sanction ($2,750) because the attorney admitted her mistake and it was the first time the court had sanctioned anyone for GenAI misuse. The court also required the attorney to attend CLE focused on GenAI and prohibited her from participating in any bankruptcy case in this court until she provided proof of compliance.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Guam - State Trial Court|Guam - Superior Court of Guam – (Judge) Elyze (M.) Iriarte

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary:
In Hocog v. Cook-Huynh, 2026 Guam Trial Order LEXIS 46 (Super. Ct. Guam Apr. 7, 2026), the defendant’s counsel included “at least thirteen . . . incorrect statutory citations, case cites, and quotes, plus misattributions and a fake case” in the defendant’s motion to dismiss. In response to the order to show cause, counsel maintained that the errors were “clerical,” that he accidentally filed a draft brief, and that his secretary failed to correct the draft. Although he also “acknowledged his ultimate responsibility, stressed the errors were inadvertent, and noted steps to prevent future issues,” the court noted that he offered “no further explanation” for the ficticious citations and instead only proposed corrective measures “focused . . . on preventing the filing of outdated drafts.” The court concluded that counsel used GenAI without “adequate human verification,” violated the Guam Rules of Professional Conduct, and violated Guam Rule of Civil Procedure 11. In imposing sanctions, the court considered the fact that this was counsel’s first violation and that there have been no similar cases in Guam yet. Judge Iriarte also considered that counsel “repeatedly blamed his secretary before conceding ultimate duty” and that the “repeated minimization of AI errors in what amounts to 42% of his citations in that document” undermines his assertion that he showed “full respect and candor” to the court. The court determined that counsel’s actions were a
“breakdown in the basic obligation to verify authorities before submission” and imposed a $1,500 sanction.
