court: Texas - Court of Appeals, Second District, Fort Worth

linkToCourtOrder.text: Texas - State Intermediate Appeals Court|Texas - Court of Appeals, Second District, Fort Worth – (Chief Justice) Bonnie () Sudderth|(Judge) Dana (Michelle) Womack|(Judge) Brian () Walker

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Stanford v. Leinart, 2026 Tex. App. LEXIS 3109 (Tex. App. Apr. 2, 2026), the pro se appellant filed an appellate brief that contained at least one fabricated case citation and numerous misquotations of real cases on multiple occasions. The court identified the fabricated citation as an apparent “hallucinated” authority generated from the use of GenAI, citing recent Texas appellate authority holding that “citation of nonexistent cases that appear to have been generated by [GenAI] is unacceptable.” On multiple occasions, the appellant attributed quotations to real cases that appeared nowhere in the cited opinions, and cited cases for propositions they did not address. The court further admonished the appellant for fabricating a statutory requirement and “attempting to mislead this court” through a fictitious quote attributed to a statute. While the court found the appellant’s briefing “inadequate” and his “gross misrepresentations of the record and misstatements of the law” to be unacceptable, it nevertheless reached the merits of the appeal and affirmed the trial court’s order dismissing the appellant’s claims on other grounds.

court: Arkansas – Court of Appeals, Division Four

linkToCourtOrder.text: Arkansas - State Intermediate Appeals Court|Arkansas – Court of Appeals, Division Four – (Judge) Stephanie (Potter) Barrett|(Chief Justice) Mark () Klappenbach|(Judge) Waymond (M.) Brown

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hrdlichka v. Bengston, 2026 Ark. App. 205 (Ark. Ct. App. Apr. 1, 2026), the pro se appellant filed an appellate brief that contained multiple fictitious case citations not found in “any recognized legal database.” The court found that these fictitious citations, combined with pervasive failures and other procedural violations, constituted “fatal briefing deficiencies” that prevented meaningful appellate review, and dismissed the appeal. Noting that this was the court’s “first opportunity to consider the impact of fictitious cases being submitted to this court,” the court addressed at length the “growing and troubling practice” of submitting appellate briefs “generated in whole or in part through artificial-intelligence tools that contain fabricated, inaccurate, or nonexistent legal citations.” The court emphasized that the use of GenAI “does not relieve a litigant of the duty to verify the accuracy of citations,” that “technology, including [GenAI], is not a substitute for legal judgment, verification, and professional diligence,” and warned that filings containing fabricated citations “may be struck, and appropriate sanctions, such as dismissal, may be imposed where warranted.” The court concluded by urging litigants to be cognizant of the court’s awareness of issues related to pervasive GenAI use and their zero-tolerance policy for “fraud…in violation of [the court’s] rules.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: Indiana - Federal District Court (Northern District)|Indiana - N.D. Ind. – (Judge) Holly (H.) Brady

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Maldonado v. Pro. Animal Ret. Ctr., No. 1:25-CV-454-HAB-ALT, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 75758 (N.D. Ind. Apr. 1, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel included inaccurate and nonexistent citations in the complaint and briefing, which “led the Court to suspect” GenAI misuse. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the plaintiff’s counsel apologized and explained that he “inadvertently filed an earlier, uncorrected working draft of the response brief” that his paralegal separately acknowledged was drafted with assistance of GenAI. He also filed a shorter corrected and updated brief without the fabricated cases. Judge Brady did not find this explanation credible, because if the plaintiff’s counsel had intended to file the second brief, he would not have “defend[ed] the extraordinary length of [his original] brief.” The court determined that the plaintiff’s counsel failed to make a “reasonable inquiry into the law” in violation of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, regardless of whether the errors were the due to GenAI. Judge Brady imposed a monetary sanction of $500 for each filing that contained the citation errors, amounting to $1,500 in total. Judge Brady also referred the Order to the Rhode Island attorney-disciplinary authority.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Alabama - Federal District Court (Southern District)|Alabama - S.D. Ala. – (Judge) Terry (Fitzgerald) Moorer

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Heimkes v. Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condo. Owners Ass'n, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 70410 (S.D. Ala. Mar. 31, 2026), the attorney for plaintiffs filed multiple pleadings in a condominium dispute that contained fabricated citations and false statements of law, were discovered by both the court and defendants. The court issued two show cause orders detailing the misstatements and directing counsel to provide copies of the allegedly fabricated authorities or a sworn declaration explaining how they were generated, but counsel’s responses were untimely, failed to fully address the court’s concerns, and “raised additional issues.” Although counsel denied using GenAI, he attached hundreds of pages of output from CoCounsel (Westlaw’s a legal AI research service), that the court found “riddled with erroneous information” and described as “gibberish.” Counsel appeared at the first show cause hearing “woefully unprepared” to address the court’s concerns and then repeatedly sought continuances, citing health issues, before ultimately refusing to attend the rescheduled hearing despite the option to appear remotely. Finding that counsel’s pattern of conduct—including fabricated citations, misrepresentations, failure to correct errors after notice, and refusal to participate in the sanctions process—amounted to bad faith, the court sanctioned counsel under Rule 11, Alabama Rule of Professional Conduct 3.3 (the Alabama professional rule analogue to Rule 11), and its inherent authority. The court imposed a public reprimand requiring counsel to file the order in all active and future cases for twelve months, ordered counsel to pay $55,597.00 in attorney fees to defense counsel, referred the matter to the Alabama State Bar with a recommendation that counsel “be found incompetent to practice law,” and directed the order submitted for publication in the Federal Supplement.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Arizona - Federal District Court|Arizona - D. Ariz. – (Judge) Dominic (W.) Lanza

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In FTC v. Noland, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69162 (D. Ariz. Mar. 31, 2026), pro se individual defendants filed two motions which “appear[ed] to contain hallucinated or otherwise inaccurate case citations.” The defendants acknowledged the existence of some errors but attributed them to having “proceeded pro se for over six years while subject to a comprehensive asset freeze, without access to Westlaw, Lexis, or comparable legal research resources.” The court found this excuse “unavailing,” citing recent authority holding that pro se litigants must follow the same rules of procedure as other litigants and that filing documents containing fictitious cases, whether generated by GenAI or “imagined” by the party, results in confusion and unnecessary work for opposing parties and the court. The court warned the defendants that they are responsible for the accuracy of any case citations in their filings and that the provision of future inaccurate citations may expose them to sanctions. The court denied the motions on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Arizona - Federal District Court|Arizona - D. Ariz. – (Senior Judge) Grant (Murray) Snow

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Montes v. Suns Legacy Partners LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 68888 (D. Ariz. Mar. 31, 2026), the defendant moved for Rule 11 sanctions against the attorneys for plaintiff after identifying at least eighteen instances of fictitious citations or fabricated quotations across four filings. The court found that the citations had “all the markings of a hallucinated case created by [GenAI] tools” and that counsel failed to conduct a “reasonable and competent inquiry” into the existence of the fabricated authorities. Counsel never took responsibility, instead attributing the errors to a law clerk, a LexisNexis cite-checking tool, “formatting” or “clerical” errors, and even to a “profound lack of diligence” by opposing counsel. The court rejected these explanations, noting that counsel’s Rule 11 obligations cannot be delegated and that running a brief through a cite-checking tool is no substitute for actually reading the cited cases. Finding that counsel’s conduct was not objectively reasonable, the court ordered both attorneys and their respective law firms, jointly and severally, to pay the defendant’s reasonable attorney fees incurred in discovering the fabricated authorities and briefing the sanctions motion. The court further ordered counsel to serve a copy of the order on their client, complete a CLE course on the ethical use of AI in law practice, and directed the Clerk to serve the order on the relevant state bars and on all judges in the district. The court granted in part and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss and denied the motion to strike on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Connecticut - Federal District Court|Connecticut - D. Conn. – (Judge) Sarala (V.) Nagala

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Ambrose v. Lee, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69545 (D. Conn. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se defendant filed a motion to dismiss in a defamation action that the court suspected was drafted, at least in part, using GenAI because of “serious misreading[s]” of cited authorities on two separate occasions. The defendant cited cases for propositions they did not support, and cases cited in support of a motion to strike that did not address relevant legal issues. The court announced a “no-tolerance policy for any briefing (AI-assisted or not) that hallucinates legal propositions or otherwise severely misstates the law,” warning that such filings “will often result in sanctions absent reasonable excuse.” The court emphasized that the failure to verify the accuracy of information generated by AI platforms implicates Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, which “applies fully to pro se litigants.” The court granted in part and denied in part the motion to dismiss on other grounds.

court: District of Columbia - Federal District Court

linkToCourtOrder.text: District of Columbia - Federal District Court|District of Columbia - D.D.C. –  (Judge) Rudolph () Contreras (Mar. 31, 2026) GenAI | Filings/Drafting | Caution

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Zavadovsky v. Republic of Austria, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69859 (D.D.C. Mar. 31, 2026), the defendant moved for sanctions following the plaintiffs’ inclusion of “nonexistent cases and fake quotations” in many of their filings. Though the plaintiffs “insist that their pleadings are not AI-generated,” the court flagged that one of the plaintiffs’ filings “read as if an unknown intelligent being were addressing Plaintiffs directly.” The court declined to impose sanctions but reminded the plaintiffs that submitting filings with “false, misleading, or nonexistent quotations or authority” violates Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and that any future filings with similar hallucinations could result in monetary and/or nonmonetary sanctions. Additionally, if the plaintiffs file new claims that appear “repetitive,” or “frivolous” or circumventive of “orders in other districts,” the court may file an order to show cause as to why “pre-filing restrictions” would be inappropriate.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Federal District Court (Northern District)|Alabama - N.D. Ala. –  (Judge) Anna (M.) Manasco

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Rivera v. Triad Props. Corp., No. 2:24-cv-01802-AMM, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 74689 (N.D. Ala. Mar. 31, 2026), the plaintiffs’ attorney (and one of the name partners of the law firm) included fabricated citations in the plaintiffs’ response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss. The attorney tried to correct the error by emailing an “accurate” version to the court ex parte. The court ordered the attorney to file a sur-reply for the court to determine whether to issue a show cause order. In the sur-reply, the plaintiffs’ attorney admitted to GenAI use, but only in a “supplemental capacity” and with “attorney review.” However, the attorney failed to acknowledge many of the specific citation issues. The court then issued a show cause order for why the attorney and his law firm should not be sanctioned. The attorney filed a response claiming that the mistakes were caused by a “computer error” and that he quickly fixed them. At the show cause hearing, the attorney then “blamed the court staff for not uploading his ‘correct’ document,” blamed opposing counsel for “not asking follow-up questions,” and provided inconsistent explanations for the errors. The court noted that the attorney had previously submitted filings with nonexistent citations in a different case. The law firm responded to the order to show cause with an assurance of commitment to candor and explained that it immediately enlisted two associates to investigate the situation when it came to light. The firm explained that the offending attorney “took several steps to cover the true extent of the situation” and never informed the firm or his clients about the extent of the filing issue. The firm eventually removed the attorney as a partner and stated its commitment to developing corrective action. The court found that sanctions were proper under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and the court’s inherent authority, and that the attorney’s conduct reflected “intentional, repeated choices—not isolated lapses in judgment attendant to unreasonable haste or excusable neglect.” The court found that the law firm was also subject to sanctions under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 because the firm did not have any “meaningful procedures or internal controls regarding its attorneys’ [GenAI use].” The court imposed sanctions including: 1) Suspending the attorney from the practice of law in Alabama for three months; 2) disqualification of both the attorney and the law firm from further participation in the case; 3) referral of the attorney to applicable licensing authorities; 4) payment by both the attorney and the firm of defendants’ attorney fees; 5) a public reprimand of both the attorney and law firm.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Illinois - Federal District Court (Central District)|Illinois - C.D. Ill. – (Judge) Colleen (R.) Lawless

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Feldman v. Dist. 6 Bd. of Educ., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69063 (C.D. Ill. Mar. 31, 2026), the attorney for plaintiffs filed a response to the defendants’ motion to dismiss in a student disability and harassment case that cited nonexistent cases and attributed unsupported legal propositions to real cases on multiple occasions. The court observed in a footnote that certain cited cases “d[id] not appear to exist, along with many others,” and that other citations, “[a]lthough the case exists, the quote does not, and the proposition the case is cited for was not a point of discussion.” The court warned that “any future citation to nonexistent cases, cases for legal propositions not discussed or supported, or other false representations to the Court will result in a show cause hearing and consideration of sanctions.” The court granted in part and denied in part the defendants’ motion to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Illinois - Federal District Court (Northern District)|Illinois - N.D. Ill. – (Judge) Andrea (R.) Wood

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Ella & Ramin LLC v. Travelers Ins. Co., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69445 (N.D. Ill. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiff repeatedly misrepresented citations and caselaw in his briefs but openly admitted to using GenAI to prepare his filings. He also asserted that he took care to avoid legal misrepresentations. The court noted that “[f]ew of the cases [the plaintiff] cited support the proposition for which he cite[d] them,” that some were completely unrelated to the asserted propositions, and that one case could not be identified in any legal database despite the parallel citations provided. The plaintiff moved for Rule 11 sanctions against the defendants’ counsel for raising the possibility of GenAI-related errors in their reply brief, but the court denied the motion as frivolous, holding that the defendants’ counsel “plainly had a good-faith basis to call the Court’s attention to potential mistakes or misrepresentations of the law” and that attorneys are “in fact obligated to raise such mistakes when necessary.” Citing recent Seventh Circuit authority, the court noted that “all litigants—represented and unrepresented—must read their filings and take reasonable care to avoid misrepresentations, factual and legal.” The court granted the defendants’ partial motion to dismiss in part and denied it in part on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Indiana - Federal District Court (Southern District)|Indiana - S.D. Ind. – (Judge) Matthew (Paul) Brookman

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Coleman v. Nexo Cap. Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 81931 (S.D. Ind. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs filed an opposition to the defendant’s motion for summary judgment which contained inaccurate citations, including a reference to a repealed statute and a nonexistent case. The court found it “unclear whether Plaintiffs’ citations are fabricated or erroneously cited,” and cautioned all parties to adhere to Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, “especially where [GenAI] may have been used to assist parties in preparing filings.” While the court did not impose sanctions, it warned that failure to comply with Rule 11 “authorizes sanctions, including the dismissal of the case.” The court granted the defendant’s motion for summary judgment on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Kansas - Federal District Court|Kansas - D. Kan. – (Judge) Daniel (D.) Crabtree

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Ford v. Sherwin-Williams, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69427 (D. Kan. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a motion for district judge review of a magistrate judge’s rulings that contained fictitious citations and quotations. The court observed that a citation in the plaintiff’s brief returned a case from a different court with different parties than represented, and suspected that the plaintiff used GenAI to produce the citation. Citing the court’s recent standing order (D. Kan. Standing Order 26-01) authorizing courts to “‘sua sponte strike any filing that appears to’ contain ‘fabricated or incorrect legal authority,’” Judge Crabtree cautioned the plaintiff that he will face sanctions—including “‘monetary sanctions’ or dismissal”—if he submits future filings containing “fabricated or incorrect legal authority.” The court granted in part and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Nevada - Federal District Court|Nevada - D. Nev. – (Judge) Richard (Franklin) Boulware II

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Allen v. W. Governors Univ., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69635 (D. Nev. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiff repeatedly submitted filings containing citations to misrepresented and nonexistent legal authorities despite multiple warnings regarding his obligations under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court issued an order to show cause, and the plaintiff’s response itself contained additional fabricated quotations attributed to real cases that contained no such language. The defendants catalogued “over sixty misrepresented or fabricated citations” across the plaintiff’s filings. The court found that the plaintiff either fabricated authorities and misrepresented their origins, or “enlisted the assistance of [GenAI],” which generated the fabricated cases, and then “failed to check their validity (even after being specifically instructed to do so).” The court further found that the plaintiff knowingly misrepresented material facts in an attempt to overcome the statute of limitations, constituting bad faith. Weighing the Ninth Circuit’s dispositive sanction factors, the court concluded that lesser sanctions were unavailable given the plaintiff’s repeated violations and failure to provide a candid explanation and dismissed the case with prejudice as a sanction. The court also ordered monetary sanctions in the form of the defendants’ attorneys’ fees and costs incurred as a direct result of the plaintiff’s citations to nonexistent or misrepresented authorities.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ohio - Federal District Court (Southern District)|Ohio - S.D. Ohio  – (Judge) Algenon (L.) Marbley (Mar. 31, 2026)

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Quandel Constr. Grp., Inc. v. Hunt Constr. Grp., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 70489 (S.D. Ohio Mar. 31, 2026), the attorneys for defendant filed an opposition memorandum that contained two incorrect case names, two incorrect case citations, and purported direct quotations from a case that did not contain the quoted language. The Plaintiff’s attorneys hypothesized that the errors were the product of a GenAI hallucination, but attorneys for the defendant denied using GenAI and sought leave to file a corrected brief, which the court granted. While the court noted that “[i]t does not appear that artificial intelligence was the culprit” based on counsel’s representations, it found that Hunt’s briefing had “failed to clarify how or why it provided the Court with two incorrect case names, two incorrect case citations, and improper direct quotations,” and emphasized that attorneys’ Rule 11 obligations to conduct a reasonable inquiry into the law apply “regardless of whether an attorney utilizes artificial intelligence in crafting legal contentions.” The court observed that false citations “damage judicial proceedings” whether or not GenAI was involved, and that no filing “‘should contain any citations—whether provided by [GenAI] or any other source—that’ a lawyer has not personally ‘read and verified.’” The court ordered all three of defendant’s attorneys to submit individual sworn submissions explaining their roles in preparing the brief and whether they used any AI tool in connection with the matter. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion on other grounds.

court: Washington - Superior Court of Washington (Pierce County)

linkToCourtOrder.text: Washington - State Trial Court|Washington - Superior Court of Washington (Pierce County) – (Judge) Diana (L.) Kiesel

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Masterson v. Nyffeler, No. 25-2-11068-6 (Wa. Super. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s fourth iteration of the same lawsuit contained several issues, including using GenAI to generate numerous pleadings that flooded the docket. The plaintiff did not deny allegations of GenAI use. The court observed that while the use of AI “is not per se improper,” when a pro se litigant uses AI to generate “an inordinate number of pleadings, it is extremely burdensome and costly to the represented party,” and reminded all parties—including pro se litigants—of their obligation to comply with CR 11. The court awarded attorney fees to the prevailing defendants and relieved them of any obligation to respond to further motions by the plaintiff unless ordered by the court.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 7th Circuit - Federal Court of Appeals|7th Circuit - 7th Cir. – (Chief Judge) Michael (B.) Brennan|(Judge) Frank (H.) Easterbrook|(Judge) Rebecca (Laine) Taibleson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Dec v. Mullin, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 9085 (7th Cir. Mar. 30, 2026), the attorney for the plaintiff-appellant included two nonexistent case citations and a false quotation in her opening brief on appeal, which the court found had “tell-tale signs of [GenAI] hallucinations.” When questioned at oral argument, counsel could not explain the citations, denied using AI, and stated she “presumably copied and pasted that portion from another brief” but could not locate the source. In a post-argument letter, counsel reiterated that she did not use AI, took responsibility, and apologized. Acknowledging the “promise and pitfalls” of AI in litigation, but expressing belief in the attorney’s “representation that she did not use AI,” the court reminded counsel that lawyers must not “knowingly misrepresent, mischaracterize, misquote, or miscite facts or authorities” and that violations “result in confusion and time wasted,” generating “unnecessary work on a case within a case.” The court publicly admonished counsel but declined to issue a show cause order for further sanctions, finding her acceptance of responsibility and apology persuasive and noting that the errors were few and “mainly supported the undisputed standard of review.” The court affirmed the dismissal on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Arizona - Federal District Court|Arizona - D. Ariz. - (Judge) Krissa (M.) Lanham (Mar. 30, 2026)

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Williams v. Crystal Springs Apts. LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 67113 (D. Ariz. Mar. 30, 2026), the court addressed the pro se plaintiffs’ use of GenAI tools to draft their amended complaint in ruling on the plaintiffs’ application for leave to proceed in forma pauperis. Though the plaintiffs’ original complaint included a disclosure that certain AI tools were used for drafting purposes and confirmed that factual allegations were based on the plaintiffs’ “personal knowledge and documentation,” the amended complaint contained no such disclosure (despite appearing to have been drafted using AI as well), “[did] not establish any plausible basis for relief[,] and assert[ed] claims that are not recognized under existing law.” The court reminded the parties that GenAI tools are not prohibited but must be used with caution. As such, Judge Lanham ordered the plaintiffs to include a “separate declaration” attached to all future filings where “Generative AI” is used, in which the plaintiffs must (i) confirm that the filing was independently “reviewed and verified;” and (ii) identify the portion(s) that “incorporate[] [GenAI] outputs. The court warned that any party who submits a filing with “inaccurate or undeclared generative AI outputs” could face sanctions. The court ultimately dismissed the plaintiffs’ amended complaint “with limited leave to amend.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: Massachusetts - Federal District Court|Massachusetts - D. Mass. – (Judge) Margaret (R.) Guzman

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Souza v. City of Fitchburg, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 67397 (D. Mass. Mar. 30, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed an opposition to the defendants’ motion to dismiss that the court found was prepared, at least in part, using GenAI. Judge Guzman observed that “[i]t is apparent to the Court that Plaintiff relied on [GenAI] in multiple instances to create his filings,” citing multiple instances in the plaintiff’s opposition where the GenAI program appeared to speak directly to the plaintiff, “using terms like ‘you’ and ‘your’ to refer to Plaintiff, and ‘he’ to refer to defense counsel.” The court warned that GenAI “is unreliable for legal assistance and often ‘hallucinates,’” citing recent authority addressing GenAI hallucinations. The court granted the defendants’ motion to dismiss in full on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)|New York - S.D.N.Y. – (Judge) Kenneth (M.) Karas

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In 76 Route 6 Holdings Inc. v. Town of Yorktown, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 71964 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 30, 2026), the plaintiff’s attorney filed an opposition brief that attributed nonexistent quotations to real cases on multiple occasions. The court found that the purportedly quoted language “appears nowhere in the cited decision” and warned counsel that they “must ensure the accuracy of all quoted materials” pursuant to their duties under Rule 11, citing authority holding that attorneys must “confirm the existence and validity of [] the legal authorities on which they rely.” The court did not impose sanctions but cautioned that counsel “runs the risk of sanctions.” The court granted the defendants’ motion to dismiss on other grounds.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Western District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: New York - Federal District Court (Western District)|New York - W.D.N.Y. – (Judge) Elizabeth (A.) Wolford

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Kassaw v. Wal-Mart Corp., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 67874 (W.D.N.Y. Mar. 30, 2026), the defendant moved for Rule 11 sanctions against the pro se plaintiff, arguing that several of the plaintiff’s filings contained hallucinated cases and misstatements of fact. The court declined to impose monetary sanctions, citing the plaintiff's pro se status and that the court had not previously admonished or warned him that sanctions could be imposed. However, the court directed the Clerk of Court to strike one of the plaintiff's memoranda from the docket because it “appears to include hallucinated cases.” The court further warned the plaintiff that “any future violation of Rule 11 may result in appropriate sanctions.” The court granted the defendant’s motion for summary judgment on all claims on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Oregon - Federal District Court|Oregon - D. Or. – (Magistrate Judge) Stacie (F.) Beckerman

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Fleck v. Del-One Fed. Credit Union, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 67368 (D. Or. Mar. 30, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss that contained misquoted local rules and a citation to a nonexistent case. The plaintiff had previously acknowledged using his company’s AI “systems” to “prepar[e] and format[]” his complaint and exhibits. The court confirmed that the purported citation “does not generate a case” and that the only source on Westlaw containing the purported quotation was the plaintiff’s own filing, noting that the plaintiff's “implicit invitation to make ‘findings’ that ‘mirror[]’ a hallucinated case” lent “further support” to the court’s assumption that the plaintiff used GenAI. Citing recent authority regarding GenAI hallucinations in litigation, the court reminded the plaintiff that Rule 11 requires all litigants to “read, and thereby confirm the existence and validity of, the legal authorities on which they rely,” and that relying on fabricated opinions “is an abuse of the adversary system.” The court warned that any further hallucinated legal citations will expose the plaintiff to sanctions. On other grounds, the court granted in part and denied in part the defendant's motion to dismiss.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 3d Cir. – Judge Peter Phipps, Cindy Kyounga Chung, and Jane R. Roth (dissenting in part)

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In McCarthy, P.A. v. United States DEA, No. 24-2704, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 9000 (3d Cir. Mar. 27, 2026), the appellant’s counsel “relied on case overviews generated by AI and provided to him by a non-attorney . . . without verifying the existence or accuracy of the citations” for his opening brief. The Government noted the “factual and legal inaccuracies” in its response brief. The appellant’s counsel then failed to check the citations in his Reply Brief, characterizing erroneous citations as “a good faith effort to chronicle Agency disparities, [and that there were] some immaterial misstatements about the cited cases' tangential details.” In response to the court’s order to provide copies of the cases, the appellant’s counsel “disclosed, for the first time, that the seven summaries were inaccurate and that one authority was hallucinated by AI,” and admitted that he first suspected GenAI use months earlier. The court ordered counsel to show cause. After a hearing, the court found that the attorney violated his duty to provide competent representation to a client under Pa. R.P.C. 1.1. In considering sanctions, the court found that the attorney’s apologies and corrective measures were mitigating factors, along with the fact that the court had “not yet addressed the failure of attorneys to use AI with appropriate guardrails” and that it did not specifically ask the attorney to address the rule. The court imposed a sanction of reprimand, involving notifying “‘all other courts before whom [Attorney] is admitted to practice and the National Disciplinary Data Bank of this discipline’ and provide them with a copy of the opinion imposing this discipline.” The court noted that it would have imposed monetary sanctions had this not been the first opportunity to address GenAI use. In a dissenting opinion, Judge Roth wrote that this case called for more severe sanctions because of the multiple instances of sanctionable conduct, the fact that the attorney received early notice of his mistakes, and that the court should not have to “explicitly forewarn against improper AI use” when it falls within existing legal rules.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Michigan - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|Michigan - E.D. Mich. – (Judge) Frances (Kay) Behm|(Magistrate Judge) Patricia (T.) Morris

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Shaltry v. Benzing, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 66498 (E.D. Mich. Mar. 27, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed multiple motions which contained false quotations and citations that the court identified as apparent GenAI hallucinations. On multiple occasions, the plaintiff attributed quotations to real cases that appeared nowhere in those opinions and cited at least one case the court was unable to find at all. The court noted that the closest a cited case came to supporting the plaintiff’s proposition “actually cuts the other way.” Citing recent Sixth Circuit authority that states, “‘no brief, pleading, motion, or any other paper filed in any court should contain any citations—whether provided by [GenAI] or any other source—that a lawyer has not personally read and verified,’” the court emphasized that the plaintiff’s pro se status does not alleviate him of his duties under Rule 11. The court cautioned the plaintiff that “further misrepresentations of fact or law will result in a recommendation for sanctions,” and denied struck the plaintiff’s motions on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: New Jersey - State Intermediate Appeals Court - Judge Heidi Willis Currier, Judge Berdote Bryne

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Amtrust N. Am. v. Liberty Mut. Ins. Co., No. A-2587-24, 2026 N.J. Super. Unpub. LEXIS 633 (Super. Ct. App. Div. Mar. 27, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel “cite[d] to four cases in his merits brief that do not exist,” whch the court suspected was the result of GenAI misuse. When the defendants pointed out in their opposition brief that they could not find one of the cases, the plaintiff’s counsel merely responded by pointing to “the existence of a case . . . with a similar citation,” but otherwise defending its position. The court was “stunned by the carelessness and dismissiveness of this statement,” noting that citing hallucinated cases, as the plaintiff’s authorities seemed to be, violates both state rules of professional conduct and court rules. The court found that this conduct warranted a “rare” sanction in part because the plaintiff’s counsel “flippantly disregarded the error and failed to make the appropriate retraction” and “glibly ignored the issue in his reply brief, in contravention of his duty of candor to the tribunal.” The court imposed a $1,000 sanction on the plaintiff’s counsel and dismissed the complaint without prejudice.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

Judge Stein suspected the plaintiff used GenAI without verifying its accuracy and warned the plaintiff that she could be subject to sanctions if she submits similar filings in the future.

results.summary: In Amtrust N. Am. v. Liberty Mut. Ins. Co., No. A-2587-24, 2026 N.J. Super. Unpub. LEXIS 633 (Super. Ct. App. Div. Mar. 27, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel “cite[d] to four cases in his merits brief that do not exist,” whch the court suspected was the result of GenAI misuse. When the defendants pointed out in their opposition brief that they could not find one of the cases, the plaintiff’s counsel merely responded by pointing to “the existence of a case . . . with a similar citation,” but otherwise defending its position. The court was “stunned by the carelessness and dismissiveness of this statement,” noting that citing hallucinated cases, as the plaintiff’s authorities seemed to be, violates both state rules of professional conduct and court rules. The court found that this conduct warranted a “rare” sanction in part because the plaintiff’s counsel “flippantly disregarded the error and failed to make the appropriate retraction” and “glibly ignored the issue in his reply brief, in contravention of his duty of candor to the tribunal.” The court imposed a $1,000 sanction on the plaintiff’s counsel and dismissed the complaint without prejudice.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D.N.Y. – Judge Sidney Stein

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Tantaros v. Fox News Network, LLC, No. 25-cv-1675 (SHS), 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 66317 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 27, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition to the defendant’s motion to dismiss and unauthorized sur-reply “contain[ed] both inaccurate and non-existent citations to cases and statutes.” After the defendant notified her of the errors, she purported to withdraw some of her citations but still included “inaccurate citations” in a later filing. Judge Stein suspected the plaintiff used GenAI without verifying its accuracy and warned the plaintiff that she could be subject to sanctions if she submits similar filings in the future.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Third Circuit - Judge Peter Phipps, Judge Cindy Kyounga Chung, Judge Jane R. Roth

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In McCarthy, P.A. v. United States DEA, No. 24-2704, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 9000 (3d Cir. Mar. 27, 2026), the appellant’s counsel “relied on case overviews generated by AI and provided to him by a non-attorney . . . without verifying the existence or accuracy of the citations” for his opening brief. The Government noted the “factual and legal inaccuracies” in its response brief. The appellant’s counsel then failed to check the citations in his Reply Brief, characterizing erroneous citations as “a good faith effort to chronicle Agency disparities, [and that there were] some immaterial misstatements about the cited cases' tangential details.” In response to the court’s order to provide copies of the cases, the appellant’s counsel “disclosed, for the first time, that the seven summaries were inaccurate and that one authority was hallucinated by AI,” and admitted that he first suspected GenAI use months earlier. The court ordered counsel to show cause. After a hearing, the court found that the attorney violated his duty to provide competent representation to a client under Pa. R.P.C. 1.1. In considering sanctions, the court found that the attorney’s apologies and corrective measures were mitigating factors, along with the fact that the court had “not yet addressed the failure of attorneys to use AI with appropriate guardrails” and that it did not specifically ask the attorney to address the rule. The court imposed a sanction of reprimand, involving notifying “‘all other courts before whom [Attorney] is admitted to practice and the National Disciplinary Data Bank of this discipline’ and provide them with a copy of the opinion imposing this discipline.” The court noted that it would have imposed monetary sanctions had this not been the first opportunity to address GenAI use. In a dissenting opinion, Judge Roth wrote that this case called for more severe sanctions because of the multiple instances of sanctionable conduct, the fact that the attorney received early notice of his mistakes, and that the court should not have to “explicitly forewarn against improper AI use” when it falls within existing legal rules.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Alabama - Federal District Court (Southern District)|Alabama - S.D. Ala. – (Judge) Harold (D.) Mooty III

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In State Nat'l Ins. Co. v. Treadwell, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 72098 (N.D. Ala. Mar. 26, 2026), the attorney for defendants filed a response to a motion filed by plaintiff that contained two fabricated quotations attributed to two different cases, neither of which existed in the cited authorities or anywhere else. The court issued an order to show cause, and counsel admitted to using ChatGPT and OpenCase to draft the filing, confirming that the quotations were hallucinations of one or both GenAI tools and that he did not thoroughly verify the citations before filing. The court found counsel’s conduct “akin to contempt” under Rule 11, noting his “cavalier approach to these proceedings,” which included a late-filed and unedited response to the show cause order and tardiness to the show cause hearing. Citing its own prior decision emphasizing that citations to fake legal authority “pose a serious threat to the fair administration of justice,” the court imposed a public reprimand, ordering counsel to provide a copy of the order to opposing counsel and the presiding judge in every pending case in which he is counsel of record and directing the Clerk to submit the order for publication in the Federal Supplement. The court declined to refer the matter to the state bar, impose monetary sanctions, or suspend counsel, but warned that each of these options “is on the table” for any future infraction.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Nev. – Judge Andrew Gordon

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Wallace v. Pennymac Loan Servs., LLC, No. 2:25-cv-02297-APG-EJY, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 64330 (D. Nev. Mar. 26, 2026), the defendants asserted in their reply to their motion to dismiss that the pro se plaintiff relied on false authority in his response brief, and “moved for leave to file supplemental authority in the form of a decision by another judge in this district who sanctioned an attorney for using fake case citations.” Judge Gordon granted the motion because the defendants accused the plaintiff of similar conduct as the conduct sanctioned in the other decision. The court found that the plaintiff cited nonexistent cases and fabricated quotations, and suspected GenAI use. Judge Gordon reminded the plaintiff of his duty under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and that further citations of AI-generated cases may result in sanctions. The court granted the plaintiff’s motion to amend and denied the plaintiff’s additional motions on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.N.J. – Judge Edward Kiel

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Baker v. Rastelli Foods LLC, No. 24-cv-08882-ESK-AMD, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 64242 (D.N.J. Mar. 26, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs included in their amended complaint cases that “are so inaccurately cited that the Court is unable to locate them, assuming they exist at all” and a “quotation that does not appear in the cited case.” In a separate case, the court had already warned the plaintiffs that failure to verify citations for similar errors may result in sanctions. Here, although the court declined to impose sanctions, it required that any further attempts to file an amended complaint or similar actions must include “PDF copies of each case cited” and that plaintiffs must “highlight in each PDF the text that supports their asserted proposition.” Should they fail to do so, the court will require them to show cause why they should not be sanctioned. The case was remanded for an order dismissing the complaint without prejudice on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Oregon – Federal District Court|Oregon - D. Or. – (Magistrate Judge) Mark (D.) Clarke

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Couvrette v. Wisnovsky, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 60329 (D. Or. Mar. 23, 2026), the court imposed sanctions on the plaintiffs and the plaintiffs’ attorney for filing three briefs containing fabricated cases and quotations, which the court held constituted violations of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and local rules. The plaintiffs continued to cite to nonexistent cases after the defendants called the errors to their attention and suggested GenAI misuse. In response to the defendants’ motion for sanctions, the plaintiffs simply deleted the non-existent citations and quotation marks while leaving the fabricated legal contentions unchanged. Plaintiffs argued that “[t]he inclusion of inaccurate citations was inadvertent and the result of reliance on an automated legal citation tool” and that they had taken proper corrective action. Defendants then re-filed their motion for sanctions. At the sanctions hearing, the court found that the plaintiffs failed to take corrective action and instead “attempted a coverup,” violating Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and the Oregon Rules of Professional Conduct. The court also found the plaintiff himself was likely the person using GenAI to draft and “share[d] responsibility [with his counsel] for the use of non-existent cases and fabricated quotations,” thus warranting sanctions against the plaintiff as well. As sanctions, the court struck without leave to refile plaintiffs’ summary judgment briefing; vacated the order allowing former local counsel to withdraw and ordered him show cause why he should not be subject to sanctions; assessed plaintiffs’ counsel a $15,000 fine to be paid to the clerk of court; requested defendants submit a bill of costs and reasonable attorney fees associated with their response to the fraudulent citations; and dismissed the plaintiffs’ claims with prejudice. In a later order, the court accepted defendant’s attorneys’ fees as reasonable, and ordered plaintiffs’ counsel and local counsel to pay defendants attorney fees totaling $94,704.38. Local counsel was ordered to pay $14,205.66, and plaintiffs’ counsel was ordered to pay the remaining $80,498.72.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Utah - Federal District Court|Utah - D. Utah – (District Judge) Ted () Stewart|(Magistrate Judge) Cecilia (M.) Romero

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Moulder v. Davis Sch. Dist., No. 1:25-cv-00052-TS-CMR (D. Utah Mar. 23, 2026), the attorneys for the plaintiffs cited two nonexistent cases and one nonexistent quotation in a briefing filed with the court. At the order to show cause hearing, counsel “admitted to using [GenAI] in preparing their arguments” and conceded that “each failed to independently verify the existence and accuracy of the two non-existent cases.” Counsel “accepted full responsibility for their lack of oversight” and described measures they had since implemented, including “purchasing software to help verify citations and employing additional staff.” The court reaffirmed that “a fake opinion generated by AI is not ‘existing law’” and found that counsel violated Rule 11(b). In light of the attorneys’ “sincerity and acceptance of responsibility” and a prior reimbursement of $1,525.50 in legal expenses to defense counsel, the court ordered counsel to complete two CLE courses “relating to AI ethics and professional responsibility within the legal profession” and submit proof of completion within six months.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. La. – Judge Carl J. Barbier

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Gentry v. Thompson, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 58718 (E.D. La. Mar. 20, 2026), defense counsel for the City of New Orleans filed a memorandum in support of a motion to dismiss that contained nine hallucinated case citations that the court observed were “likely” generated by GenAI. After the court issued an order to show cause, the primary drafting attorney appeared and admitted to using ChatGPT to find supporting caselaw and conceded that he neither checked the citations nor read the cited cases before filing. His supervising attorney acknowledged that he reviewed and revised other sections of the memorandum but failed to verify the hallucinated citations. The court found three attorneys whose names were on the filing violated Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11 by failing to conduct a reasonable inquiry into the legal authority cited in the brief. The court sanctioned the drafting attorney $250 and his supervising attorney $1,000, reasoning that the supervisor bore greater responsibility given his roughly thirty years of experience and his duty to ensure the accuracy of filings by attorneys under his supervision. The court declined to sanction the Chief Deputy City Attorney but issued a formal admonishment, and the newly appointed City Attorney outlined remedial steps the office was undertaking, including a department-wide meeting and development of a written GenAI policy.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ky. Ct. App. – Judge Susanne M. Cetrulo (Judge Sara W. Combs and Judge Allison E. Jones concurring)

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Blair v. Sanctuary Bluff Homeowners Ass'n, 2026 Ky. App. Unpub. LEXIS 180 (Ky. Ct. App. Mar. 20, 2026), the opposing party moved to strike the pro se plaintiff’s initial appellant brief and dismiss the appeal due to nine hallucinated citations and repeatedly misquoting precedent. The plaintiff admitted to using GenAI and catching the mistakes, but claimed to have filed the wrong draft. The court denied the motion to dismiss but granted the motion to strike the brief and allowed the plaintiff to refile with a certification “that her brief was prepared without AI.” Ten days later, the plaintiff submitted a new brief certifying that while she used GenAI for editing purposes, she did not use it for drafting or research. The opposing party renewed its motion to dismiss, as the refiled brief again contained hallucinated precedent—an allegation to which the plaintiff admitted. The court again denied the motion to dismiss “in an abundance of grace.” However, when the plaintiff then submitted a reply brief with yet another hallucinated case and without the required GenAI certification, the court ruled that this “deliberate disregard for a Court’s ruling . . . likely sabotaged her own litigation.” The court nevertheless addressed the case on its merits and dismissed the appellant’s appeal, evidently in part because of her pattern of hallucinated citations.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Neb. – Per Curiam (Justice Stephanie F. Stacy concurring)

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Prososki v. Regan, 2026 Neb. LEXIS 38, 321 Neb. 38 (Neb. Mar. 20, 2026), counsel for the appellant in a dissolution of marriage appeal filed a brief containing numerous fictitious case citations, real cases with fabricated quotations and mischaracterized holdings, and fictitious quotations. The appellee moved to strike the brief and dismiss the appeal, asserting that the errors were the product of GenAI. Counsel denied using GenAI, attributing the errors to “sloppy” copying and pasting from Westlaw and having filed the wrong draft due to “a screen crack on his laptop,” but the court found this explanation “lack[ed] credibility” given the volume of fictitious quotations that could not be located anywhere in Nebraska jurisprudence. Noting that this presented a novel issue for Nebraska courts, the court held that the submission of fictitious authority, whether through GenAI or not, “can be resolved by application of [Nebraska’s] existing rules of professional conduct and court rules,” including the duties of candor toward the tribunal, competence, diligence, and good faith argumentation. The court struck the appellant's brief, dismissed his appeal, and referred his counsel to the Counsel for Discipline of the Nebraska Supreme Court.

court: Ohio - Eleventh Appellate District Court of Appeals

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ohio Ct. App., 11th Dist. – Judge Eugene A. Lucci ((Judge) Robert J. Patton concurring| (Judge) John J. Eklund concurring in part and dissenting in part)

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In State v. Coleman, 2026-Ohio-965, 2026 Ohio App. LEXIS 988 (Ohio Ct. App. Mar. 20, 2026), defense counsel in a criminal appeal alleged prosecutorial misconduct by filing an application to reopen, in part based on allegedly “inflammatory, improper statements” by the prosecutor. That application contained fabricated transcript quotations, which defense counsel admitted was generated by ChatGPT, allegedly as a result of his paralegal using the tool without adequate supervision or verification. However, the purported quotes by the prosecutor were never made, which the court held impugned not only the prosecutor but also the trial judge (implying that the court permitted such improper statements). After the State identified the fabrications in its responsive filing and moved for sanctions, counsel neither withdrew nor amended the application, and subsequently appealed the denial to the Ohio Supreme Court without disclosing the fabricated content. Two months after the sanctions hearing, counsel filed a motion in an unrelated case that contained an embedded ChatGPT prompt in the text of the filing itself. And although counsel eventually proposed “remedial measures,” including the creation of an internal AI policy for his firm, that policy itself “bore the hallmarks” of having itself been generated by GenAI. Finding bad faith based on counsel’s familiarity with GenAI tools yet failing to verify citations, the significant nature of the hallucinations, and his failure to take corrective action, the court imposed comprehensive sanctions: a $2,000 monetary fine, referral to the Office of Disciplinary Counsel, a two-year requirement to serve the sanctions order on the presiding judge of every court in which counsel enters a new case, a two-year certification and verification requirement for all filings, six hours of mandatory CLE on AI ethics, striking of the fabricated application, removal as counsel, and written apologies to the individuals defamed by the fabricated filing.

After the State identified the fabrications in its responsive filing and moved for sanctions, counsel neither withdrew nor amended the application, and subsequently appealed the denial to the Ohio Supreme Court without disclosing the fabricated content. Two months after the sanctions hearing, counsel filed a motion in an unrelated case that contained an embedded ChatGPT prompt in the text of the filing itself. And although counsel eventually proposed “remedial measures,” including the creation of an internal AI policy for his firm, that policy itself “bore the hallmarks” of having itself been generated by GenAI. Finding bad faith based on counsel’s familiarity with GenAI tools yet failing to verify citations, the significant nature of the hallucinations, and his failure to take corrective action, the court imposed comprehensive sanctions: a $2,000 monetary fine, referral to the Office of Disciplinary Counsel, a two-year requirement to serve the sanctions order on the presiding judge of every court in which counsel enters a new case, a two-year certification and verification requirement for all filings, six hours of mandatory CLE on AI ethics, striking of the fabricated application, removal as counsel, and written apologies to the individuals defamed by the fabricated filing.

The court reversed the order on other grounds.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Brownfield v. Cherokee Cnty. Sch. Dist. No. 35, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 57452 (E.D. Okla. Mar. 19, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a motion for sanctions that contained eight citations identified by the defendant as either fictitious or inaccurate. The court issued a sua sponte order to show cause, and the plaintiff acknowledged using GenAI to assist in organizing legal research for the motion without verifying the citations for accuracy. The court found that the plaintiff’s submission of fictitious and inaccurate citations violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b), emphasizing that pro se litigants “must ‘follow the same rules of procedure that govern other litigants.’” In an attempt to mitigate sanctions, the plaintiff voluntarily completed a two-month AI program covering hallucinations and ethical considerations and pledged not to use GenAI in any future matter before the court prior to the show cause hearing. The court ordered the plaintiff to pay the defendant $500 for attorney’s fees incurred researching the fabricated cases, finding this the minimum sanction sufficient to deter and punish considering mitigating factors, and warned that future Rule 11 violations would result in harsher penalties.

court: Washington - Division Three Court of Appeals

linkToCourtOrder.text: Wash. Ct. App. – Chief Judge Robert E. Lawrence-Berry, Judge John Cooney, Judge Tracy Staab, and Judge Megan Murphy

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In In re Est. of Carey v. Whitman, 2026 Wash. App. LEXIS 562 (Wash. Ct. App. Mar. 19, 2026), the pro se appellant’s briefs contained citations to “incorrect, chimerical, or inapplicable case law” throughout, including citations to cases that did not exist and misquotations or misrepresentations of the holdings of cases that did exist. The court declined to review arguments premised on fictitious authority, holding that citations to fictitious legal authority “equates to a lack of legal authority” under Rule of Appellate Procedure 10.3(a)(6), which requires parties to provide citations to relevant legal authority in support of their arguments. The court also found the appeal “so devoid of merit that there was no possibility for reversal,” thus rendering it frivolous. While the court declined to impose independent sanctions for the appellant's use of fictitious case law and failure to comply with the Rules of Appellate Procedure, it did impose sanctions for the appellant’s frivolous appeal and granted attorneys fees to appellees

Although the court recognized that pro se litigants “may seek technological assistance when preparing filings,” it emphasized that it is the litigant’s responsibility to ensure that all filings “are accurate, truthful, and grounded in existing law and authority” under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b). The court prohibited the plaintiff from using any GenAI tool to draft or assist in drafting any future filings in the case and ordered that all future briefs or motions include a certification under penalty of perjury that the filing “was prepared solely by [the plaintiff] and was not drafted or assisted by any [GenAI] tool.” The court warned that failure to comply may result in sanctions, including dismissal of claims or striking of filings.

The court affirmed the trial court on other grounds.

court: North Carolina - State Intermediate Appeals Court

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Forest Ridge Townhomes Corp. of Greensboro v. Heag Pain Mgmt. Ctr., P.A., 2026 N.C. App. LEXIS 253 (N.C. Ct. App. Mar. 18, 2026), counsel for the respondents cited irrelevant and inapplicable legal authority throughout an appellate brief, which the court in turn dismissed as meritless. Respondents’ counsel admitted that he had “supplemented” his traditional research “with research through Perplexity.AI,” for which the court chastised him for his “regrettable oversight” in failing to adequately review GenAI-generated work product. In so ruling, the court cited the North Carolina State Bar's formal opinion holding an attorney “individually responsible for reviewing, evaluating, and ultimately relying” on such work product. However, although petitioner moved to sanction respondents and their counsel for “substantially failing to comply” with the North Carolina Rules of Appellate Procedure and for bringing the appeal for an improper purpose, the court ultimately declined to impose sanctions, finding that the legal errors did not rise to the level of “gross . . . noncompliance.” The court affirmed the trial court's order on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Or. Ct. App. – Judge Scott Shorr

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Doiban v. Or. Liquor & Cannabis Comm'n, 347 Or. Ct. App. 742 (Mar. 18, 2026), the petitioner’s counsel included in the opening brief “case citations to nonexistent cases and quotations that did not exist anywhere in Oregon case law.” In response to the order to show cause, the petitioner’s counsel apologized and explained that he failed to verify the citations and quotations from legal research delegated to his staff. He further explained that his office does not permit GenAI use for drafting legal documents or conducting legal research and that they did not use GenAI for actual drafting, although they did use it for administrative tasks. Counsel explained that he believes the errors were from what they thought were legitimate sources found through internet searches . Counsel and the law firm took steps to remedy the errors, including reviewing all citations in all court filings, restriction of citation to only Westlaw and Lexis databases or “other published and verified legal precedent,” and implementation of a “system of increased review” to permit additional time for verification and accuracy. Despite these proposed corrective actions, the court found that a $10,000 sanction on the petitioner’s counsel was warranted, particularly because the respondent’s counsel pointed out the errors, but the petitioner’s counsel did not acknowledge or address them until the court acted, thereby “minimiz[ing] the gravity of the situation.” The court allowed the petitioner to file a replacement brief, limited to the same arguments, relying only on the nonfabricated authorities.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ore. Ct. App. – Presiding Judge Scott A. Shorr, Judge Steven R. Powers, and Judge Ryan T. O’Connor

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Doiban v. Or. Liquor & Cannabis Comm’n, 2026 Ore. App. LEXIS 361 (Or. Ct. App. Mar. 18, 2026), petitioner’s counsel filed an opening brief in an administrative appeal that contained at least fifteen fabricated case citations and at least nine nonexistent quotations. Counsel explained that law firm staff used an “unverified search engine” for legal research after finding limited results on traditional platforms, but admitted that the resulting citations were not verified in Westlaw or Lexis before filing. Although counsel maintained that his office policy prohibits the use of GenAI for drafting or legal research, the court was skeptical that a “bad search engine” alone could account for the errors. Notably, opposing counsel flagged the fabricated citations months before oral argument, but petitioner's counsel did not respond or seek to correct them for seven months, acknowledging the errors only when questioned by the court at oral argument. The court found that sanctions were warranted under ORCP 17 C(3), which provides that an attorney who signs a brief certifies that its legal positions are warranted by existing law. The court imposed a $10,000 monetary sanction on counsel, permitted the filing of a replacement brief which cites only to the “five remaining [non-hallucinated] cases listed in the table of authorities” (provided that those cases are accurately described), and required counsel to certify that GenAI was not used to produce the redacted brief and that all citations had been personally verified.

App. 268 (Mar. 17, 2026), the pro se plaintiff included “several citations to fabricated cases and cases that are unrelated to the propositions for which she cites them” in her opening brief. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the plaintiff claimed that she “conducted her research in good faith despite limited access to legal research tools [and] did not intend to submit fabricated authority.” As evidence for her good faith, she pointed the court to another appeal in which she was notified of inaccurate citations, and she had corrected those issues. The court was not impressed by this explanation because the plaintiff “apparently took no steps to ensure that her opening brief in this case did not contain the same issues to which she had been alerted in the separate appeal.” Because the plaintiff “was on notice of the potential for her research and writing process to produce fabricated authority,” and knew how to “alert[] this court to the fact that her brief might contain fabricated authority,” the court imposed a $500 sanction.

The court warned plaintiffs’ counsel that it would issue an order to show cause why sanctions should not issue if they again submit fictitious legal authority. On unrelated grounds, the court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss.

The court further noted that the plaintiff’s citation to nonexistent authority “bolsters the Court’s conclusion” that the plaintiff could not adequately represent the interests of other litigants in the putative class action. App. Mar. 16, 2026), the pro se appellant “quot[ed] fictitious statutory language, cite[d] inapplicable statutes, and relie[d] on cases that do not exist” throughout his appellate briefing. linkToCourtOrder.text: 6th Cir – Judge John K. Bush

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Whiting v. City of Athens, Nos. 24-5918/5919, 25-5424, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 7479 (6th Cir. Mar. 13, 2026), the court observed that plaintiff-appellant’s attorneys’ briefs contained “over two dozen fake citations and misrepresentations of fact.” The court ordered the attorneys to show cause why they should not be sanctioned. Instead of responding to the show cause order, the attorneys argued that “the show cause order was ‘void on its face for failing to include a signature of an Article III judge,’ was ‘motivated by harassment of the Respondent attorneys,’ and ‘reflect[ed] illegal ex-parte [sic] communications within this Court.’” The court decided to sanction the attorneys under both its inherent authority and Fed. R. App. P. 38, which permits courts to award damages and single or double costs to the appellee if an appeal is frivolous. The court reasoned that misconduct such as misrepresenting caselaw or the record can render an appeal frivolous as argued and determined that the plaintiff-appellant acted in bad faith for relying on fake cases, acting with improper purpose by attempting to reach a result that is not possible under existing law, and arguing the show cause order instead of providing an explanation for the false citations. In determining the severity of the sanctions, the court considered four aggravating factors: (i) the attorneys engaged in further misconduct on appeal of sanctions orders; (ii) the attorneys have already been disciplined for lack of candor to the tribunal; (iii) the attorneys “defied [the] court’s order to show cause and refused to provide the information [the court] requested”; and (iv) the responses the attorneys filed “show[ed] a stunning lack of respect for this court, the members of the panel and their staffs, and the rule of law.” The court imposed the following sanctions: 1) the attorneys must reimburse the defendants-appellees for attorneys’ fees on appeal; 2) the attorneys must pay double costs to appellees; 3) appellees will file an accounting of costs and the attorneys can file responses or objections; and 4) the attorneys must individually pay the court $15,000.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 4th Cir - Judges A. Marvin Quattlebaum Jr., Allison Jones Rushing, DeAndrea G. Benjamin

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In In re Nwaubani, No. 25-9517, 2026 WL 687194 (4th Cir. Mar. 11, 2026), the attorney representing the plaintiff on appeal cited a case in his opening brief that “did not exist.” In response to the court’s order to show cause, the attorney maintained that he intended to cite a different case and that the error was not the result of GenAI. His revised opening brief, however, simply omitted the argument that contained the nonexistent citation. Furthermore, the attorney’s reply brief contained two citations to nonexistent cases. The Standing Panel on Attorney Discipline issued a supplemental notice to show cause, where the attorney again claimed that the citations were errors and that he meant to cite different cases. Those cases, however, “appear[ed] to have minimal relevance to the facts or law” of the brief. After formal briefing and oral argument, the Standing Panel decided that the proper standard of proof for ethical violations is clear and convincing evidence, and that the District of Columbia Bar Rule of Professional Conduct 8.4(d) that prohibits “conduct that seriously interferes with the administration of justice” is the proper ethical rule to consider. The court found that regardless of whether the attorney used GenAI, “submitting a brief with nonexistent cases” where the “legal relevance of [the] existing cases to his arguments [] is tenuous at best” constitutes such conduct. The panel ultimately determined there was “clear and convincing evidence” that the attorney’s conduct violated the professional rules and the court’s Local Rule 46(g)(1)(c). As discipline, the court issued a public admonishment.

Moses, alleging that “the directive to file the R&R in ‘every court’ is unduly prejudicial and beyond the proper scope of relief.” However, Judge Broderick acknowledged that at least three “hallucinated” cases to which the petitioner cited were nonexistent and may have been included due to GenAI tools. The court disagreed that requiring copies of the R&R and order submitted in every other court would be “unduly prejudicial” and instead warned the petitioner that the failure to disclose GenAI use and the inclusion of hallucinated cases in future filings could result in sanctions. Judge Broderick adopted the remainder of the R&R and denied the petition and motions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Texas - Federal District Court (Northern District)|Texas - N.D. Tex. – (Magistrate Judge) Rebecca (Tustin) Rutherford

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hawkins v. DeSoto Indep. Sch. Dist., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 70417 (N.D. Tex. Mar. 11, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response to the defendants’ motions to dismiss in an employment discrimination and retaliation action that contained improperly cited cases, at least two of which did not exist. The court observed that these errors “are typical of unverified, AI-generated filings, which have become an increasing concern for courts in recent years.” Magistrate Judge Rutherford reminded the plaintiff that while no federal or local rule explicitly prohibits the use of GenAI in litigation, all parties, including those proceeding pro se, “must fulfill their obligations under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b),” which “necessitates a careful review of AI-generated statements and citations to ensure accuracy.” The court further noted that Local Rule 7.2(f), adopted after the relevant pleadings were filed, requires parties to disclose the use of GenAI. The court recommended granting the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Okla. – Judge Scott Lawrence Palk

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Dillon v. Stephenson, No. CIV-25-484-SLP, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 50055 (W.D. Okla. Mar. 11, 2026), the court flagged that the plaintiffs included “incorrect citations” in their response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss. When the court asked plaintiffs’ counsel whether GenAI was used in connection with the response brief, they denied using such tools to “generate any content” but suggested using GenAI as a “research assistant.” However, the court found additional incorrect citations that “may have been the result of reliance on a generative-AI program.” Judge Palk admonished plaintiffs’ counsel and warned that future GenAI misconduct “will result in the imposition of sanctions and any other action the Court deems necessary.” The court granted the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

Mar. 10, 2026), the court noted that “some of [the plaintiff’s] filings [in connection with the defendant’s motion to dismiss] suggest he may be using [GenAI].” Judge Lanham reminded the parties that the court’s policy on GenAI use requires a disclosure and certification that the party has independently verified the filing’s accuracy and that any failure to follow the policy may result in sanctions. The court ultimately reminded the plaintiff that he “must comply with the court’s procedures regarding [GenAI]” when preparing his amended complaint and dismissed the plaintiff’s amended complaint (with leave to amend) on other grounds.

Bank & Tr., No. CV-25-03106-PHX-KML, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 74217 (D. Ariz. Mar. 10, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff “used [GenAI] in drafting most, if not all, of his filings” because the filings “present[ed] lengthy but irrelevant arguments,” “cite[d] obscure cases for incorrect legal propositions,” and included nonexistent quotations. Judge Lanham had previously warned the plaintiff that submitting further filings that cite non-existent cases may result in sanctions. The court “attempt[ed] to decipher” the plaintiff’s arguments but ultimately disregarded many of them because they were not relevant to the litigation. The court granted the defendants’ motions to dismiss, some with and some without prejudice, on the merits.

The trial court’s decision was affirmed on other grounds.

Magistrate Judge Barr instead struck the plaintiff’s response brief from the record, granted the plaintiff leave to refile, and admonished the plaintiff that in the future he must comply with the federal and local rules or may face sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D.N.Y. – Judge Jennifer Louise Rochon

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

Mar. 10, 2026), the attorney-defendant included “pervasive citations to non-existent authority” in his briefing in support of the motion to dismiss. The defendant claimed that the errors were the result of using Protégé in LexisNexis, but LexisNexis confirmed that the defendant did not subscribe to Protégé. Judge Rochon noted that the defendant had received warnings and sanctions for similar litigation misconduct in other jurisdictions, and that those warnings, sanctions, and CLE courses “did not have an appreciable impact on [the defendant].” The court admonished the defendant with the “lengthy historic recitation” of his misconduct and warned that further misconduct could lead to sanctions or “a referral for discipline.” Judge Rochon granted and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.

The court noted that this was the attorney’s first infraction in this court (though the attorney had submitted a filing with “seemingly similar defects in federal district court”), and the errors did not “impede[] [the appellee’s] ability to respond.” Judge Markle therefore warned the attorney that submitting future filings with hallucinated citations may result in sanctions. The court affirmed the judgment on other grounds.
