Judge Gordon reminded the parties that “citing fake cases drafted by [GenAI] may violate [Fed. R. Civ. P. 11]” and “may result in sanctions” in the future. The court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss and for a more definite statement in part on other grounds.

court: District of Columbia - State Supreme Court
District of Columbia - D.C. Ct. App.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.C. Court of Appeals – Chief Judge Anna Blackbume-Rigsby and Judge Milton C. Lee

results.summary: In In re A.I. Task Force of the D.C. Courts, 2026 D.C. App. LEXIS 82 (Mar. 6, 2026), the District of Columbia Court of Appeals established a standing AI Task Force comprising 18 appointed members (including judges, court administrators, and IT staff) tasked with examining and developing best practices for “the use of AI in the courts,” expanding the courts’ use of AI, ensuring that AI “is used appropriately and the integrity of the court process is preserved and maintained,” and to “[d]evelop court rules with respect to disclosure, transparency, ethics, accuracy, authenticity[,] and certification of AI use in court pleadings and proceedings.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. La. – Judge Jerry Edwards, Jr.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Kattom v. Bondi, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 46975 (W.D. La. Mar. 6, 2026), the court sanctioned an attorney $1,000 for filing a motion for a temporary restraining order and accompanying memorandum containing hallucinated citations and incorrect quotations. The attorney used ChatGPT to draft the memorandum in support of the motion, which was filed on an expedited basis due to concerns that removal of the petitioner was imminent. Despite understanding the risks associated with GenAI use, including the potential for generating fabricated legal authority, the attorney admitted to “not fully vetting or verifying the governing law or the citations within the submitted memorandum.” The attorney took responsibility, acknowledged the seriousness of his actions, and informed the court that he had undertaken remedial measures, including enrolling in CLEs on GenAI-assisted legal practice and developing strengthened verification protocols. Citing the Fifth Circuit’s observation that courts are increasingly “burdened [with] addressing AI hallucinations in court filings,” Judge Edwards emphasized that a lawyer's duty remains “unchanged” regardless of whether GenAI is used, as the lawyer must “ensure that the legal propositions and authority generated are trustworthy.” While commending the attorney for his “candor, honesty, and the remedial measures he has undertaken,” the court found that these mitigating factors did not excuse the attorney’s conduct, as Rule 11 requires attorneys to verify the accuracy of submissions to the court at a minimum. The court imposed a $1,000 monetary sanction to be paid personally by the attorney.

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Okla. – Magistrate Judge Chris Stephens

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Clark v. Corecivic, Inc., No. CIV-25-775-SLP, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 50645 (W.D. Okla. Mar. 6, 2026), the pro se plaintiff mischaracterized case law in his response to the motion to dismiss, in what the court found was “likely from the use of AI software without independent verification of case citations.” The court issued an order admonishing the plaintiff and warned that further misconduct could result in sanctions. Though the plaintiff apologized and promised “that he had reviewed all authorities ‘to ensure their accuracy,’” he made similar errors in his surreply. Magistrate Judge Stephens again warned the plaintiff of his responsibility to submit accurate citations to the court and recommended that the court grant summary judgment to the defendant on other grounds.

judge: Acting Presiding Judge Joan K. Irion
applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In In re Domestic P'ship of Torres Campos & Munoz, 2026 Cal. App. LEXIS 130 (Cal. Ct. App. Mar. 5, 2026), the court considered an appeal from a family court order and addressing respondent’s counsel’s citation to fictitious legal authorities in a letter sent to opposing counsel, in the respondent’s declaration, and in briefs filed with the appellate court. Respondent’s counsel admitted to citing non-existent cases, stating that one “may have” been generated by GenAI and that the other was sourced from a “Reddit article that was authored by a non-attorney blogger,” and that she “did not verify” those authorities. The family court then issued an order which relied upon those non-existent cases first cited by respondent (and then later cited by petitioner); the appellate court ruled it constituted an abuse of discretion for the family court to rely “in material part on fictional case authorities in rendering a decision or making an order.” However, the fact that the petitioner had also cited the fictitious cases and failed to identify them earlier (before belatedly raising them as grounds for reversal) constituted forfeiture of that claim of error. In addressing the GenAI misuse, the appellate court noted that—despite respondent counsel’s admission to citing the cases in question—she “doubled down” in her defense of the fake citations even when confronted with them by opposing counsel. The court found this conduct to fall “far short of [the attorney’s] duty of candor with the court” under California’s Rules of Professional Conduct and the Rules of the Court. As a result, the appellate court imposed $5,000 in sanctions on respondent’s counsel (as well as ordering her to report the sanction to the state bar), particularly because she both “persisted in and aggravated the misconduct” by defending the fictitious citations, and because she “still has not been entirely forthcoming” about how one of the citations was originally sourced.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. La. – Judge Brandon S. Long

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Woodward Harbor L.L.C. v. City of Mandeville, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 45132 (E.D. La. Mar. 5, 2026), following an order to show cause, the court sanctioned one of the plaintiffs’ attorneys for using GenAI to draft an opposition brief that contained “citations to at least eleven cases that either did not exist or were incorrectly summarized, relied on, or quoted from.” Even after the defendant brought the nonexistent case law to the court’s and plaintiff’s attention through its reply brief, the plaintiffs took no corrective action. The court had previously dismissed the plaintiffs’ complaint with prejudice for failure to state a claim, noting the numerous hallucinated citations and concluding they were likely the product of GenAI, and ordered the attorney to show cause as to why he should not be sanctioned for the hallucinate citations. Following the order to show cause hearing (in which the filing attorney admitted to using GenAI tools and being “unaware” of the risk of hallucinations), the court remarked that it was “troubled” that an experienced attorney could be so unfamiliar with “an issue so well-publicized in the legal community.” Although the court recognized the attorney’s candor and agreement to repay the relevant client’s fees, the court nonetheless imposed a $1,000 personal sanction on the offending attorney and ordered him to attend two hours of CLE on GenAI. The court also declined to sanction three other attorneys whose names appeared on the brief (based on their declarations that the filing attorney bore sole responsibility for verifying the brief’s accuracy), but reminded them of their “personal, nondelegable responsibility” under FRCP 11.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Kentucky - Federal District Court (Eastern District)|Kentucky - E.D. Ky. – (Magistrate Judge) Edward (B.) Atkins

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Carroll v. Univ. of Ky., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 73704 (E.D. Ky. Mar. 4, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response in opposition to the defendants’ motion for partial dismissal that contained a citation to a nonexistent case. After the defendants identified the error in their reply, the plaintiff filed a notice of errata correcting the citation errors and substituting different case law in support of his position. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion for leave to file a sur-reply, noting that he had already addressed the defendants’ allegation in his notice of errata. While the court appreciated the plaintiff’s correction, Magistrate Judge Atkins warned that continued citation of nonexistent case law “may result in sanctions such as the pleading being stricken, filing restrictions being imposed, or recommendation that his case be dismissed.” The court recommended granting the defendants’ motion to dismiss in part and denying it in part on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Utah - Federal District Court|Utah - D. Utah - (Judge) Dustin (B.) Pead

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Vernieri v. Dyno Nobile Ams., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 73221 (D. Utah Mar. 4, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition to the defendants’ motion to dismiss contained citations to non-existent cases that “appear[]” to be GenAI hallucinations. The court warned the plaintiff that, within the 10th Circuit, courts have dismissed plaintiffs’ complaints in full without any ruling on the merits when parties “misuse . . . generative AI tools.” Though Judge Pead declined to do so here, he cautioned that future GenAI misuse could lead to “dismissal of the action and any other appropriate sanctions.” The court recommended that the defendants’ motion to dismiss be granted on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Wash. – Judge Benjamin H. Settle

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Dixon v. Multicare Health Sys., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 44646 (W.D. Wash. Mar. 4, 2026), the pro se plaintiff repeatedly cited nonexistent cases and cases that did not support the propositions for which they were offered in filings submitted to the court. The defendant requested that the court sanction the plaintiff for his improper filings, Judge Settle noted that while a pro se litigant is not held to the same standard as a licensed attorney, pro se litigants “must follow the same rules of procedure that govern other litigants” and “should not be treated more favorably than parties with attorneys of record.” The court observed that it has “no formal rule against the use of [GenAI] to write pleadings and briefs,” but emphasized that parties are bound by Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, which requires parties to certify that their “legal contentions are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law.” Finding that the plaintiff’s “repeated and presumably intentional failure to verify cited authority supports an award of sanctions,” the court admonished the plaintiff for his inappropriate conduct and warned that monetary sanctions may be awarded if, in the future, the plaintiff repeats this behavior. On the merits, the court granted in part and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss.

court: Washington - Wa. Ct. App., Div. Three

linkToCourtOrder.text: Wash. Ct. App. – Judge John Cooney (Judge Tracy Staab and Judge Megan Murphy concurring)

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In in re Est. of Carey, 2026 Wash. App. LEXIS 418 (Wash. Ct. App. Mar. 3, 2026), the pro se appellant’s briefs contained citations to “incorrect, chimerical, or inapplicable case law” throughout, including citations to cases that did not exist and misquotations or misrepresentations of the holdings of cases that did exist. The court declined to review arguments premised on fictitious authority, holding that citations to fictitious legal authority “equates to a lack of legal authority” under Rule of Appellate Procedure 10.3(a)(6), which requires parties to provide citations to relevant legal authority in support of their arguments. The court also found the appeal “so devoid of merit that there was no possibility for reversal,” thus rendering it frivolous. While the court declined to impose independent sanctions for the appellant's use of fictitious case law and failure to comply with the Rules of Appellate Procedure, it did impose sanctions for the appellant’s frivolous appeal.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Kan. – Chief Judge John W. Broomes

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hunt v. True Sky Fed. Credit Union, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 38632 (D. Kan. Feb. 25, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited to a non-existent case in his brief. The court made the plaintiff aware of the court’s standing order on the use of AI in court filings, entered after the plaintiff filed his brief. The standing order requires a “a litigant to review and verify the accuracy of all content filed with the court that was drafted or assisted using an AI tool.” Judge Broomes warned that future filings which violate the standing order could result in sanctions. The court granted in part and took under advisement in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Ohio – Judge Walter H. Rice

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Kettering Adventist Healthcare v. Collier, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 38680 (S.D. Ohio Feb. 25, 2026), the defendants cited “at least twelve cases that either do not exist or do not remotely stand for the purported propositions of law.” See also Kettering Adventist Healthcare v. Collier, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 124 (S.D. Ohio Jan. 2, 2026) (detailing violations). The attorneys continued citing fabricated citations even after opposing counsel brought the nonexistent cases to their attention. In response to the order to show cause, the attorneys made “timely and contrite filings to the Court, including intended affirmative steps to prevent future errors.” But despite these promises, the attorneys proceeded to submit “patent misstatements and mischaracterizations of the law” to Magistrate Judge Silvain in their mediation statements in the underlying case, “[t]he breadth and depth of [the attorneys’] continued malfeasance leav[ing] [the Court] almost at a loss for words.” Judge Rice concluded that the attorney’s actions “constitute the most egregious, inexplicable, and repeated violations of Rule 11(b) by any attorneys that have ever appeared before the undersigned,” and that the attorneys also violated Ohio R. Prof’l Conduct 3.3(a)(1). The court imposed sanctions against the attorneys and found them in contempt of court for their misrepresentations to Magistrate Judge Silvain. As sanction, the court ordered the defendant’s motion to dismiss stricken without prejudice, and imposed a monetary sanction of $5,000 against one attorney and $2,500 against the other (who appeared later in the case). Judge Rice also referred the attorneys’ conduct to the Supreme Court of Ohio’s Office of Disciplinary Counsel and requested that the Chief Judge determine whether additional sanction is necessary for the contempt of court.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Haw. – Judge Gonzalo P. Curiel

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mau v. Honolulu, No. 24cv0253-GPC-WRP (D. Haw. Feb. 24, 2026), the defendant’s counsel cited “fake cases” and cases that “do not support the propositions stated,” prompting the court to issue an order to show cause. In response to the order to show cause, the attorneys admitted the mistakes and accepted responsibility, but asserted that they did not use GenAI. The court found that a hearing was not necessary since there was no dispute that the attorneys violated Rule 11 by submitting fictitious citations to the court. Because the attorneys accepted responsibility and did not defend the inaccuracies, Judge Curiel imposed a “modest” sanction of $1,000 each against the two attorneys.

results.summary: In Lee v. Cap. One Bank, No. 2:25-cv-540, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 52839 (D. Utah Feb. 24, 2026), the defendant raised concerns about the pro se plaintiff’s reliance on GenAI for his filings and requested that the court “disregard any AI-generated portions of Plaintiff's opposition to the extent they are inaccurate or hallucinated” or that the plaintiff “relies primarily on such content without providing specific factual support for its claims.” The court declined, instead admonishing the plaintiff “to take greater care to avoid misrepresentations in his filings or face possible sanctions.” Magistrate Judge Pead recommended that the court grant the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. v. Dean, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 43000 (E.D. Tex. Feb. 24, 2026), two of the pro se defendant’s motions submitted to the court contained citations to nonexistent cases and cases that did not stand for the cited propositions. The court suspected that the errors stemmed from the use of GenAI, noting that the problem has become “a regrettably common problem, wasting party and court resources alike.” Magistrate Judge Davis emphasized that by signing and filing documents with the court, parties certify under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b)(2) that their legal contentions are warranted by existing law, and that false citations “contradict that certification and are tantamount to dishonesty.” The court further noted that the defendant's pro se status “in no way relieves her of responsibility for the accuracy and quality of the documents she submits to the court” under Local Rule CV-11(g). The court warned that citing nonexistent cases “undermines the integrity of the judiciary and the court's trust in litigants” and is “unacceptable,” and that the defendant will be subject to sanctions if she files another document containing hallucinated or otherwise nonexistent citations. On unrelated grounds, the court dismissed one of the Defendant’s motions and recommended the denial of another.

The plaintiff then continued to file documents containing nonexistent or misleading citations and Judge Gujarati issued another warning. The plaintiff did not attempt to correct the inaccurate filings, even after the defendants indicated they would be filing a motion for sanctions. The plaintiff eventually and belatedly admitted to the inaccuracy of some of the citations and the use of the Gemini GenAI tool (after “at least five prior declarations to the contrary during the conference and numerous representations over the prior seven months to other judges and counsel”). The plaintiff then sought leave to amend the complaint and an opposition to the defendant’s motion for sanctions, but that motion itself also contained fabricated authorities. Judge Eichenholtz recommended granting the defendant’s motion for sanctions including dismissal with prejudice and fees related to the motion, or granting the defendant’s motion to dismiss and dismissing the complaint with prejudice on other grounds.

results.summary: In Reynolds v. Progressive Techs., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69377 (W.D. Tenn. Feb. 20, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss that contained multiple manufactured quotations attributed to real cases and citations to completely fictitious cases. While the defendant separately requested that the plaintiff be ordered to show cause concerning his alleged use of GenAI-hallucinated case citations, Magistrate Judge Christoff decided not to impose or recommend sanctions at that time, and instead “admonished [the plaintiff] in the strongest possible terms that fictional case law citations are entirely inappropriate.” She warned that “any future infractions may result in the imposition of sanctions, up to and including the dismissal of his case,” and ordered the plaintiff to review any legal citations for accuracy before including them in future filings. The court recommended granting in part and denying in part the motion to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Bankr. D. Mass. – Judge Elizabeth D. Katz

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In in re: Marrett v. Aroostook Cnty. Fed. Sav. & Loan (Bankr. D. Mass. Feb. 19, 2026), the pro se debtors cited a nonexistent case in their complaint as the only authority supporting one of their causes of action. The court therefore concluded that “the Debtors have not provided . . . any authority to support a cause of action.” The court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim.

linkToCourtOrder.text: C.D. Cal. – Judge Fred W. Slaughter

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Veramancini v. Cnty. of Orange, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 35478 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 19, 2026), the court sanctioned plaintiffs' counsel for submitting briefing containing inaccurate case quotations and characterizations indicative of GenAI use. The court first noted potential GenAI misuse in response to inaccuracies in counsel's motion to dismiss briefing stage, after which counsel submitted a declaration acknowledging the errors and pledging corrective measures. However, in a subsequent briefing, opposing counsel informed the court that plaintiff’s counsel had misrepresented the holding of a state supreme court case, and the court again raised the issue at a hearing. Despite these warnings, counsel's summary judgment briefing repeated the same types of errors, attributing quotes to cases that did not contain those quotes and misrepresenting the holding of an appellate case. Throughout the proceedings, counsel repeatedly told the court that he did not use GenAI to draft his briefs, but ultimately acknowledged using Google Gemini for “grammar, spelling, and conciseness.” The court found that sanctions were appropriate under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11, emphasizing that “citing nonexistent case law or misrepresenting the holdings of a case is making a false statement to a court, regardless of whether [GenAI] suggested the content.” While counsel represented that his errors were “unintentional,” the court noted that it had already allowed him “substantial latitude” by not issuing sanctions in response to the first or second round of misrepresentations. The court sanctioned counsel by ordering him to (1) pay $2,000 personally to the Clerk of the Court and (2) serve a copy of the order on the State Bar of California.

linkToCourtOrder.text: C.D. Cal. – Judge Mark C. Scarsi

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Varsobia Home Care Servs., LLC v. Com. Borrowing Fund, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 34499 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 19, 2026), the court suspected that the plaintiff prepared its complaint with the assistance of GenAI, along with several other threshold deficiencies. The court noted that complaint contained unfilled GenAI placeholder text (including bracketed instructions such as “[describe specific irregularities, e.g., failure to provide required documentation, improper notice, or other procedural deficiencies].”). Judge Scarsi ordered that any party in this matter who uses GenAI in connection with filings must attach a separate declaration disclosing the use and certifying that the filer has reviewed and verified the content as “accurate and in compliance with Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11.” The declaration must identify which portions of the filing incorporate GenAI outputs. The court also warned that presenting inaccurate or undeclared GenAI outputs may result in sanctions. On unrelated grounds, the court ordered the plaintiff to show cause as to why the complaint should not be dismissed without prejudice.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Fla. – Magistrate Judge Panayotta Augustin-Birch

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Pena v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 33823 (S.D. Fla. Feb. 19, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs’ renewed motion for preliminary injunction cited a real case for a proposition it does not support and referenced a non-existent page of that opinion, leading the court to suspect that the plaintiffs obtained their analysis from “an unreliable source, such as [GenAI].” The court noted that it had “previously admonished Plaintiffs for potentially using [GenAI] hallucinated legal citations,” and warned that if the plaintiffs include any other false or inaccurate legal citations that the court suspects were GenAI-generated, it will order them to appear in person to explain those citations and why they should not be sanctioned. The court recommended denying the motion on unrelated grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 5th Cir. – Chief Judge Jennifer Elrod, Judge Jerry E. Smith, Judge Cory T. Wilson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Fletcher v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., No. 25-20086, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 4834 (5th Cir. Feb. 18, 2026), the court found that the plaintiff’s counsel used GenAI to draft her reply brief and failed to verify its accuracy. The brief contained at least 16 fabricated quotations and 5 serious misrepresentations of law or fact. In response to the order to show cause, the plaintiff’s counsel denied GenAI use, and asserted that she “relied on publicly available versions of the cases, which [she] believed were accurate.” The court did not find this explanation credible and asked for additional explanation. The plaintiff’s counsel then admitted to using GenAI, but only to “help organize and structure [her] arguments and to break up overly long paragraphs.” She attributed the inaccurate quotations to “‘paraphrased summaries,’ which she claims to have ‘mistakenly believed . . . reflected the actual language of the cases,’” but the court could not find such summaries. The court also concluded that because counsel “did not explain the steps she took to verify” assertions in her brief, the court “concludes that she took none.” The court therefore found that she use GenAI to draft her brief and failed to check it for accuracy, and thus imposed a $2,500 sanction under Fed. R. App. P. 46(c) and the court’s “inherent power to impose sanctions for abuse of the judicial process.” The court noted that “[h]ad [counsel] accepted responsibility and been more forthcoming, it is likely that the court would have imposed lesser sanctions.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: Cal. Ct. App., 2d Dist., Div. 3 – Judge Mark Hanasono, with Judge Lee S. Edmon and Judge Rashida A. Adams concurring

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In EFD USA, Inc. v. Band Pro Film & Digital Inc., 2026 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 1114 (Cal. Ct. App. Feb. 18, 2026), the appellate court identified several “fabricated quotations and inaccurate descriptions of legal authority” in the appellant’s briefing. The appellant’s lead attorney had used a GenAI “consultant” to prepare a draft opening brief and later used an “unidentified ‘AI system’” to shorten the brief to meet the word limit, but failed to confirm whether the system changed any citations during that process. The opening brief contained several fabricated quotations from real cases, including a leading case cited for a proposition it did not address and a purported quotation that did not appear in the cited authority. The respondents’ brief identified the deficiencies, but the appellant failed to acknowledge or explain the inaccuracies in its reply. In fact, the appellant’s reply brief also contained at least two fabricated quotations. The court then issued an order to show cause why the appellant’s attorneys should not be sanctioned for pursuing a frivolous appeal. In response, the lead attorney accepted responsibility for the errors and attributed them to the GenAI system he used to shorten the briefs. The court emphasized that it is “a fundamental duty of attorneys to read the legal authorities they cite in appellate briefs” and that counsel “plainly” did not read the cases before filing. Finding that the fabricated citations violated the California Rules of Court, which require that arguments be supported “with citations to real (as opposed to fabricated) legal authority,” the court imposed a $900 sanction against the lead attorney alone, payable to the clerk of the court. The court noted that while the deficiencies were “similar in kind” to those in a recent case where it imposed a $10,000 sanction, they were “far less widespread.” The court affirmed lower court’s judgment on all grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Or. – Judge Amy M. Baggio

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Martin v. ODS Cmty. Dental Ins., No. 3:26-cv-00298-AB, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 32712 (D. Or. Feb. 18, 2026), the court noticed that the pro se plaintiff’s motion for a temporary restraining order contained inaccurate or incomplete citations. Judge Baggio warned that “to the extent that Plaintiff is using [GenAI] to aid him in his briefing … the [c]ourt will not tolerate further inaccurate or incomplete citations to legal authority.” The court denied the plaintiff’s motion for a TRO on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Or. – Magistrate Judge Jolie Russo

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Jones v. Target Corp., No. 3:23-cv-01301-JR, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 47888 (D. Or. Feb. 18, 2026), on review of the defendant’s motion for terminating sanctions against the pro se plaintiff, the court determined that the plaintiff “filed baseless motions, withheld basic discovery, and relied on ‘hallucinated’ case citations.” In assessing whether terminating sanctions were appropriate, the court considered, among other things, the plaintiff’s behavior that caused unnecessary delays in the case, the plaintiff’s failure to participate in discovery, and the availability of lesser sanctions. The court determined that lesser sanctions “would seemingly have no effect” and recommended terminating sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Tex. – Judge J. Campbell Barker

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Walder v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 33215 (E.D. Tex. Feb. 18, 2026), Judge J. Campbell Barker reviewed de novo and adopted Magistrate Judge Mitchell’s report and recommendation (2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 34005) denying the pro se plaintiff's motion for summary judgment. The plaintiff objected on three grounds, including that her citation of fictitious cases was “unintentional, not made in bad faith, and not intended to mislead the Court.” The court noted its appreciation of the plaintiff’s clarification, while reiterating the magistrate judge’s warning “that further citation to nonexistent cases and noncompliance with Local Rule CV-11(g) may result in sanctions, including her pleadings and other papers being struck, filing restrictions, dismissal of her claims, and monetary penalties.” The court overruled the plaintiff's objections, denied the motion without prejudice, and permitted the plaintiff to refile by the court's dispositive motion deadline.

court: Oklahoma -  Court of Criminal Appeals of Oklahoma
judge: Vice Presiding Judge William J. Musseman

linkToCourtOrder.text: Okla. Ct. Crim. App. – Presiding Judge Gary L. Lumpkin, Vice Presiding Judge William J. Musseman, Judge Robert L. Hudson, and Judge Scott Rowland

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: The Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals adopted Rule 1.17 governing the use of GenAI in documents filed before the court, entitled “Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence.” See In re Addition of A New Rule to the Rules of the Ct. of Crim. Appeals, 2026 OK CR 7 (Okla. Crim. App. Feb. 18, 2026). Rule 1.17 defines GenAI as “any type of artificial intelligence that generates content or data in response to a prompt or query by a user.” The rule requires that when GenAI has been “used in the drafting of any document for filing in this Court,” the party or their counsel must “ensure that any portion of the document produced or modified by [GenAI], whether in whole or in part, has been verified as accurate by a person responsible for the document.” Courts may issue sanctions (including, but not limited to, waiver of issues, striking documents, and contempt) for failure to comply. In adopting the rule, the court recognized GenAI's potential to “produce misinformation or even to hallucinate.” Presiding Judge Lumpkin specially concurred, emphasizing that a lawyer’s signature on any document constitutes a certification that “all citations of authority, and cases in the document are true and correct, and all quoted language is accurate and correctly referenced,” and that any document that proves to be incorrect in its use of legal authority “subjects the lawyer to sanctions and disciplinary actions.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Ohio – Judge Edmund A. Sargus, Jr.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mardis v. Dealer Loyalty Prot., No. 2:25-cv-1237, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 32806 (S.D. Ohio Feb. 18, 2026), the defendants alerted the court that the pro se plaintiff likely cited cases hallucinated by GenAI in his motions. Judge Sargus denied the defendants’ motion for sanctions but warned that citing to AI-generated caselaw violates Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court directed the plaintiff to cease the behavior and warned that further violations could result in sanctions. The court denied the plaintiff’s motions on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Tex. – Magistrate Judge Christina A. Bryan

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Robertson v. TrueAccord Corp., No. 4:25-CV-2816 (S.D. Tex. Feb. 18, 2026), the pro se plaintiff's response to the defendant's motion to dismiss contained several inaccurate or false case citations. The court noted that the citations “mush together case names, action numbers, and database identifiers from real decisions,” raising the concern that the plaintiff was using GenAI to draft her filings. While recognizing that the plaintiff may not be aware of the risks that GenAI tools pose, and acknowledging the difficulty pro se litigants may face without access to legal databases to verify such citations, the court emphasized that pro se status “does not exempt a party from compliance with relevant rules of procedural and substantive law.” The court reminded the plaintiff that filings containing false citations or quotations violate Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 regardless of whether they are GenAI-generated, and that she must also comply with the court's General Order 2025-04 regarding the use of GenAI in court filings. The court warned that failure to comply may result in sanctions, including monetary sanctions and dismissal of the plaintiff's claims with prejudice.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In in re Escobedo, Nos. 25-12572 MER, 25-1223 MER, 2026 Bankr. LEXIS 390 (Bankr. D. Colo. Feb. 17, 2026), the court noticed that the pro se defendant cited a fake case and included “what appears to be prompts from some artificial intelligence guidance” in his motion to dismiss. Judge Romero warned that GenAI use without verifying the accuracy of citations may result in sanctions. The court granted in part and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.

Ct. 8.204, which requires that all litigants (including pro se parties) verify accuracy of citations in briefs. Judge Adams declined to impose sanctions but cautioned that continued fabricated quotations and citations may result in sanctions. The court affirmed the judgment on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Ga. – Judge Steven D. Grimberg

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In his Standing Order Regarding Civil Litigation (N.D. Ga. Feb. 17, 2026), Judge Grimberg addresses GenAI use by stating that while parties may any AI tools, “only human beings will be held responsible for the outcome.” If a filing contains factual or legal errors, the party and lawyers who filed it will be held accountable, “includ[ing] each and every lawyer listed on the signature block of the filing.” The standing order does not impose any disclosure or certification requirements related to GenAI use.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 10th Cir. – Judge Paul J. Kelly Jr.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Picon-Diaz v. Bondi, No. 25-9530, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 4417 (10th Cir. Feb. 13, 2026), the petitioners’ counsel relied on or quoted from a nonexistent citation “multiple times” in their brief. The court could not find “any relevant federal case similarly captioned or containing any of the quotations that counsel attributes to this apparently nonexistent case” and suspected GenAI use without proper review. Judge Kelly “disregard[ed] [the p]etitioners’ reliance on this case.” The court declined to impose sanctions but warned the petitioners’ counsel and all attorneys “of the responsibility to ensure that citations to legal authority are not fabrications.” The court denied the petition for review.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 18th Jud. Cir. Fla. – Chief Judge Melanie Chase

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: Chief Judge Chase's Administrative Order No. 26-10, titled “Disclosure of Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence by Attorneys and Self-Represented Litigants,” requires attorneys and self-represented litigants appearing before the Circuit and County Courts of the Eighteenth Judicial Circuit to disclose the use of any GenAI tool in the preparation of pleadings, motions, memoranda, responses, proposed orders, or other court records. The order notes that such tools may generate inaccurate assertions or citations, and that the court “should not have to parse case citations and parentheticals to discern whether cases exist.” Filings prepared with GenAI assistance must therefore include a certification stating that all factual assertions, legal authority, and citations have been independently reviewed and verified for accuracy, and that the filer accepts full responsibility for the filing's contents. The order emphasizes that GenAI use does not relieve any party of the duty of candor to the tribunal, the obligation to independently verify all factual assertions and legal authority, or compliance with applicable statutes, procedural rules, and the Rules Regulating the Florida Bar. All GenAI-generated content is deemed the work product of the filing party. The order also prohibits the submission of fictitious, fabricated, or hallucinated content; reliance on GenAI-generated citations without independent verification; misrepresentation of GenAI-generated content as independently researched authority; and failure to disclose GenAI use as required by the order, noting that failure to comply may result in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 7­th Cir. – Judges Frank H. Easterbrook, David Frank Hamilton, and Doris Lenea Pryor

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mahwikizi v. Uber Techs., Inc. & Rasier, LLC, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 4411 (7th Cir. Feb. 13, 2026), the court noted that it could not find two of the cases the pro se plaintiff cited in his reply brief. The court did not impose sanctions but reminded litigants that whether or not the errors were the result of GenAI use, they must “carefully review their submissions before filing in this court.” The court affirmed the district court’s dismissal for failure to prosecute on other grounds.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D.N.Y. – Judge Jennifer H. Rearden

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Allah-El v. Columbia Univ., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 30842 (S.D.N.Y. Feb. 13, 2026), the court denied with prejudice the plaintiff’s counsel’s motion for admission pro hac vice. In a prior related action before Judge Denise L. Cote, counsel had filed default judgment requests that were “incomprehensible” and which contained “numerous errors” and “an entire conversation between plaintiff's counsel and ChatGPT.” After noting that counsel’s submissions were clearly drafted by GenAI, Judge Cote ordered counsel to appear and explain why she should not be sanctioned for this conduct, among other deficiencies, and ultimately dismissed that action without prejudice. When the plaintiff refiled the case that was assigned to Judge Rearden, counsel violated Judge Cote’s order to immediately bring the transcript of the prior proceedings to the new presiding judge’s attention. Counsel also made several other procedural errors related to her pro hac vice status and other filings. The court found that counsel's repeated errors and noncompliance with court orders demonstrated that she was unfit to be admitted pro hac vice and denied her application with prejudice. In conjunction, the court directed the plaintiff to retain qualified counsel or file a letter indicating that he intends to proceed pro se.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ind. App. – Judge Mary DeBoer

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Rechlicz v. Price Point Builders, LLC, 2026 Ind. App. LEXIS 48 (Ind. App. Feb. 12, 2026), the appellants requested that the court sanction the appellee for its improper citations in its brief. The court found that while the appellee’s brief contained case citations with typos and one case that does not exist, the mistakes seemed to be “inadvertent typographical errors” that “did not hinder [the court’s] review of [appellee’s] arguments.” Judge DeBoer admonished the appellee for failing to properly proofread the brief but declined to impose sanctions and affirmed the trial court’s judgment on unrelated grounds.

court: North Dakota - Supreme Court of North Dakota

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Sup. Ct. – Justice Daniel J. Crothers

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In City of Dickinson v. Helgeson, 2026 ND 34 (N.D. Feb. 12, 2026), the North Dakota Supreme Court granted the City’s request for sanctions against the pro se appellant for his citations to several fictitious cases in his brief in support of his appeal from the district court’s order designating him a vexatious litigant. The state Supreme Court determined that the appellant had similarly submitted fictitious citations to the district court. Judge Crothers noted that while North Dakota courts embrace the use of technology as a means of improving access to justice, litigants must verify arguments and authorities generated by these tools. The court concluded that the appellant was “responsible for the consequences of his errors” regardless of whether they were the result of GenAI. Judge Crothers awarded the City $500 as sanction for the appellant’s violation of N.D.R.App.P. 28(l), which requires that briefs presented to the court be accurate. The court further warned all litigants that citing to nonexistent cases could result in sanctions “appropriate under the circumstances.” The court affirmed the district court’s order on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Ind. – Magistrate Judge Mark J. Dinsmore

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Virgil v. Experian Info. Sols. Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 29090 (S.D. Ind. Feb. 12, 2026), the plaintiff’s attorneys cited to nonexistent caselaw and caselaw that did not relate to the propositions for which it was cited in their response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss. The plaintiff’s attorneys continued to cite to fabricated caselaw even after the defendants raised the issue. At the Show Cause Hearing, the court determined that only one of the plaintiff’s attorneys was responsible for the filings, as the other had never seen the briefs and did not consent to the addition of his signature. The filing attorney took full responsibility and informed the court that he had since implemented preventative measures going forward. Magistrate Judge Dinsmore found that the attorney’s failure to “make the requisite reasonable inquiry into the law” and to “take the most basic of actions—checking the citations in his own briefs,” regardless of whether GenAI was the source of the errors, violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. In imposing sanctions, the court considered the amount of sanctions awarded in similar cases and concluded that the “imposition of modest sanctions has failed to act as a deterrent.” Magistrate Judge Dinsmore recommended $10,000 in personal sanctions and referred the matter to the Chief Judge for consideration of further discipline. The court also ordered the attorney to provide a copy of the order to his client.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

Feb. 11, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se petitioner used GenAI in drafting her brief because it misrepresented several cited cases. Judge Baldock noted that the court had the power to impose sanctions in this instance but chose not to, though warned that “parties who use AI products should know they can and do ‘hallucinate’ legal citations.” The court denied the petition on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ore. App. – Judge Anna Joyce

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Powell v. Emp. Dep't, 2026 Ore. App. LEXIS 171 (Ore. App. Feb. 11, 2026), the pro se claimant’s brief contained nonexistent legal citations, quotations that the court could not find “anywhere in Oregon case law,” and cases cited for propositions they did not support. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the claimant admitted that the errors were inadvertent and the result of GenAI. The claimant was previously admonished by a different court for inaccurate citations and had acknowledged that the tools he used led to incorrect citations. The court noted that even though the claimant was on notice that his drafting process could result in fabricated authority, he failed to alert this court about the potential issue or amend his briefs. Judge Joyce concluded that the claimant’s actions prejudiced the other side and wasted the court’s resources, imposing a $500 monetary sanction as a penalty. The court affirmed the Employment Appeals Board’s decision on unrelated grounds.

The panel reminded the plaintiff-appellant that pro se litigants who use GenAI to assist with court filings must make sure that their filings do not include misrepresentations of fact or law, and that further misrepresentations may result in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 90th Jud. Dist. Tex. – Judge Phillip Gregory

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In his Standing Order Regarding Use of Artificial Intelligence (90th Jud. Dist. Tex. Feb. 10, 2026), Judge Gregory addresses the use of AI in all pending and future cases in the 90th Judicial District Court for Young and Stephens Counties, Texas.  The order observes that GenAI systems “are being incorporated into common professional use,” but notes that the technology “is currently unreliable and prone to bias, and often fabricates information.” The order requires all self-represented litigants and attorneys who utilize “any form of artificial intelligence for legal research or drafting” to certify, using a form appended to the order, that all “language, quotations, sources, citations, arguments, and legal analysis created or contributed to by [GenAI]” were verified as accurate “through traditional (non-AI) legal sources” before submission.  Filers must also acknowledge that they will be “held responsible, and potentially sanctioned” for failure to comply with the order. The order also prohibits the use of AI in the courtroom and court offices, including the use of AI “to record, listen to, or transcribe any activity in the courtroom from whatever vantage point, or court office.”  The attached certificate for litigants makes clear that failure to comply may result in sanctions under the Texas Rules of Disciplinary Conduct, Texas Rule of Civil Procedure 10 regarding the withdrawal of an attorney, the inherent power of the court, or contempt of court.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Colo. – Magistrate Judge Maritza D. Braswell (Feb. 10, 2026) – GenAI | Filings/Drafting | Warning

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Labonte v. Bokf, N.A., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 27530 (D. Colo. Feb. 10, 2026), the pro se plaintiff's responses to defendants' motions to dismiss in a foreclosure-related action contained “multiple inaccuracies that may stem from the improper use of [GenAI].” The court provided several illustrative examples, including a nonexistent quotation attributed to a real case, a real case cited with an incorrect reporter that did not stand for the proposition asserted, and a real case misidentified as a Colorado court decision when it was in fact decided by another federal circuit and had no connection to the cited proposition. The court “strongly condemn[ed] the submission of inaccurate citations and mischaracterized authority,” finding that “[s]uch errors undermine the reliability of Plaintiff's filings and diminish Plaintiff’s credibility.” However, “[o]n careful consideration of the errors and circumstances of this case, including its dismissal,” the court declined to impose disciplinary sanctions. The court then granted the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.Y. App. Div – Judge Cynthia S. Kern

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Matter of Zareh, 2026 N.Y. App. Div. LEXIS 689 (N.Y. App. Div. Feb. 10, 2026), the court directed the respondent to demonstrate why reciprocal discipline should not be imposed in New York against the New York-barred respondent for GenAI misconduct which occurred and for which the respondent was disciplined in the Northern District of Texas. After an order to show cause, the district court had found that the drafting attorney, who the respondent supervised, filed a brief drafted using GenAI. The district court also found that although the respondent did not use GenAI or know that the drafting attorney used it, the respondent’s failure to review the cited cases “constituted bad faith” and was a violation of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The district court issued a public reprimand as sanction. In considering the Attorney Grievance Committee’s motion for reciprocal discipline, the New York court found that respondent had no available defenses: He received sufficient due process, there was adequate proof establishing misconduct, and the conduct for which the respondent was sanctioned constituted misconduct under the New York Rules of Professional Conduct. The court concluded that the appropriate sanction should be equivalent to that issued by the district court and ordered a public censure.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Ind. – Judge Matthew P. Brookman

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In McCarty v. Ind. Gaming Comm'n, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 54449 (S.D. Ind. Feb. 10, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response brief that included a citation the court could not locate. The court noted that it was “unclear why Plaintiff cited at least one case that does not appear to exist.” Although the court declined to impose sanctions, it cautioned all parties to adhere to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11, “especially where [GenAI] may have been used to assist parties in preparing filings.” On unrelated grounds, the court granted the defendant’s motion for summary judgement.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Amarsingh v. Frontier Airlines, Inc., 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 3928 (10th Cir. Feb. 9, 2026), the court imposed sanctions on the plaintiff for her misuse of GenAI in researching and drafting her appellate brief. The plaintiff, an attorney representing herself, submitted a brief with serious flaws, including citations to nonexistent cases and attribution of propositions or quotations to real cases that did not stand for those propositions or contain the quotations. The court ordered the plaintiff to provide accurate copies of the suspect cases or explain how the fabricated citations ended up in her brief and to show cause why she should not be sanctioned. In response, the plaintiff admitted that she had used ChatGPT as a research and drafting aid, which generated the fabricated case citations, and acknowledged that she did not verify that the cases existed or that the quotations actually appeared in the cited cases. The court found that while there is “nothing inherently problematic with the use of GenAI in the practice of law, careless use of the tool can waste both judicial resources and the opposing party's time and money, and it can damage the credibility of the legal system.” Proceeding under Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 38, the court determined that the appeal was frivolous as argued, noting that an appeal may be frivolous as argued if supporting briefs contain “citations of inapplicable or irrelevant authorities” or “misrepresent[] facts or law to the court.” The court declined to impose the “harsh sanction” of dismissal but ordered the plaintiff to pay $1,000 to the defendant to offset attorney fees and costs incurred in responding to the problems identified in the plaintiff's brief. The court also directed the Clerk's Office to transmit a copy of its Order and Judgment to the attorney-disciplinary authority of the State of Maryland.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.S.C. – Judge Sherri A. Lydon

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Bruce v. United States, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 26192 (D.S.C. Feb. 9, 2026), the court observed that the pro se plaintiff’s “filings . . . bear characteristics the court has increasingly observed in briefs generated by [GenAI] programs.” These characteristics included a nonexistent quotation from a local rule and other citation errors. Judge Lydon did not impose sanctions, but warned the plaintiff that he could be subject to sanctions should he cite to AI-generated legal authority in the future. The court adopted the Magistrate Judge’s R&R and granted the United States’ motion to dismiss without prejudice on other grounds.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Tatum v. Am. Amicable Life Ins. Co. of Tex., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22283 (E.D. Tex. Jan. 9, 2026), a pro se plaintiff was accused of citing at least six nonexistent cases that appeared to be the product of GenAI. The court confirmed that it could not locate any of the cited cases, observing that the citations “make it impossible for these cases to exist because they would overlap with other existing cases.” The court emphasized that under Local Rule CV-11(g), litigants who use GenAI “remain[] responsible for the accuracy and quality of legal documents” and must verify all content for compliance with Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court further found that the plaintiff violated the court's standing order requiring certification of GenAI use. The court noted the harms of GenAI misuse, including wasted time and resources for opposing counsel and the court, potential reputational harm to judges and parties, and promotion of “cynicism about the legal profession and the American judicial system.” The court declined to consider the plaintiff's legal arguments supported by the fabricated citations and warned that any further GenAI misuse may result in sanctions under Rule 11.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Bettis v. Gaston, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 26147 (N.D. Ill. Feb. 9, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s response brief contained citations that the court identified as “hallmarks of [GenAI].” The court noted that the plaintiff cited a nonexistent case, a real case that did not support the asserted proposition, and a nonexistent quotation attributed to another real case. Citing recent Seventh Circuit authority, the court reminded the plaintiff that “all litigants—represented and unrepresented—must read their filings and take reasonable care to avoid misrepresentations, factual and legal.” The court warned the plaintiff that future filings submitted without exercising reasonable care to check their accuracy may result in sanctions, finding that "[b]ased on the current available technology, relying on computer-generated language without verification is not reasonable care." The court granted the defendants' motion to dismiss with prejudice on other grounds, finding the plaintiff's constitutional claims time-barred and his state law claim barred for lack of a private cause of action.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Tax Ct. – Judge Mark Van Dyke Holmes

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Clinco v. Comm’r, T.C. Memo 2026-16, 2026 Tax Ct. Memo LEXIS 16 (Tax Ct. Feb. 9, 2026), the court suspected that the petitioners cited cases hallucinated by GenAI because they do not exist as cited. The court warned that submitting “fictitious caselaw” violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, but did not impose sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: C.D. Cal. – Judge Maame Ewusi-Mensah Frimpong

results.summary: In Khan v. NSO et al., No. 2:25-cv-11961 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 6, 2026), Judge Frimpong issued a resources document for the pro se plaintiff. The document notes that while the court does not restrict any party's use of AI, parties are reminded that AI “may output information that is false, inaccurate, or biased” and that it is the parties’ responsibility to “personally ensure the accuracy of any statements and representations made in the course of the litigation even if it has been generated by AI.” The court’s resources guide also directs the parties to the California State Bar's Practical Guidance on the use of AI.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Colo. – Judge Nina Y. Wang

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Berg v. United Airlines, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 25650 (D. Colo. Feb. 6, 2026), the court denied the pro se plaintiff’s motion for extension and motion for reconsideration of Judge Prose’s prior order denying the plaintiff’s motion to compel discovery. Both Magistrate Judge Prose and Judge Wang had previously warned the plaintiff that her “filings contained erroneous and hallucinated citations consistent with misuse of [GenAI]” and that further violations could result in sanctions. Despite the warning, the plaintiff’s motion for extension cited to nonexistent and misrepresented cases. The court denied both motions on other grounds. Judge Wang imposed a filing restriction to counter the plaintiff’s “untimely, duplicative, and noncompliant” filings. The restriction prevented the plaintiff from filing any further motions without leave of court before the court rules on the defendant’s motion for summary judgment or vacates the order, noted that it will not grant leave without a certification that the use of AI is in compliance with the court’s standing order.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Conn. – Judge Stefan R. Underhill

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Davila v. Roblen, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 24971 (D. Conn. Feb. 6, 2026), the court addressed the conduct of the plaintiff's attorneys who filed a brief containing quotes generated by GenAI from legal authorities that did not contain those quotes. The court noted that one of the attorneys in question had previously been sanctioned by the court for submitting filings with GenAI hallucinations. See Cojom v. Roblen, LLC., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 225325 (D. Conn. Nov. 17, 2025). The plaintiff's lead attorney admitted that he used a GenAI tool, failed to verify the citations it produced, and based legal arguments on the fabricated quotes. The court found that this conduct constituted a “negligent[] . . . [failure] to perform [his] responsibility as an officer of the court” and negatively impacted the client, opposing parties, and the court itself by depriving the client of arguments based on legitimate precedent and requiring opposing counsel and the court to contend with “arguments based on phony law.” Noting the lead attorney’s acceptance of responsibility and commitment to remediation, the court ordered that he complete three hours of CLE on responsible AI use and publish a written article on the topic. The court declined to sanction co-counsel (who had requested to file a “corrected version” of the erroneous brief without disclosing that GenAI hallucinations were the reason for doing so), concluding that her lack of candor was not “a false statement of fact or law.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ind. Ct. App. – Judge Paul A. Felix

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mutugu v. Kiaraho, 2026 Ind. App. Unpub. LEXIS 203 (Ind. Ct. App. Feb. 6, 2026), the court dismissed the pro se appellant's appeal due to his “significant noncompliance with Indiana Appellate Rule 46,” which requires appellate briefs to be both accurate and follow certain filing guidelines, and awarded the appellee attorneys' fees. The appellant's brief was “deficient and violate[d] Appellate Rule 46(A) in almost every possible way,” including hallucinated and inaccurate citations. Of the 21 legal authorities cited in the appellant's argument, 5 cases did not exist, and 11 of the remaining authorities were “incorrectly cited or described.” Judge Felix observed that given the appellant's “citations to ‘nonexistent legal authorities and to real legal authorities that have nothing to do with the propositions they purport to support,’” it was likely that the appellant used GenAI to draft his brief. The court noted that “[c]itations to fictitious, AI-generated authority is a growing problem nationwide” and that “[c]ourts have sanctioned both attorneys and pro se litigants for including them in briefs.” Because the appellant's noncompliance substantially impeded appellate review and his appeal was “permeated with meritlessness,” the court granted the appellee's motion to dismiss, denied the appellant's motion to amend his brief, and remanded for a determination of reasonable appellate attorneys' fees.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D.N.Y. – Judge Katherine Polk Failla

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary:
In Flycatcher Corp. v. Affable Ave. LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 23980 (S.D.N.Y. Feb. 5, 2026), counsel for defendant submitted a brief containing at least thirteen hallucinated citations and eight faulty citations to caselaw that existed but did not stand for the proposition he claimed. When co-defendant's counsel alerted the attorney to these errors, he offered a convoluted excuse involving his use of CaseText, CoCounsel, and vLex for research and cite-checking, citing his lack of access to traditional legal research databases. After co-defendant’s counsel reported the attorney to the court, an order to show cause was issued. In response, the attorney filed a submission containing “conspicuously florid prose”, including extended quotes from irrelevant books of fiction, and a quote misattributed to a case when it came from an article discussing that case. Despite explicit warnings, the attorney then filed a proposed reply brief containing another nonexistent citation, which led to a sanctions hearing.

At the sanctions hearing, the attorney was placed under oath and admitted to using various GenAI tools including vLex, Paxton AI, and NotebookLM, but “attempted to minimize his responsibility.” The court found that the attorney's AI misuse resulting in erroneous citations, exacerbated by his "insouciant approach to cite-checking," was done in bad faith, concluding that he knew or consciously avoided learning that the cases he cited were not warranted by existing law, and continuously presented to the court faulty, GenAI-created citations and briefs. The court weighed the five factors relevant to terminal sanctions, finding: (i) intentional bad faith; (ii) prejudice to the court and parties; (iii) a pattern of misbehavior rather than an isolated incident; (iv) failure to correct the misconduct; and (v) likelihood of future misconduct. Accordingly, pursuant to Rule 11, the court entered default judgment against the attorney's client, struck the defendant's filings, and granted leave for co-defendant's counsel to submit a fee application.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Utah – Magistrate Judge Cecilia M. Romero

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Odunze v. Lake Effect, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 24146 (D. Utah Feb. 4, 2026), the court addressed an opposition brief filed by the pro se plaintiff. The court noted that the plaintiff cited to a case that the court was “unable to locate.” Magistrate Judge Romero admonished the plaintiff that “[i]f artificial intelligence technology is being used,” he must “be in compliance with Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11,” which requires that legal contentions be warranted by existing law. The court warned that “[c]ontinued misconduct of this nature may lead to sanctions.” On unrelated grounds, the court granted one of the defendant’s motions and denied the other as moot.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Cal. – Magistrate Judge Susan van Keulen

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Rako v. VMware LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232131 (N.D. Cal. Nov. 25, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition briefs to the defendants’ motions to dismiss contained various “inadvertent citation errors,” which he did not attempt to correct until nearly six weeks after the defendants flagged these errors in their reply briefs. Judge Van Keulen reminded the plaintiff that all parties, regardless of their status of representation, must ensure that all assertions are verifiable and accurate. As such, the court ordered the plaintiff to include a footnote corresponding to “each citation located through AI” in all future filings that confirms that the authority is real and accurate (i.e., “Located through AI; Checked.”). On February 4, 2026, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 23692, after observing the parties “weaponizing accusations regarding the use of AI” since her November order, the court imposed an additional requirement: all disputes over whether a party has properly used GenAI are now subject to the meet-and-confer and joint statement requirements that apply to discovery disputes under the court's standing order . Any disputes that cannot be resolved through good-faith conferral must be presented in a joint statement not to exceed five pages.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.N.J. - Judge Evelyn Padin

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Kabir v. WebMD LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22400 (D.N.J. Feb. 3, 2026), the court addressed the pro se plaintiff's repeated non-compliance with filing rules and prior warnings about hallucinated citations. The plaintiff, who had filed numerous motions and voluminous submissions throughout the litigation, “repeatedly attempted to circumvent” applicable filing rules and directly contacted both the Chief Judge and the presiding judge in violation of the court's policies. The court revoked the plaintiff's electronic filing privileges and ordered the Clerk of Court not to docket any future submissions through the alternate document submission system. Judge Padin also noted that the plaintiff's opposition brief “relies on hallucinated quotations,” citing a nonexistent case, despite prior warnings that “[h]allucinated citations will not be excused” and that the court “may sanction Plaintiff for further uses of hallucinated citations.” The court reiterated its prior warning that hallucinated citations “will not be permitted under any circumstances.” Judge Padin cautioned that if the plaintiff continues to inappropriately rely on GenAI, he may be subject to a pre-filing injunction or other sanctions, and denied the plaintiff’s pending motion with prejudice.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Kan. – Judge Julie A. Robinson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Lexos Media IP, LLC v. Overstock.com, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258649 (D. Kan. Dec. 15, 2025), the court granted in part and denied in part the plaintiff’s motion for leave to file a corrected opposition to a Daubert motion after the defendant identified “nonexistent quotations,” “nonexistent or incorrect citations,” and “misrepresentations about cited authority” in the plaintiff’s original brief—errors the plaintiff attributed to counsel’s unverified use of GenAI. The court held that allowing substantive changes would function as an improper sur-reply and that Plaintiff failed to show excusable neglect under Fed. R. Civ. P. 6(b), emphasizing the prejudice to the defendant and the waste of judicial resources caused by the defective filing. Citing recent authority warning that careless use of GenAI can waste resources and undermine credibility, the court declined to permit a “do-over,” denied any additions of text or authority, and struck the original response. The court ordered Plaintiff to file a corrected brief by December 19, 2025 that omits all nonexistent quotations, nonexistent/incorrect/inapposite citations, and misrepresentations about cited authority. On February 2, 2026, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 20994, following the corrected brief’s filing, the court issued an order admonishing all five plaintiff attorneys and sanctioning four of them, imposing fines ranging from $1,000 to $5,000, revoking one attorney’s pro hac vice status and ordered him to self-report to the state’s legal disciplinary authority, ordering the managing member of the law firm to create a more effective GenAI firm policy, and accepting local counsel’s “voluntarily sanction[]” of not serving as local counsel for a year. The court declined to sanction the fifth attorney, the “most junior attorney on this matter,” as he had no responsibility to review his supervisors’ work.

linkToCourtOrder.text: DeKalb Cnty. State Ct., Ga. – Judge Ana María Martinez

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Am. Express Nat'l Bank v. Flippin, 2026 Ga. State LEXIS 185 (Ga. Cnty. Ct. Feb. 2, 2026), the pro se defendant filed an “avalanche” of GenAI-generated motions, notices, responses, and other filings in a debt collection case, totaling approximately sixty or more distinct filings since October 2025. The court had previously entered a standing order requiring any party who uses AI to disclose such use and certify that every citation has been verified as accurate, applicable to all parties including pro se litigants. Despite including certifications of compliance with the Standing Order, the defendant’s filings were “replete with manufactured arguments and hallucinations,” invoking both real cases that did not support the propositions for which they were cited and “fictitious authority pulled from thin air.” The struck the defendant’s motion to compel from the record after concluding that it was “based exclusively on two nonexistent cases.” Although the defendant admitted to having submitted nine fictitious cases in good faith, the court observed that his “torrent of filings continued with frivolous arguments and misrepresentations of the law, which are just as troublesome.” Because of the defendant’s pro se status and his good faith correction concerning the fictitious cases, the court declined to impose sanctions but warned that “future abuses in this Court or others may not be met with such lenience.” On unrelated grounds, the court entered judgment in favor of the plaintiff.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Cal. – Magistrate Judge Susan van Keulen

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In her updated Standing Order for Civil and Discovery Referral Matters (N.D. Cal. Feb. 2, 2026), Magistrate Judge Susan van Keulen sets forth procedures applicable to civil and discovery matters before her court in the Northern District of California, including provisions governing GenAI use. The standing order provides that the signature of counsel or a self-represented party on any submission containing GenAI-generated content, including citations generated by GenAI, constitutes a certification that the signing attorney or self-represented party has personally verified the content's accuracy. The court will impute any errors by GenAI tools to the attorney or party whose signature appears on the document containing those errors. The order warns that the failure to verify the accuracy of submissions, particularly the accuracy of citations to law and evidence, may be grounds for sanctions. Further, the order requires counsel to maintain records of all prompts or inquiries submitted to any GenAI tools in the event those records become relevant “at any point.”

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Smith v. Kreeger, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22187 (S.D. Fla. Feb. 2, 2026), a pro se plaintiff filed a complaint challenging state court custody proceedings. The court observed that the plaintiff's complaint relied on “non-existent case citations that appear to have been generated by [GenAI].” The court warned that the plaintiff's “pro se status does not absolve [her] of the responsibility to abide by a duty of candor to the Court” and emphasized that sanctions against parties who submit fabricated citations, including dismissal, are not uncommon. On unrelated grounds, the court recommended the denial of the plaintiff’s motion.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Nev. – Magistrate Judge Nancy J. Koppe

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Schaaf v. Nellis Auction Holdings, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22121 (D. Nev. Jan. 30, 2026), the court addressed plaintiff counsel’s continued misconduct, including hallucinated citations in three briefs stemming from the use of GenAI. In declining to impose terminating sanctions, the court acknowledged that case-dispositive sanctions are reserved for extreme situations and distinguished this case from one in which the complaint itself contained potentially hallucinated factual allegations. The court explained that here, the defendants pointed only to incorrect citations in briefing that had already been addressed through the underlying motion practice. The court, however, admonished plaintiff's counsel for his “lapse in his duties as an officer of the Court,” warning that continued misuse of GenAI would result in serious sanctions, including “case-dispositive sanctions imposed on his clients,” monetary penalties, and referral to the state bar. The court concluded by denying the defendants’ motion for case-terminating sanctions, motion to strike, and motion to stay.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Mo. – Judge Matthew Thomas Schelp

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mills v. City of St. Louis, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 19989 (E.D. Mo. Jan. 30, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff used GenAI in preparing filings, which “contained misquotations or misrepresentations about case law he cited and discussed” with the “egregiousness of these misstatements increas[ing] over time.” In response to the order to show cause, the plaintiff admitted to using “electronic drafting tools that paraphrase legal principles.” Judge Schelp concluded that the plaintiff violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 by submitting fictitious and misleading cases to the court, regardless of whether the plaintiff used GenAI. In determining the appropriate sanction, the court considered that the plaintiff “made numerous misstatements in multiple filings,” continued making false representations after the court put the plaintiff on notice, and is well-educated and thus “should have known better.” Judge Schelp therefore reasoned that an “admonition, reprimand, or censure” would not sufficiently deter plaintiff from repeating this conduct, so the court dismissed the action with prejudice.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Wis. – Judge Pamela Pepper

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In SEC v. Nantomah, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 18126 (E.D. Wis. Jan. 30, 2026), the court noted that the pro se defendant’s citations in his response to the SEC’s complaint “bear the hallmarks of generative artificial intelligence.” Judge Pepper reminded the defendant of his obligations under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and warned that future filings that “contain citations to nonexistent cases or that quote language that does not appear in a cited case—whether the document is AI-generated or not” may result in sanctions. The court granted the SEC’s motion to strike the defendant’s answer on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ind. Ct. App. – Judge Melissa S. Mattingly-May

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Wilcox v. Gingrinch, 2026 Ind. App. LEXIS 29 (Ind. Ct. App. Jan. 30, 2026), the pro se appellants cited a “substantial number” of cases that “either do not exist or have nothing to do with the propositions they purport to support.” The court observed that the “pattern and nature of the citation errors in the [] brief strongly suggest the use of [GenAI] without adequate verification.” Furthermore, Judge May could not evaluate the merits of the appellants’ primary legal argument because it was based on fabricated authorities. The court therefore considered those issues waived, noting that “[h]ad the [plaintiffs] performed basic verification, the waiver we find today could have been avoided.” Because this was the appellants’ first infraction, Judge May did not impose sanctions, but cautioned that “the submission of briefs citing nonexistent authorities in future proceedings may result in sanctions.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Wash. – Judge Tiffany M. Cartwright

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Greene v. GSK PLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 17248 (W.D. Wash. Jan. 29, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited “several authorities which do not support her proposition or do not contain the text which she quotes” in her motion for court-assisted service, leading the court to suspect GenAI misuse. Judge Cartwright reminded the plaintiff that she is bound by Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 which requires that “legal contentions are warranted by existing law…” and warned that she could face sanctions if she submits nonexistent authority to the court in the future. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion without prejudice on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Wis. – Judge James D. Peterson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mims v. Brown, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 23032 (W.D. Wis. Jan. 29, 2026), the court addressed filings by the pro se plaintiff that contained indications of GenAI use. The court noted that several of the plaintiff's recent filings had metadata in PDF files listing ChatGPT as the source and one document bearing the ChatGPT logo.
