Judge Peterson warned the plaintiff to “proceed with caution,” observing that GenAI-generated court filings “often contain inaccurate factual representations, 'hallucinated' citations to non-existent cases, or fake quotations.” The court emphasized that all litigants, regardless of representation status, have an obligation to “read their filings and take reasonable care to avoid misrepresentations, whether factual or legal,” and that failure to do so may result in sanctions under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11, “including dismissal of their case with prejudice.” Judge Peterson encouraged the plaintiff not to use GenAI to draft submissions to the court, noting that even when such documents do not contain misrepresentations, “AI-generated legal documents often appear formal and legalistic, but contain very little helpful substance.” The court advised that it would be more helpful for the plaintiff to explain his position “using simple, everyday language, even if the result is that his filing looks less like a formal legal document drafted by a lawyer.” Judge Peterson concluded by reminding the plaintiff that if he does choose to use GenAI, he must still review his documents before filing and ensure that the facts and the law are correct, as he remains responsible for the content of documents filed in court.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Colo. – Magistrate Judge Susan Prose

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Berg v. United Airlines, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 15973 (D. Colo. Jan. 28, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff used GenAI in her Motion to Compel Discovery because it included citations to “seemingly nonexistent cases.” The plaintiff included such citations despite the fact that she had also “included an artificial intelligence certification with each of her motions,” and the court had warned her on “multiple occasions to either avoid using artificial intelligence or to very carefully review her filings in order to avoid this exact scenario.”  Magistrate Judge Prose did not impose sanctions but warned that she will consider recommending dismissal or other sanctions if the plaintiff repeats this behavior in the future

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Kan. – Chief Judge John W. Broomes

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In his Standing Order on Use of Artificial Intelligence (“AI”) in Preparing Court Filings (D. Kan. Jan. 28, 2026), Chief Judge Broomes reminds litigants that “AI tools can produce false statements of fact or law, including citations to fabricated or incorrect legal authority.” The court warns that “all lawyers and parties, including pro se litigants . . . . are responsible for the content of their own filings,” must verify all content “drafted or assisted by an AI tool,” and may not make or fail to correct “a false or misleading statement of material fact or law.” The court has discretion to require a party to include a sworn statements disclosing the extent of AI use and certifying personal verification of the filing’s contents. Violation of the standing order could result in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Tex. – Magistrate Judge K. Nicole Mitchell

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Walder v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 34005 (E.D. Tex. Jan. 27, 2026), Magistrate Judge K. Nicole Mitchell recommended denying the pro se plaintiff’s motion for summary judgment in a Fair Credit Reporting Act case and warned the plaintiff about her citation to fictitious cases. The defendant pointed out in its joint response that many of the cases cited in the plaintiff’s motions either did not exist or did not support the plaintiff’s propositions, suggesting the citations may have been the result of GenAI “hallucinations.” The court confirmed that it was unable to locate any of the three cases the plaintiff cited in her motion. Magistrate Judge Mitchell reminded the plaintiff that Local Rule CV-11(g) warns litigants that GenAI “may produce factually or legally inaccurate content” and requires litigants to “review and verify all content” for compliance with Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court emphasized that filing papers with citations to nonexistent law is “unacceptable” and that a “fake opinion is not ‘existing law.’” In light of the plaintiff's pro se status and consistent with other courts' treatment of similar issues, the court declined to impose sanctions but warned the plaintiff that further citation to nonexistent cases and noncompliance with Local Rule CV-11(g) may result in sanctions, “including her pleadings and other papers being struck, filing restrictions, dismissal of her claims, and monetary penalties.”

court: New York - Suffolk County Supreme Court

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.Y. Sup. C. – Judge Linda Kevins

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Cassata v Michael Macrina Architect, P.C., 2026 N.Y. Misc. LEXIS 325 (N.Y. Sup. C. Jan. 27, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel alleged that the defendant’s counsel plagiarized a brief that itself was improperly drafted using GenAI. In response to the order to show cause why the defendant’s attorney, supervising attorney, and law firm should not be sanctioned, the law firm stated that the attorney no longer worked at the firm and the supervising attorney had “no indication” that the attorney’s “work was plagiarized or improperly relied upon by [GenAI].” He further stated that the firm conducted additional training on the importance of compliance with the GenAI provisions in the firm handbook. At the hearing, the defendant’s attorney denied GenAI use in research but “appeared sincerely sorry” and admitted that she “found a brief on Westlaw which she incorporated into her opposition without verifying the citations, quotations, and propositions of law.” The court found this unpersuasive, and concluded that “citing non-existent cases, quotations, and specific propositions of law for which they do not stand and/or do not support, is unethical and sanctionable conduct” regardless of GenAI use. The courts considered the defense attorneys’ apologies, request for a “redo,” and inexperience as an attorney, ultimately concluding that sanctions were still warranted. In determining the amount of sanctions, the court found that the defendant’s attorney failed to verify cited authority before filing her opposition; that the attorney, supervising attorney, and the law firm “failed to promptly correct the record once inaccuracies were discovered”; and that the firm and supervising attorney lacked proper oversight and safeguards to prevent further misconduct. The court fined both the attorney and supervising attorney $1,000 (payable to the Lawyer’s Fund for Client Protection) and awarded $8,000 in attorney’s fees payable by the defendant’s law firm. The court further ordered that the attorney serve a copy of the decision and order on the clerk of court and Lawyer’s Fund for Client Protection.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 17th Jud. Cir. Fla. – Chief Judge Carol-Lisa Phillips

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: Chief Judge Phillips’ standing order titled, “Use of Artificial Intelligence in Court Filings” permits the use of GenAI to assist in preparation, research, drafting of pleadings and documents, filing, and discovery, but requires that all GenAI-generated information have “appropriate human oversight,” including “checking citations, verifying factual claims, and analyzing conclusions.” The order further requires that any filing prepared with GenAI assistance identify the specific tool used and include a certification attesting to their personal review and independent verification of the filing’s accuracy, noting that failure to do so could result in sanctions (as can the submission of “false or misleading AI-generated content”). The order also emphasizes that attorneys may use GenAI only to the extent they can reasonably guarantee compliance with their ethical obligations under the Rules Regulating the Florida Bar. The order reminds pro se litigants that they must be aware of GenAI's propensity to generate fictitious legal authorities and must ensure such citations do not appear in court filings.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Pa. – Judge Mark A. Kearney

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary:
In Lifetime Well LLC v. IBSpot.com Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 13363 (E.D. Pa. Jan. 26, 2026), the court identified a briefing “contain[ing] at least eight false case citation hallucinations generated by artificial intelligence” submitted jointly by out-of-state counsel and local co-counsel for plaintiffs. In response, the court issued a show cause order, after which both sets of counsel admitted lapses in diligence tied to GenAI use. The out-of-state counsel terminated at least three attorneys responsible for preparing the briefing in response to the show cause order on her own accord, an action the court criticized by stating, “[the senior lawyer] did not explain why she did not use this mistake as a lesson rather than a reason to fire the new lawyer.” During the senior attorney’s investigation of her subordinate’s behavior, she found that the junior attorney relied on Lexis+ AI and LexisNexis Protégé as their primary research tools.

The court also noted the peril out-of-state counsel placed local counsel in by asking him to sign and file the motion without diligently checking its citations, while admonishing local counsel for failing to properly vet the briefing before submitting it to the court. After learning of the filing’s deficiencies, local counsel voluntarily took CLE courses related to GenAI, reviewed all recent decisions from the Third Circuit and other courts related to GenAI misuse, and made a new agreement with out-of-state counsel to allow for ample review time of all motions prior to future filings with the court.

The court found that both attorneys violated Rule 11 by submitting false citations and quotations without independently verifying the authorities, emphasizing that the signing attorney remains the final auditor of all legal and factual assertions. As tailored deterrence, the court imposed a $4,000 fine on the out-of-state attorney personally and required remedial educational outreach (including providing a copy of the court’s memorandum and order, the out-of-state counsel’s new GenAI firm-wide policy, and a cover letter to both local legal organizations and justices from other courts to whom the subordinate attorney previously submitted hallucinated citations to). The court imposed non-monetary sanctions on local counsel in the form of similar outreach to a local bar association. The court declined to refer either attorney to their respective bar disciplinary panels, and reminded all attorneys to use caution when submitting authority to the court.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Md. – District Judge Deborah K. Chasanow

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Future Field Sols., LLC v. Norstrand, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 12278 (D. Md. Jan. 23, 2026), the court addressed defendants’ request for a show cause order and sanctions premised on indications that plaintiff’s reply brief was drafted using GenAI (including “incorrect case citations, unusual grammatical errors, responses to arguments [not made], and false factual assertions”). In ruling on the motion, the court explained that no rule forbids GenAI, but emphasized that Rule 11(b) requires a reasonable pre filing inquiry and that submissions drafted with GenAI without appropriate verification are sanctionable, further noting that courts are more likely to impose sanctions “when a party doubles down” on denials “rather than accept responsibility for doing so at the first opportunity.” The court found plaintiff counsel’s response to the defendants’ request did not fully address the accusations and, by partially shifting blame to the client, suggested insufficient attorney oversight. Although the court declined to issue a show cause order and denied the sanctions request without prejudice, Judge Chasanow noted that the issue of sanctions may be revisited later if appropriate. On unrelated grounds, the court granted summary judgment to the third party defendant and largely to the counterclaim defendants, denied the counterclaim plaintiff’s cross motions, dismissed any remaining claims, and ordered the company to show cause why default should not be entered.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Tex. – Magistrate Judge Renée  Harris Toliver

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Olali v. Ampex Brands, LLC, No. 3:25-CV-1602-K-BK, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 37552 (N.D. Tex. Jan. 23, 2026), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff prepared his filings using GenAI because he cited to nonexistent cases, but failed to disclose that GenAI use, in violation of local rules. The court noted that this conduct also violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court found that the plaintiff’s “filings—built on AI-generated cases that stand for legal propositions in direct contravention of actual case law—are ‘the epitome of baseless.’” Magistrate Judge Toliver recommended granting the defendant’s motion to dismiss with prejudice, in part based on the GenAI misuse, and warned that further violations may result in sanctions.

court: Georgia - Ga. Ct. App., Second Div.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ga. Ct. App. – Judges Kenneth B. Hodges, Christopher McFadden, and John Pipkin concurring

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Tolbert v. State, 2026 Ga. App. LEXIS 21 (Ga. Ct. App. Jan. 22, 2026), the court found the appellant’s brief rife with briefing defects, including reliance on irrelevant or overruled authority, quotations that do not appear in the cited cases, and material misrepresentations of the record. Although the court declined to make “factual findings here as to how or why [plaintiff] provided inappropriate authority to this Court,” notably not accusing him of misusing GenAI, the court concluded that “such misrepresentations exist and this appeal is frivolous as a result.” The court then ordered the appellant’s counsel to serve the opinion on his client and the State Bar of Georgia, file a certificate of compliance within ten days, and complete three hours of CLE on brief writing before filing any further briefs, warning that future violations may trigger contempt and jeopardize his authority to practice, and noting that monetary sanctions are not authorized in criminal cases. The court affirmed the judgment, in part because of the brief’s deficiencies.

court: Texas - Tex. Ct. App., Third Dist., Austin
applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Campbell v. Campbell, 2026 Tex. App. LEXIS 475 (Tex. App. Jan. 22, 2026), the pro se appellant filed motions that cited authorities that do not exist and purported quotations that do not appear in the referenced opinions—conduct the court observed suggests use of GenAI. The court cautioned that pro se and represented litigants are held to the same rules and that use of hallucinated citations can lead to serious consequences, citing decisions that have imposed significant sanctions for such misuse. The court dismissed the appeal on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Cal. – Magistrate Judge Chi Soo Kim

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In That Xiong v. Wofford, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 12054 (E.D. Cal. Jan. 21, 2026), the court flagged two citations in a briefing filed by petitioner’s counsel (which included a certified law student and a supervising attorney) that “appeared to be [GenAI] hallucinations” and directed counsel—via a minute order—to be prepared to address them at a hearing. Petitioner’s counsel promptly filed a notice admitting error, apologizing, and supplying citations to actual, relevant cases in place of the hallucinated authorities. At the hearing, the court addressed the hallucinated citations and imposed corrective measures, including ordering counsel to obtain the transcript, provide it to the law student and any staff who assisted with the filings, require them to read it, and file written confirmation. On unrelated grounds, the court denied the motion.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Ga. – Magistrate Judge Catherine M. Salinas

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Parker v. Labat, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 11877 (N.D. Ga. Jan. 21, 2026), the court issued a show‑cause order after the plaintiff’s brief contained “misstatements of law, non‑existent quotations, and non-existent case citations”—conduct the court emphasized is sanctionable whether stemming from GenAI or “old‑fashioned” attorney error, because citations submitted to the court must be warranted by existing law. The order required a sworn explanation and a statement disclosing whether counsel used GenAI, yet counsel failed to respond in any way. Concluding that the submission of fabricated or misrepresented authorities and the assertion of frivolous claims violated Rule 11(b), the court recommended sanctions, including a monetary fine of $1,500, mandatory CLE, and a sworn verification in future filings attesting that all citations and quotations were personally checked for accuracy. On unrelated grounds, the court recommended dismissing the plaintiff’s complaint.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Nev. – Judge Cristina D. Silva

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Jian Lu v. USCIS, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9666 (D. Nev. Jan. 20, 2026), the pro se petitioners included both AI prompts given to a GenAI tool and the GenAI tool’s incorrect legal arguments in their motion for reconsideration. The court recognized that AI is a powerful tool that can assist pro se litigants but flagged that the petitioners here “treaded into perilous territory” based on their “clear ‘cut and paste’ arguments” and lack of supporting legal authority. Though the motion was ultimately denied on the merits, Judge Silva warned the petitioners that they must comply with “applicable law and procedural rules,” including Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b)(2)’s requirement that all claims “are warranted by existing law.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: M.D. Ga. – Judge Tilman E. Self, III

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Med. Buyers Grp., LLC v. Pence, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9814 (M.D. Ga. Jan. 20, 2026), the defendants identified hallucinated cases cited by the plaintiff in its response to the defendants’ motion to dismiss. In response to an initial order to show cause as to why sanctions should not be granted, plaintiff’s counsel admitted to submitting hallucinated cases and explained what he had done to remediate this error, including (i) informing his client of the error, (ii) asking defendants’ counsel to provide attorney’s fees for the matter; (iii) confirming the accuracy of the other citations in its briefing; (iv) drafting amended responses; (v) modifying the firm’s cite check procedures; and (vi) attending a CLE course on “Common AI Mistakes.” As the defendants did not separately move for sanctions as required by Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(c)(2), the court imposed sanctions on plaintiff’s counsel based on its inherent authority. Judge Self acknowledged plaintiff’s counsel “sincer[e] . . . apology” but indicated that even unintentionally citing hallucinated cases constitutes “bad faith.” As such, the court ordered plaintiff’s counsel to pay $10,000 in attorney’s fees divided in half between the defendants’ two sets of attorneys.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Ohio – Judge Jeffrey P. Hopkins

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Lattimore v. Tri-State Auto Sales, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 10013 (S.D. Ohio Jan. 20, 2026), the court granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss the pro se plaintiff’s complaint and flagged in a footnote that the plaintiff admitted to using an unidentified GenAI tool “in preparation of his objections” to the magistrate judge’s report and recommendations. Notwithstanding his pro se status, Judge Hopkins reminded the plaintiff of his obligations to adhere to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b) and warned that he could face sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Wash. – District Judge David G. Estudillo

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary:
In Button v. Jimison, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 205291 (W.D. Wash. Oct. 17, 2025), the defendant, in his response to pro se plaintiffs’ motion to strike his answer, identified that plaintiffs’ motion included hallucinated quotes and cases. In response the court warned plaintiffs that “they are responsible for verifying that all information and all citations submitted are accurate and real pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11,” recommended that plaintiff “should not rely on [AI] programs to conduct research or draft legal arguments, and ordered that all of plaintiffs’ future filings include a signed certification that states “I have reviewed each court decision cited in this filing. I certify that each decision cited is a valid decision and that any language quoted from a decision that is included in this filing is accurate.” The court further warned that if any of plaintiffs’ future pleadings do not include the certification, they will be stricken.

Since this warning, the plaintiffs filed four subsequent motions and pleadings, all of which included the required certification. However, two of the pleadings nonetheless contained hallucinated and inaccurate citations. As a result, on January 20, 2026, the court struck the two pleadings that contained inaccurate citations in accordance with the court’s prior warning, noted that two other courts have recently admonished the plaintiffs for similar conduct, and warned that further filing of fictious citations may lead to the imposition of sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Md. Judge Brendan Abell Hurson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Tafah v. Lake Vill. Townhomes, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 8657 (D. Md. Jan. 16, 2026), the court observed that the pro se plaintiffs cited to “inaccurate or non-existent” cases in the amended complaint. Judge Hurson cautioned that the plaintiffs “may face consequences if they continue to cite to non-existent cases in future litigation.” The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Mich. – Judge Anthony P. Patti

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Tiffany K. v. Comm'r of Soc. Sec., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9392 (E.D. Mich. Jan. 16, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel cited to a nonexistent case in her briefing “likely caused by use of [GenAI].” Judge Patti warned counsel and her law firm that “citations of this sort and unconfirmed use of AI have led to sanctions” and that “future instances of this will not be tolerated.” Judge Patti granted the plaintiff’s motion for summary judgment and remanded the case to the Commissioner of Social Security on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Mich. – Judge Judith E. Levy

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Lepp v. Mallett, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9446 (E.D. Mich. Jan. 16, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed an exhibit in support of her Motion for Relief from Judgment that contained an “objective and dispassionate review of the Court's [R&R]” performed by ChatGPT. The court declined to consider the Report in part because legal arguments are not appropriately filed in an exhibit. Furthermore, the court found the AI-generated legal report inaccurate and unreliable and that it contained an incorrect discussion of caselaw. Judge Levy warned that submissions with inaccurate citations could result in sanctions. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion for relief from judgement on other grounds.

court: Kansas - Court of Appeals of Kansas

linkToCourtOrder.text: Kan. App. – Judge Karen Arnold-Burger

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Riverchase, LLC v. Goldwyn, 2026 Kan. App. Unpub. LEXIS 17 (Kan. App. Jan. 16, 2026), the court observed that the pro se plaintiff’s brief “contains numerous citation errors, mischaracterizations, and even citations to cases that appear not to exist or have no relevance” to the matter at issue. Judge Arnold-Burger reminded the parties that pro se litigants must follow procedural rules and held that regardless of whether the plaintiff used GenAI, the plaintiff has “waived and abandoned” her arguments “due to wholly inadequate briefing.” The court warned that while it did not impose consequences, the briefing errors justified dismissing the claims and imposing sanctions. The court affirmed the default judgment on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Ill. – Judge Lindsay C. Jenkins

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hobbs v. Goncharko, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 9005 (N.D. Ill. Jan. 16, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited to a nonexistent case in his response brief. The plaintiff had cited to the same nonexistent case in a prior filing and the court had warned that sanctions could result. The court declined to impose sanctions for this repeated infraction but warned the plaintiff that another citation to a nonexistent case could result in dismissal of his case with prejudice. Judge Jenkins denied the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 11th Jud. Cir. Fla. – Chief Judge Ariana Fajardo Orshan

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: Judge Orshan’s standing order titled, “Disclosure of use of Generative Artificial Intelligence by Attorneys and Self-Represented Litigants” requires attorneys and self-represented litigants to disclose the use of GenAI in the “preparation” of various court filings, include a certification stating that GenAI was used, attest that all factual assertions, legal authority, and citations have been independently reviewed and verified for accuracy, and acknowledge that the filer accepts full responsibility for the contents of the filing. The order also prohibits the “submission of fictitious, fabricated, or hallucinated legal authority, statutes, quotations, or facts;” reliance on GenAI-generated citations “without independent verification;” “misrepresentation of GenAI-generated content as independently researched authority;” and “failure to disclose” GenAI use. Failure to comply with the order may result in sanctions including striking the filing, denial of relief, monetary sanctions, contempt proceedings, and referral to the Florida Bar.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Tex. – Magistrate Judge Christina A. Bryan

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Peters v. Banner Health, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 10367 (S.D. Tex. Jan. 15, 2026), the pro se plaintiff repeatedly submitted materials with false quotations and hallucinated case citations to the court, even after receiving “multiple admonishments.” The court concluded these erroneous filings “appear to be the product of [plaintiff’s] use of [GenAI]” use and noted that the plaintiff admitted to using ChatGPT in a prior filing. As a result of these hallucinations, the court did not prohibit GenAI use but reminded the plaintiff of her Rule 11 obligations and ordered that no document generated with GenAI may be filed unless she ensures that all citations are real and support the propositions asserted. The court also warned that violations could trigger sanctions up to and including dismissal. In so doing, the court noted the perils of submitting fake citations to the court, “including wasting the opposing party's time and money, the Court's time and resources, and reputational harms to the legal system.” After the plaintiff ignored these warnings and orders from the court, and continued to submit false quotes and citations, the court then required her to verify citations using the courthouse law library and attach a certification to all filings indicating she had done so. However, when the plaintiff also failed to include that required certification in various submissions, the court ultimately recommended dismissing her claims with prejudice.

linkToCourtOrder.text: C.D. Cal. – Judge Blumenfeld

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: Judge Blumenfeld has updated his standing orders for civil cases to require parties using GenAI to “generate any portion of” a court filing, to submit a separately filed document disclosing the use of GenAI and certifying the review and accuracy of any AI-generated content. This language appears to be specific to the use of GenAI in drafting court filings, and does not appear to apply to the use of GenAI for research or other supportive, non-drafting tasks.

judge: Senior Judge James A. Kenney III

linkToCourtOrder.text: Md. App. – Judge Terrence Zic

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Boyd v. Lee, 2026 Md. App. LEXIS 41 (Md. App. Jan. 14, 2026), the appellant’s counsel cited to “‘hallucinated’ and unsubstantiated case law” in his brief. The court identified that “[o]f the 14 cases cited in [his] brief, at least [six] contain a citation irregularity.” Counsel was ordered to show cause why he should not be sanctioned and referred to the Attorney Grievance Commission for violating the Maryland Attorney’s Rules of Professional Conduct. Although the attorney responded to the order to show cause, he neglected to respond to the court’s other prior orders to show cause (both requiring him to file paper copies of the brief). The court dismissed the appeal on other grounds and did not state whether it would ultimately refer the attorney to the Grievance Commission.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Cal. – Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hang Zhang v. Driscoll, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 8654 (N.D. Cal. Jan. 14, 2026), the pro se plaintiff included “multiple citations to fictitious cases” in her reply brief in support of her motion for preliminary injunction. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the plaintiff maintained that the citations were of real cases. When the court ordered the plaintiff to file copies of the cases, the plaintiff claimed the citations instead resulted from editing errors and attached two published cases. Judge Martínez-Olguín compared the cases and concluded that the “form and content of both are too far apart to plausibly result from a mere drafting error.” The court further concluded that citing to fictitious cases and the plaintiff’s subsequent dishonesty violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court sanctioned the plaintiff in the amount of $500 and warned that further violations would result in “case-terminating sanctions.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Tenn. – Judge Sheryl H. Lipman

results.summary:
In Young v. City of Memphis, 2:2025cv02799 (W.D. Tenn. Jan. 14, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel “unknowingly and unintentionally” recorded a status conference using Read AI, a Microsoft Teams feature. The defendants raised concerns that the plaintiff’s conduct was intentional and had occurred previously. Judge Lipman noted that the recording of a status conference violated Local Rule 83.2(a)(1), which prohibits the
“recording of transmission of Court Proceedings.” The court ordered that the plaintiff’s counsel file an affidavit detailing whether they have recorded any other judicial or non-judicial proceeding using AI without first informing opposing counsel.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Ariz. – Judge Krissa M. Lanham

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mavy v. Comm’r of Soc. Sec. Admin., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 157358 (D. Ariz. Aug. 14, 2025), Magistrate Judge Alison S. Bachus found that an opening brief that was “replete with citation-related deficiencies,” including non existent and otherwise deficient authorities, suggested possible use of GenAI. Magistrate Judge Bachus ordered counsel to show cause under Rule 11. Counsel’s response described a two layer internal review process, but acknowledged widespread citation problems. Magistrate Judge Bachus concluded that “well over the majority” of the brief’s citations were fabricated, misleading, or unsupported and that counsel failed to conduct a reasonable inquiry, thus warranting sanctions, including revoking the attorney’s pro hac vice status, striking the plaintiff’s opening brief, and ordering the attorney to notify her client and various courts about her errors—while expressly declining to find subjective bad faith. On review, Judge Lanham held (see 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 5981 (D. Ariz. Jan. 13, 2026)) that, although counsel acted recklessly in failing to ensure citation accuracy, sua sponte Rule 11 sanctions require conduct “akin to a contempt of court” (typically necessitating bad faith or the equivalent), which was not met here. Judge Lanham emphasized that warnings about GenAI use had been issued, that a two-layer review occurred, and that after problems surfaced counsel investigated other filings and took corrective steps—facts consistent with recklessness rather than bad faith—so she vacated the Rule 11 sanction in part. However, to protect the orderly administration of justice, the court left in place the striking of the opening brief and revocation of the filing attorney’s pro hac vice status, which she concluded were justified on non-Rule 11 grounds given the impaired administration of justice caused by a filing “riddled” with citation errors.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.N.J. – Judge Christine P. O’Hearn

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Doe v. United States, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 5356 (D.N.J. Jan. 12, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s filings contained content that “appear[ed]” to include GenAI-generated material with hallucinated or nonexistent legal authorities. The court therefore cautioned that any additional submissions must comply with Rule 11’s verification obligations, and reminded the parties that pro se parties remain subject to sanctions for failing to ensure that claims and legal contentions are warranted by existing law. On unrelated grounds, the court dismissed the complaint without prejudice, denied emergency injunctive relief, and allowed thirty days for the plaintiff to amend the filing.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Mich. – Magistrate Judge Elizabeth A. Stafford

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hardy v. Whitaker, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 44834 (E.D. Mich. Jan. 12, 2026), the court recommended dismissing the pro se plaintiff’s civil rights action with prejudice under Fed. R. Civ. P. 41(b) and enjoining him from filing new actions without leave of court. The plaintiff, who had been filing lawsuits in Michigan’s federal district courts for over 20 years, flooded the docket with frivolous motions and unauthorized documents throughout the litigation, repeatedly violating court rules and prior orders. The court noted that the plaintiff’s motion for summary judgment included fake case citations, which the court suspected resulted from the plaintiff's use of GenAI. Magistrate Judge Stafford observed that the use of GenAI when drafting legal filings “is a very risky practice,” as litigants remain subject to Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b)(2), and that “even in the absence of bad faith, Rule 11 sanctions for use of AI generated phantom cases may be warranted.” The court further noted that pro se plaintiffs are “increasingly likely to clog dockets with frivolous and improper documents.” Applying the factors for dismissal under Rule 41(b), the court found that the plaintiff had engaged in bad faith conduct by stubbornly resisting court orders, prejudicing defendants, and ignoring repeated warnings, noting that lesser sanctions would be ineffective given his in forma pauperis status. The court recommended that the plaintiff be enjoined from filing any new federal actions in the Eastern District of Michigan or against any state or federal judge, officer, or employee without first obtaining leave of court.

linkToCourtOrder.text: M.D. Fla. – Judge Tom Barber

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hayes v. Chipotle Mexican Grill, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 3792 (M.D. Fla. Jan. 9, 2026), the court found that the pro se plaintiff’s opposition to the defendant’s motion to dismiss cited nonexistent authorities, which the court observed likely resulted from the use of GenAI. Judge Barger cautioned that misuse of AI may warrant sanctions under both the court’s inherent authority and Rule 11 (which applies equally to unrepresented litigants). The court warned that continued misrepresentation of the record or reliance on nonexistent authority will result in sanctions, emphasizing that even a pro se litigant must conform to both the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and the court’s local rules. On unrelated grounds, the court dismissed the complaint without prejudice and granted leave to amend.

court: New York - App. Div., Third Dep't
judge: Justice of the Peace Sharon A.M. Aarons

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.Y. App. Div., Third Dep’t – Justice Fisher

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Deutsche Bank Natl. Trust Co. v. LeTennier, 2026 N.Y. App. Div. LEXIS 46, (3d Dep’t Jan. 8, 2026), the court confronted an appeal in which the defendant’s appellate court filings contained numerous fabricated legal authorities, which counsel “reluctantly” admitted was the result of the use of GenAI. Although acknowledging that GenAI can benefit the profession, the court stressed that GenAI use does not diminish an attorney’s obligations of candor and accuracy. Applying New York’s law on frivolous conduct (22 NYCRR § 130-1.1), the court found the submission of fabricated authorities to be “completely without merit” and therefore sanctionable, especially given that the misconduct continued “after [the attorney] was on notice of such issue.” Noting that it was the first New York appellate decision imposing sanctions for misuse of GenAI, the court levied $5,000 against defense counsel to deter future violations, and imposed an additional $2,500 penalty on counsel and $2,500 on defendant due to the frivolous nature of the appeal. The court affirmed the challenged orders while noting a history of defendant’s vexatious litigation tactics.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D.N.Y. – Judge Dale E. Ho

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: On January 8, 2026, Judge Ho issued an update to his Individual Practices in Civil Pro Se Cases, which apply to all civil cases involving pro se litigants. The new rule on the “Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence” requires filers to disclose GenAI use in court filings and certify that they have reviewed and verified any GenAI-created content.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hollinger v. Enlisted Ass’n of the Nat’l Guard of the United States, No. 1:25-cv-01349 (ECF 112) (E.D. Va. Jan. 7, 2026), the pro se plaintiff admitted using GenAI to generate legal authorities that do not exist, ultimately requiring the court to address fabricated and inaccurate citations that wasted judicial and party resources. Despite a prior order to show cause and a sworn declaration from the plaintiff promising to use only verifiable citations going forward, the plaintiff continued to file materials containing inaccurate quotations and page cites, and again relied on authorities that did not support the asserted propositions. The court also noted additional filings in a companion matter where the plaintiff also persisted with fabricated citations and doubled down in subsequent briefing, even after being put on notice about his GenAI misuse. Based on this ongoing misrepresentation of legal authorities and broader noncompliance, the court dismissed the action with prejudice, but declined to award fees and costs because no proper itemized fee application accompanied the defendant’s motion for fees and costs.

judge: Magistrate Judge Maximiliano D. Couvillier III

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Nev. – Magistrate Judge Maximiliano D. Couvillier III

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In his First Amended Standing Order & Chambers Practices (D. Nev. Jan. 5, 2026), Magistrate Judge Couvillier includes a section on Artificial Intelligence. The order provides that “[a]ny counsel or pro se party may use AI to draft documents or process discovery consistent with Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (Fed. R. Civ. P.), this Standing Order, and any other applicable legal or ethical obligations.” All filings must contain a certification indicating “whether or not AI was used to prepare or assist in preparing such filing.” Compliance with the order also requires parties to verify accuracy of all cases and assertions. Parties may not upload documents “filed under seal” or that “contain personal-data identifiers” onto any “open AI system.” The order also encourages parties to include protocols addressing AI in protective orders. Violation of the order may result in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Mich. – Judge F. Kay Behm

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Jarrus v. Governor of Michigan, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 852 (E.D. Mich. Jan. 5, 2026) the court denied both the pro se plaintiffs’ motions for reconsideration of sanctions. The court had previously sanctioned each plaintiff $300 for submitting to the court filings containing misrepresented cases after being warned about GenAI use (See Jarrus v. Governor of Michigan, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234830 (E.D. Mich Dec. 2, 2025)). One plaintiff asked for reconsideration because he “uses Chat GPT [sic] as ‘assistive’ technology and cannot afford his $300 sanction,” and requested a reduced sanction or payment plan. The other asked for reconsideration because she purportedly “did not draft the filings at issue, did not use ChatGPT, did not commit any violation, [and] has extremely limited income.” To the former, Judge Behm noted that the court considered the plaintiff’s financial circumstances when imposing sanctions, and the plaintiff did not previously indicate that he would not be able to pay a fine. To the latter, Judge Behm reiterated that the fact that the plaintiff did not draft the specific filings does not absolve her of her duty under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. Furthermore, the court expressed concern that reducing or eliminating the monetary sanction for the plaintiff will cause her to “misunderstand the seriousness of her conduct.” Judge Behm noted that the plaintiffs immediately followed the Magistrate Judge’s warning “with the very conduct they were warned about,” increasing the court’s doubt that a nominal penalty would deter the wrongful conduct.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Fla. – Magistrate Judge Panayotta Augustin-Birch

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hylton v. Hylton, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 11758 (S.D. Fla. Jan. 5, 2026), several of the pro se plaintiff’s filings contained inaccurate citations and purported case quotations. The court reminded the plaintiff that Rule 11 creates an obligation to ensure that filings accurately represent existing law, and that relying on GenAI to create cases to support a proposition without verifying their accuracy may lead to sanctions.  On unrelated grounds, the court granted a defendant’s motion to dismiss, while denying the other defendants’ motions as moot.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Idaho – Judge B. Lynn Winmill

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Allen v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 1645 (D. Idaho Jan. 4, 2026), the court found that the pro se plaintiff’s motion to dismiss counterclaims, strike affirmative defenses, and stay or bifurcate contained “fabricated quotations [and] misrepresented authority.” The court expressly cautioned that “mistaken reliance on [GenAI] for legal research[] is not a defense to Rule 11 sanctions” and warned that future fabrications or mischaracterizations may trigger a show-cause order under Rule 11. On unrelated grounds, the court denied the motion to dismiss, denied the motion to strike defenses, and declined to stay or bifurcate.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.S.C. – Judge Jacquelyn D. Austin

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Sevilla v. Ross, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 268240 (D.S.C. Dec. 31, 2025), the court observed that the pro se plaintiff’s filings have “many hallmarks of motions created using artificial intelligence or found on the internet” and warned the plaintiffs that they could be subject to sanctions for further “frivolous or inappropriate” filings. The court denied the plaintiff’s numerous pending motions without prejudice as moot.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Nev. – Judge Elayna J. Youchah

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Silverberg v. Bonomo, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 268189 (D. Nev. Dec. 30, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s briefings cited cases for propositions that had nothing to do with the actual opinions and included a nonexistent quotation. The court warned that “misrepresented citations, even if not intentional, will not be tolerated by the Court” and cautioned that future errors, if the result of misuse of GenAI, will result in sanctions. The court granted the defendants’ motion to quash service on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Nev. – Judge Gloria Maria Navarro

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In M.T. Real Est. Inv. Inc. v. Servis One, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 268188 (D. Nev. Dec. 30, 2025), the plaintiff’s responses to the defendant’s motion to dismiss and motion to strike the first amended complaint contained “five cited authorities which [the court] could not locate.” In response to Judge Navarro’s order to show cause, plaintiff’s counsel admitted that the cases “were not in fact real” and contended that the errors were the result of his paralegal’s GenAI use. Although Judge Navarro found that counsel’s conduct did not violate Local Rule IA 11-8(d) (which only prohibits false statements made “knowingly,” and counsel maintained that “he did not know that the hallucinated authorities were fake), the court found that the plaintiff’s counsel did violate Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b)(2) for failing to verify the accuracy of citations submitted to the court. After considering  “sanctions imposed by other courts for similar conduct, the need to deter the future use of fake authorities, and [plaintiff’s counsel’s] conduct in this case,” the court struck the plaintiff’s responses (which it noted had the result of making the defendants’ motion to strike “unopposed, which constitutes [plaintiff’s] consent to the granting of the Motion”); awarded the defendants attorney’s fees and costs for work related to the false citations; ordered the plaintiff’s counsel to serve a copy of the order on the plaintiff; and directed the clerk to serve a copy of the order on the Nevada State Bar Association. The court noted that it would have also granted the defendant’s motion to strike on the merits, and then granted one of the defendants’ motions to dismiss the complaint with prejudice.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Mich. – Judge Anthony P. Patti

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Jones v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 267568 (E.D. Mich. Dec. 30, 2025), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff may have been “led astray” by GenAI while preparing her brief in opposition to defendant’s motion to stay discovery because she included a citation to a case that did not stand for the proposition for which it was cited. Judge Patti reminded the plaintiff that “AI cannot give legal advice” and warned that “even in the absence of bad faith, Rule 11 sanctions for use of AI generated phantom cases may be warranted.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.S.C. – Judge Sherri A. Lydon

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Cherleatha B. v. Bisignano, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 266530 (D.S.C. Dec. 29, 2025), the court noted that the pro se plaintiff’s filings “bear characteristics the court has increasingly observed in briefs generated by artificial intelligence (‘AI’) programs.” Taking into consideration the plaintiff’s pro se status and the relative “novelty” of the technology, the court chose not to impose sanctions, but warned the plaintiff that “she could be subject to sanctions . . . should she cite fake, AI-generated legal authority to the court again.” Judge Lydon adopted Magistrate Judge Brown’s Report and Recommendation and dismissed the case without prejudice on other grounds.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D.N.Y. – Magistrate Judge Gary Stein

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Brian NG v. Amguard Ins. Co., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 266712 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 29, 2025), the pro se plaintiff cited to a case that “does not appear to exist” to support his “argument as to liability for insurance agents for their individual torts.” Magistrate Judge Stein warned the plaintiff that “[s]ubmitting fake citations to a court is unacceptable” and that future submissions with nonexistent citations may result in sanctions. The court also reminded all parties of Judge Broderick’s recent update to the Individual Rules and Practices in civil cases, which clarified that all parties, regardless of pro se status, must disclose the use of GenAI tools “in the preparation of any documents filed with the Court” and verify the documents’ accuracy, “including all citations.”  Judge Stein granted the defendants’ motion to vacate the clerk’s certificate of default on other grounds.

court: North Carolina - Federal District Court (Western District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D.N.C. – Judge Kenneth D. Bell

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Kennedy v. Navy Fed. Credit Union, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 266163 (W.D.N.C. Dec. 29, 2025), the court noted that the plaintiff’s response to the motion to dismiss “clearly violates” the court’s Standing Order on AI (3:24-mc-104), identifying “numerous citations to cases that do not exist or do not arguably stand for the propositions” asserted, and finding that Plaintiff’s “improper use of AI unjustly burdens the Court and the Defendant.” While concluding it could and “certainly should” strike the response for the violation, the court deemed striking unnecessary in light of its dismissal of the complaint and denied remand.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Neb. – Judge Robert Francis Rossiter Jr.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In LeBer v. Bryan Med. Ctr., No. 8:25CV401, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 264750 (D. Neb. Dec. 23, 2025), the pro se plaintiff “repeatedly cite[d] to a case which appears to be a hallucination of [GenAI], despite [the plaintiff's] certification” that he verified the accuracy of the citations and the court’s previous warning to him about GenAI use. The court again warned the plaintiff to verify the accuracy of the citations and legal authority in his submissions to the court, and that further violations may result in sanctions. The court denied the plaintiff's motion for reconsideration on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Tex. – Judge Sidney A. Fitzwater

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Allen v. Amazon, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 264873 (N.D. Tex. Dec. 23, 2025), the pro se plaintiff included citations to non-existent authority and hallucinated quotes in his motion to compel. As the plaintiff failed to disclose the use of GenAI in his brief, Judge Fitzwater found him to have violated Local Rule 7.2(f)(1) but declined to impose sanctions given his pro se status and first-time offense. However, the court warned that should the plaintiff continue to violate Local Rule 7.2(f)(1), he could face various sanctions, including stricken filings, filing restrictions, fines, and dismissal of the action.

court: Vermont - Vt. Super. Ct., Chittenden Unit

linkToCourtOrder.text: Vt. Super. – Judge Megan J. Shafritz

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Fitzgerald v. Brady, 2025 Vt. Super. LEXIS 792 (Vt. Super. Dec. 23, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition to defendant’s motion to dismiss contained citations to “non-existent authorities and fake quotations,” which court observed may have been created by GenAI. The court warned that any future use of fake or nonexistent legal authorities, whether generated by GenAI or otherwise, violates V.R.C.P. 11 and may result in sanctions. The court also reminded the parties that by presenting a filing, a party certifies that legal contentions are warranted by existing law, noted that fabricated opinions are not “existing law,” and emphasized that self-represented litigants remain bound by Rule 11’s obligations and potential sanctions. On unrelated grounds, the court granted defendant’s motion to dismiss in part and denied it in part.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ind. Tax Ct. – Judge Justin L. McAdam

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Donovan v. Clark Cnty. Assessor, 2025 Ind. Tax LEXIS 51 (Ind. Tax Ct. Dec. 22, 2025), the pro se petitioners cited to two non-existent cases in their opening and reply briefs and, during the hearing on the merits, admitted to using an AI tool to assist with drafting. Though the court did not impose any sanctions, the petitioners were “admonishe[d] . . . for failing to confirm the accuracy of their legal presentations” and reminded of their duty to ensure that courts can rely on the accuracy of parties’ assertions.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Northern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D.N.Y. – Judge Mae A. D’Agostino

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Smith v. Smith, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 263592 (N.D.N.Y. Dec. 22, 2025), the court found that the plaintiff’s objections and reconsideration briefing bore a “distinctive style and syntax” characteristic of AI and relied on fictitious or mis-cited authorities, attributing the errors to GenAI “hallucinations.” The court adopted the magistrate judge’s report and recommendation, which recommended that (1) the plaintiff's complaint be dismissed with prejudice as to the plaintiff's constitutional claims against certain defendants; (2) the complaint be dismissed without prejudice and with leave to amend as to the plaintiff's constitutional claims against other defendants; and (3) the court decline to exercise its supplemental jurisdiction over the plaintiff's causes of action arising under state law. The court also denied the plaintiff’s motion for reconsideration and warned that future submissions citing fabricated or AI-generated case law may result in sanctions, including striking filings, filing restrictions, monetary penalties, or dismissal.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Md. – Judge Julie R. Rubin

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Charles G. Menk, III v. The Mitre Corp., No. 1:23-cv-00053-JRR (D. Md. Dec. 19, 2025), the court addressed a hallucinated citation included in the plaintiffs’ opposition to a motion to sever. The plaintiffs’ counsel explained that he used ChatGPT for preliminary research, which supplied the nonexistent citation; he provided those materials to co-counsel who drafted the filing, reviewed the response without cite-checking, signed it, accepted responsibility, apologized, and stated he would refrain from using ChatGPT for future legal issues. Citing Rule 11’s reasonable-inquiry requirement and Fourth Circuit guidance on sua sponte sanctions, the court declined to issue a Rule 11 show cause order in light of counsel’s transparency, contrition, and credibility, noting that the public docket entry sufficed as deterrence. The court warned, however, that any future citation to hallucinated authorities or similar conduct will prompt an order to show cause and potential Rule 11 sanctions and/or attorney-misconduct referral, and emphasized that if AI is used, “thorough verification of cited authorities is essential” to avoid rule violations and unnecessary consumption of court resources.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.N.J. – Judge Christine Patricia O'Hearn

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Fantini v. Westrock Co., No. 22-cv-04351, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 262957 (D.N.J. Dec. 19, 2025), the pro se plaintiff cited to “what appear[ed] to be non-existent case law” in his cross-motion for summary judgment. The court warned the plaintiff that pro se parties, like all parties, are bound by Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and can be sanctioned for “failing to conduct a ‘reasonable inquiry’ into whether ‘the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions are warranted by existing law.’” The court denied the plaintiff’s motion on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Miss. – Judge Sharion Aycock

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Disability Rts. Miss. v. Palmer Home for Children, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 263114 (N.D. Miss. Dec. 19, 2025), the plaintiff included various hallucinated citations in three separate motions. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the filing attorney stated that she “relied on an internal reference document developed for efficiency in drafting” and that the “citation errors” were due to “old-fashioned copy and paste” and “paraphrased summary lines for internal use only” that were not removed. The attorney also denied using any GenAI tools but admitted to failing to review certain cases pre-filing. Judge Aycock noted that this is the first instance that a Mississippi federal court considered an attorney’s misconduct with respect to citation mistakes (as opposed to those from a pro se litigant) and was “highly suspicious” that AI was used to generate the citations and did not believe the attorney’s explanations (because, for example, the purported the “summaries” the attorney drafted “misrepresent[ed], either entirely or in part, the facts and rulings of the cases cited”). However, because the attorney did not confirm her use of AI, the court did not conclude that GenAI was the cause of the errors, but noted that it “clearly demonstrate[d] an extreme deviation from [the attorney’s] professional and legal responsibilities.” The court found the attorney to have violated Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and acted in bad faith by “attempt[ing] to perpetrate fraud on the Court by citing fabricated legal authority in her filings and subsequently making certain misrepresentations.” As the review following the hallucinations was “a complete waste of valuable judicial resources,” Judge Aycock used her inherent powers to impose the following as sanctions: (i) ordering the Clerk of Court to send a copy of this decision to all district and magistrate judges in the district and the State Bar of Mississippi; (ii) ordering the Clerk of Court to file a copy of this decision in the other Northern District of Mississippi case where the plaintiff’s counsel is counsel of record; (iii) requiring plaintiff’s counsel to attend a 3-hour CLE class on AI hallucinations (separate from any CLE obligation under the Mississippi Bar) and submit a copy of her attendance; and (iv) requiring plaintiff’s counsel to pay the defendants $20,883.10 in attorney’s fees and costs within 30 days. The court specifically noted that the plaintiff, a “federally funded nonprofit agency,” was not responsible for the attorney’s misconduct, and further noted that the agency had retained new counsel and “accepted [the offending attorney’s] resignation.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Va. – Judge Jasmine H. Yoon

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Simpson v. Portfolio Recovery Assocs., LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 262777 (W.D. Va. Dec. 19, 2025), the pro se plaintiff included various misrepresented and hallucinated citations in his motion for summary judgment. Although the court was sympathetic to the plaintiff’s “hurdles,” Judge Yoon noted that including false citations wastes resources and could constitute a violation of Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. Judge Yoon warned the plaintiff that continuing to include hallucinated or inaccurate citations in future filings could result in an order to show cause as to why sanctions under Rule 11 would not be warranted.

court: California - Cal. Ct. App., Third Dist.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ca. App. 3rd – Judge Laurie M. Earl, Judge Ronald B. Robie, Judge Shama H. Mesiwala

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Jpmorgan Chase Bank, N.A. v. Buenzli, No. C102530, 2025 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 8160 (Cal. App. 3rd, Dec. 18, 2025), the pro se appellant’s brief contained citations to legal authority that were “at best incorrect, at worse fabricated.” The court ruled that regardless of whether the errors were the result of GenAI, it need not address arguments unsupported by legitimate legal authority. It declined to impose sanctions and affirmed the judgment below.

court: District of Columbia - Federal District Court

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.D.C. – Judge Beryl A. Howell

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Zhang v. National Public Radio, Inc., No. 25-699 (BAH) (D.D.C. Dec. 18, 2025), the court sanctioned plaintiff’s counsel after striking an opposition brief that contained “two completely fictitious and eight seriously mischaracterized citations.” The court recounted counsel’s explanation that he relied on “summaries” from web searches that he was “not even aware” were “being generated by artificial intelligence,” and “failed to read at least 10 cases” cited in the brief. Concluding that the submission “unreasonably” multiplied proceedings and was “singularly egregious,” the court imposed sanctions under 28 U.S.C. § 1927, directing counsel to reimburse the defendant for reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs associated with the stricken filing and the response to the court’s Order to Show Cause. The court also granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss the complaint in full.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Wis. – Chief Judge Pamela Pepper

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Gerou v. George, No. 25-cv-1160-pp, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 261544 (E.D. Wis. Dec. 18, 2025), in an order addressing various motions, the court indicated its suspicion that the pro se petitioners cited GenAI hallucinated cases in their petition demanding proof of origination of funds. The petition contained “multiple direct quotes that do not appear in the cited cases” and citations to “multiple cases that the court could not locate in any legal database.” The court notes that neither “[c]arelessness, good faith, or ignorance,” nor petitioners’ “self-represented status,” excuse them from their obligation under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 to make a “reasonable inquiry into the supporting law.” Judge Pepper warned petitioners that they may be subject to monetary sanctions if they “file any more documents in any case in this district that contain citations to nonexistent cases or directly quote language that does not appear in a cited case.” The court dismissed the case on other grounds.

court: Vermont - Vt. Super. Ct., Lamoille Unit

linkToCourtOrder.text: Vt. Super. – Judge Benjamin D. Battles

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Lafayette v. Abrami, 2025 Vt. Super. LEXIS 774 (Vt. Super. Dec. 18, 2025), the court imposed sanctions on the pro se plaintiff for his “repeated use of fake case citations and quotations” in multiple filings, including the initial complaint and preliminary injunction motion. The court further noted that the plaintiff had been warned by at least one other court regarding his use of fake citations. The plaintiff continued to file documents including fabricated cases and quotations after Judge Battles warned that he was considering sanctions. The court found that the plaintiff’s failure to confirm the validity of the cases on which he relied violated V.C.R.P. 11(b)(2). Considering the plaintiff’s history of “serial filings” which violate procedural rules, the plaintiff’s demonstrated bad faith, the burden imposed on the defendant, and the fact that no other sanction would be adequate, the court enjoined the plaintiff from filing any document in the Lamoille Civil Division pro se, unless the plaintiff first obtains permission from the judge.

court: California - Cal. Ct. App., Second Dist.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Cal. Ct. App., 2d Dist., Div. 4 – Judge Tamzarian, with Acting P.J. Collins and J. Mori concurring

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Outreach v. Medoff, 2025 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 8133 (Cal. Ct. App. Dec. 17, 2025), the court struck appellant’s reply brief and sanctioned counsel after finding that “the majority of the case citations in the brief were incorrect in some significant way,” emphasizing that attorneys must read cited authorities to ensure they support the stated propositions and noting this duty applies to all court filings, not just appellate briefs. Counsel admitted the reply brief was filed “without [her] review” and that citations “are not correctly used or summarized”; the court rejected her new, inconsistent claim at oral argument that a paralegal filed the wrong version. Citing prior case precedent, the court underscored that filing briefs replete with fabricated or misused authorities—often associated with GenAI misuse—reflects a “‘fundamental abdicat[ion]’” of counsel’s responsibilities. The court imposed $5,070 in monetary sanctions payable to opposing counsel, ordered the opinion forwarded to the State Bar under Bus. & Prof. Code § 6086.7(a)(3), and required counsel to provide the opinion to her client and certify compliance; the underlying judgment was affirmed.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Ariz. – Judge Stephen M. McNamee

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Dodge v. FirstService Residential Ariz. LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 270557 (D. Ariz. Dec. 17, 2025), the plaintiff’s response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss contained “two misrepresented case holdings, five misquoted cases, and one case citation that does not exist.” During the hearing on the court’s order to show cause, plaintiff’s counsel admitted to using Federally Lawyer, an “add-on” to ChatGPT Enterprise that he uses “as a research and drafting tool to assist with his case load.” Given the delay in filing, plaintiff’s counsel stated that he did not independently verify the citations in the response brief and instead asked ChatGPT to perform a cite check using a prompt asking it to confirm that “all legal authority and citation[s] [are] up to date and accurate, meaning is it still good precedent, and are they all real cases?” Judge McNamee ultimately did not impose additional sanctions on the plaintiff’s attorney, the attorney self-reported his conduct to the State Bar of Arizona, and is manually reviewing filings in his other cases to report other errors. The court acknowledged the attorney’s caseload as a solo practitioner and found that the errors ultimately did not prejudice the plaintiff due to merits deficiencies. As such, the “public humiliation in this matter [was] a sufficient remedy.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Cal. – Magistrate Judge Dennis M. Cota

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Lowe v. Vilsack, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 261126 (E.D. Cal. Dec. 17, 2025), the court denied Plaintiff’s Rule 30(d)(2) motion for sanctions arising from defense counsel’s conduct during the deposition of the defendant’s National Pandemic Coordinator. In so doing, the court chose to consider the plaintiff’s deposition record that was allegedly created using an AI tool called “Readback Active Reporting” over the defendant’s objection that the process violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 28 and 30. A representative from Readback joined the deposition for its entirety. The defendant described the Readback AI tool as “court reporting reimagined—a remote reporting service powered by patented AI technology and human editors that delivers transcripts at breakneck speeds.” In response, the plaintiff provided evidence from Readback that the company does not use AI “to generate the transcript.” The court concluded that the deposition transcript bore sufficient “indicia of reliability” for the court to consider it for purposes of the sanctions motion, despite any allegation of GenAI use. The court admonished both sides and threatened sanctions for reasons unrelated to GenAI use.

court: Florida - Fla. Dist. Ct. App., Fourth Dist.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Fla. App. 4th DCA – Judge Jeffrey Kuntz, Judge Cory Ciklin, Judge Shannon Shaw

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Friend v. Serpa, No. 4D2024-2959, 2025 Fla. App. LEXIS 9580 (Fla. App. 4th DCA, Dec. 17, 2025), in a per curiam affirmance on appeal, a Florida appellate court addressed a pro se appellee’s citation to a “phantom authority” that the court said must be “expressly disregarded by this court.” The three-judge panel reminded appellee that the “court has the authority to sanction” him for using GenAI in a submission without ensuring its accuracy, in violation of Florida procedural rules. The court declined to impose sanctions and affirmed on the merits without further discussion.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Ill.  – Judge Iain D. Johnston

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Halpern v. FRB of N.Y., No. 3:25-cv-50381, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 261702 (N.D. Ill. Dec. 17, 2025), the pro se plaintiff included a nonexistent quotation and cited a case for a proposition for which it did not stand in his motion response, supplemental memorandum, and motion for clarification. Regardless of “[w]hether this quotation was hallucinated by [GenAI]—which seems likely to be the case—or whether it ‘reflects a widely circulated secondary description” of the underlying case, as the plaintiff claimed, the court warned that the improper use and attribution of the case subjected the plaintiff to the possibility of sanctions. Taking into account the posture of the case and the plaintiff's pro se status, Judge Johnston did not impose sanctions, but “admonish[ed the plaintiff] . . .  that quotations attributed to cases should only come directly from those cases.” The court denied the plaintiff's motion to remand and dismissed the complaint on other grounds without prejudice to the plaintiff filing an amended complaint.

court: North Carolina - Federal Bankruptcy Court (Middle District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: Bankr. M.D.N.C. –  Judge Benjamin A. Kahn

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In in re Bryant, 2025 Bankr. LEXIS 3390 (Bankr. M.D.N.C. Dec. 16, 2025), the pro se debtors filed “multiple documents that contain misstatements of law, frivolous arguments, fictitious case cites, and fictitious case holdings,” leading the court to issue a show cause hearing as to why they should not be sanctioned. At the hearing, the court declined to issue sanctions, instead reminding the debtors of their obligations to the court under Federal Rule of Bankruptcy Procedure 9011, which requires unrepresented parties to not make inaccurate or improper filings to the court. When the debtors continued to make arguments that were “frivolous” and unsupported by existing law, including arguments that had previously been rejected by the court, Judge Kahn reminded the debtors that, “fictitious cases and cites are not the only way litigants present frivolous arguments.” Judge Kahn cautioned that “further violations of Rule 9011 will result in sanctions that will include striking pleadings, denying relief requested, and monetary sanctions.” On unrelated grounds, the court denied the debtors’ motion against defendants for sanctions for violating automatic stay and discharge injunction.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Fernandez v. Cnty. of Los Angeles, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 272126 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 16, 2025), the pro se plaintiff submitted a non-existent case citation in a briefing to the court. Upon investigation, both counsel for defendant and the court discovered that the cited case did not exist. The court then admonished plaintiff to read and review the authorities cited in her filings while reminding her that Rule 11 applies equally to unrepresented parties. The court also cited to a case that emphasized that GenAI “hallucinations” can violate Rule 11 and lead to sanctions. On unrelated grounds, the court granted summary judgment for the defendant.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Taylor v. Prince George’s Cnty., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 259300 (D. Md. Dec. 16, 2025), while recommending denial of the defendants’ motion for attorney’s fees, the court identified multiple citation errors in the plaintiffs’ response brief and expressly noted they are the “types of errors that [GenAI] tools make when hallucinating case citations.” Although the court stated it was “unclear” whether counsel definitively improperly relied on GenAI and made no recommendation regarding the errors, the magistrate judge flagged the issue for the district court, referencing decisions where courts sanctioned parties and attorneys for reliance on hallucinated, AI-generated case citations.

judge: Magistrate in Chancery Christian Douglas Wright

linkToCourtOrder.text: Del. Ch. – Magistrate in Chancery Christian Douglas Wright

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Dorsey v. Jones, C.A. No. 2024-0134-CDW (Del. Ch. Dec. 16, 2025), the court ordered that any future filings by defendant and counterclaim plaintiff prepared using GenAI must include a sworn certification confirming GenAI was used, identifying the specific GenAI tool, pinpointing the pages/paragraphs/sections generated by GenAI, and affirming human review for accuracy and completeness, including verification that all legal citations are accurate and support the cited propositions. The order followed the filing of two briefs containing false and mis-cited legal authority and the court’s suspicion that GenAI “hallucination” occurred; it defined GenAI for purposes of the order and warned that failure to comply may result in sanctions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Or. Tax Ct. – Magistrate Allison R. Boomer

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Holmes Family Tr. v. Multnomah Cty. Assessor, No. TC-MD 250247N, 2025 Or. Tax LEXIS 88 (Or. T.C. Dec. 16, 2025), the court suspected that the plaintiff’s citation to a nonexistent case in its response was the result of GenAI after the defendant directed the court’s attention to a case that “does not exist” and sought sanctions. Magistrate Judge Boomer noted that while the court had the authority to impose sanctions, it declined to do so because it “ha[d] not previously given any direction to Plaintiff concerning the use of AI.” The court instead admonished the plaintiff to verify cases before submission to the court, and that further citations to nonexistent case law may result in sanctions. The court granted in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss the plaintiff’s amended complaint on other grounds.
