applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In in re Zimmer M/L Taper HIP Prosthesis or. M/L Taper HIP Prosthesis with Kinectiv Tech., No. 18-MD-2859 (JMF), 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 266051 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 16, 2025), the court imposed sanctions on plaintiff’s counsel for filing “papers with AI-generated hallucinations and inaccuracies,” where the plaintiff’s oppositions to defendants’ motion to exclude opinions of a witness and defendants’ motion for summary judgment contained false citations and misrepresentations of case law. The court ordered the parties to confer to agree on the size of the award, which should reflect a reimbursement of the fees and costs the defendants incurred from the problems with the plaintiff’s filings, and to submit a letter to the court if such an agreement could not be reached.

court: California - Cal. Ct. App., Fourth Dist.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Cal. App. 4th – Judge Joan Irion, Judge Jose Luis Castillo Jr., Judge Julie Rubin

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Howell Mgmt. Servs., LLC v. Vashisht-Rota, No. D086055, 2025 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 8006 (Cal. App. 4th Dec. 15, 2025), the court sanctioned a pro se appellant for “prosecuting a frivolous appeal and unreasonably violating the California Rules of Court by citing nonexistent legal authorities.” Judge Irion rejected the plaintiff’s argument that she should not be sanctioned because her GenAI use only resulted in inadvertent, rather than intentional, errors. The court noted that her responsibilities include ensuring the correctness of every citation submitted to the court. The court ordered that the appellant pay $59,235 in attorney fees to the respondent and $15,000 to the clerk of court for her violations and dismissed the appeals on grounds unrelated to the misuse of GenAI.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Conn. – Judge Vernon D. Oliver

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Braica v. Frankowski, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 263098 (D. Conn. Dec. 15, 2025), the pro se plaintiff included various unrelated and hallucinated citations in his brief in response to the defendant’s partial motion to dismiss, which the court found to be “highly indicative of unvetted AI use.” The plaintiff attempted to rectify these errors by filing a sur-reply without seeking the court’s leave but maintained that some of his unrelated cases were applicable and continued to cite to hallucinated and irrelevant cases. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the plaintiff admitted to using “publicly available websites” but did not admit to using any GenAI tool. Judge Oliver found the plaintiff’s failure to verify and correct his citations and the fact that he “minimized the role of AI in his filings” to be violative of Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. Given the “interests of justice and deterrence,” the court struck both the response and sur-reply without leave to amend and warned the plaintiff that “more stringent Rule 11 sanctions” could be imposed if he submits another filing with GenAI hallucinations.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Kan. – Judge Julie A. Robinson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Lexos Media IP, LLC v. Overstock.com, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258649 (D. Kan. Dec. 15, 2025), the court granted in part and denied in part the plaintiff’s motion for leave to file a corrected opposition to a Daubert motion after the defendant identified “nonexistent quotations,” “nonexistent or incorrect citations,” and “misrepresentations about cited authority” in the plaintiff’s original brief—errors the plaintiff attributed to counsel’s unverified use of GenAI. The court held that allowing substantive changes would function as an improper sur-reply and that Plaintiff failed to show excusable neglect under Fed. R. Civ. P. 6(b), emphasizing the prejudice to the defendant and the waste of judicial resources caused by the defective filing. Citing recent authority warning that careless use of GenAI can waste resources and undermine credibility, the court declined to permit a “do-over,” denied any additions of text or authority, and struck the original response. The court ordered Plaintiff to file by December 19, 2025, a corrected brief that omits all nonexistent quotations, nonexistent/incorrect/inapposite citations, and misrepresentations about cited authority, noting that sanctions would be addressed separately.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Nev. – Judge Jennifer A. Dorsey

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Harvey v. Torrent Leasing, Inc., No. 2:25-cv-00824-JAD-DJA, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 260942 (D. Nev. Dec. 15, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s brief included “several cases” that were “incorrectly cited or [] misquoted.” The court noted that while it does not prohibit litigants from using GenAI, use of “hallucinated or inaccurate legal citations . . . to support arguments” may violate Fed. R. Civ. P. 11’s requirement that parties’ arguments are “warranted by existing law.” Judge Dorsey warned that additional reliance on “inaccurate or fake authority” may result in sanctions. The court granted the defendant's motion to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.N.J. – Judge Leo. M. Gordon

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Research
Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Gardner v. Combs, No. 2:24-cv-07729, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258523 (D.N.J. Dec. 15, 2025), the plaintiff’s counsel cited to a nonexistent case and “fabricated legal propositions derived from [GenAI] in support of Plaintiff's opposition to Defendants’ motions to dismiss.” Counsel failed to provide an explanation for the false citation after oral argument despite numerous inquiries by defendant’s counsel, leading the court to issue an order to show cause. In response to the order to show cause, counsel admitted that the error was the result of GenAI and “took ‘full responsibility’” for the mistake. The attorney had previously been sanctioned for GenAI use in another jurisdiction, and Judge Gordon noted that despite him taking preventative “corrective actions,” counsel “nevertheless again failed to act with diligence and address issues arising from his reliance on AI research.” The court concluded that his “negligent reliance on unverified (AI-hallucinated) case law and subsequent failure to communicate with respect to the court's follow-up inquiry” violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court imposed a $6,000 sanction on the attorney, describing the penalty as “on the higher end of the range due to his repeated behavior. The court also ordered that counsel self-report the opinion and order to show cause to NJ and NY attorney licensing entities and serve copies on “his client and discuss their implications with her.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.N.M. – Magistrate Judge Steven C. Yarbrough

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Maturin v. T-Mobile USA, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258578 (D.N.M. Dec. 15, 2025), the court recommended granting in part and denying in part defendant’s motion for sanctions alleging bad-faith conduct and frivolous pleadings, ordering the plaintiff to pay defendant’s reasonable expenses for investigating “false citations” used in the plaintiff’s filings and for litigating the sanctions motion and supplement. The court further recommended that, in all future filings, the plaintiff must disclose any use of AI in drafting and certify under Rule 11 that all AI-provided information, including all case citations, has been verified for accuracy. While denying terminating sanctions, the court warned that failure to comply with court orders or sanctions may result in dismissal, and permitted defendant to seek additional sanctions for noncompliance.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D.S.C. – Magistrate Judge William S. Brown

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Jeter v. Adusa Transp. LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 270555 (D.S.C. December 15, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s response to defendant’s motion to dismiss cited nonexistent authorities, misquoted existing case law, and invoked cases for propositions they do not support—conduct the court observed is “consistent with on the use of [GenAI] . . . . without proper verification.” The court cautioned that misusing GenAI in this manner “may violate Rule 11” and emphasized that misquotations and nonexistent authorities reflect failing to read governing law. Given the novelty of GenAI and the plaintiff’s pro se status, the court declined sanctions but instructed that any GenAI-assisted submissions must be verified by reading the actual cases, rules, and statutes. The court warned that continued failure to verify citations and quotations will wrongfully multiply proceedings and may evidence bad faith, exposing the plaintiff to sanctions in the future. The court recommended granting in part and denying in part the motion to dismiss on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: M.D. Pa. – Judge Julia K. Munley

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Williams v. Assistant DA John R. Canavan, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258853 (M.D. Pa. Dec. 15, 2025), the court addressed the pro se plaintiff’s reliance on GenAI in multiple filings, noting an “uptick” in pro se litigants using “cut-and-paste AI outputs” and finding that the plaintiff’s guardian-ad-litem motion included an AI “hallucination.” The court also observed AI-aided objections that included a table of authorities mismatched to the filing and stylistic discrepancies from Plaintiff’s handwritten IFP motion. Citing Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b)(2)-(c), the court warned the plaintiff that future “unchecked reliance on AI . . . will result in sanctions,” including striking pleadings and dismissal with prejudice. Substantively, the court dismissed the pro se plaintiff’s claims without prejudice on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: C.D. Cal. – Judge Fred W. Slaughter

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In N.Z. v. Fenix Int’l Ltd., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 259988 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 12, 2025), the court denied the plaintiffs’ motion for leave to withdraw multiple opposition briefs and file “corrective” briefs after identifying AI-generated inaccuracies, and imposed Rule 11 sanctions on the plaintiff’s counsel and co-counsel for misuse of GenAI and failure to verify AI-modified content. The court found that four opposition briefs contained hallucinated authorities, mischaracterized law and quotations, and incorrect record citations—including misstatements of the court’s prior order—and held that allowing corrective briefs would “not remediate, but rather exacerbate” prejudice where defendants had already expended substantial resources addressing the tainted filings and where proposed corrections still contained errors. The plaintiff’s counsel also admitted to using ChatGPT to “draft large portions of the briefs, analyze the legal research [the attorney] performed,” and “combine and shorten” materials. The court also observed that the attorney failed to realize when ChatGPT modified text and authorities, did not verify accuracy, and did not inform her co-counsel of her GenAI use; and co-counsel, relying on the drafting attorney’s prior work and facing a deadline, did not perform a full cite check. Concluding that counsel had certified content not warranted by existing law and failed to supervise consistent with Cal. R. Prof’l Conduct 5.1 and the court’s AI standing order, the court sanctioned both the primary offending individual attorney and his law firm (jointly and severally) $10,000 and sanctioned the plaintiffs’ individual co-counsel $3,000, required status reporting on remedial measures and certification that all future citations accurately support the asserted propositions, directed service of the order on the California and Arizona state bars and each individual plaintiff, and discharged the order to show cause.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Or. – Magistrate Judge Mark D. Clarke

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Couvrette v. Wisnovsky, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 273533 (D. Or. Dec. 12, 2025), the court imposed sanctions on the plaintiffs and the plaintiffs’ attorney for filing three briefs containing fabricated cases and quotations, which the court held constituted violations of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and local rules. The plaintiffs continued to cite to nonexistent cases after the defendants called the errors to their attention and suggested GenAI misuse. In response to the defendants’ motion for sanctions, the plaintiffs simply deleted the non-existent citations and quotation marks while leaving the fabricated legal contentions unchanged. Plaintiffs argued that “[t]he inclusion of inaccurate citations was inadvertent and the result of reliance on an automated legal citation tool” and that they had taken proper corrective action. Defendants then re-filed their motion for sanctions. At the sanctions hearing, the court found that the plaintiffs failed to take corrective action and instead “attempted a coverup,” violating Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and the Oregon Rules of Professional Conduct. The court also found the plaintiff himself was likely the person using GenAI to draft and “share[d] responsibility [with his counsel] for the use of non-existent cases and fabricated quotations,” thus warranting sanctions against the plaintiff as well. As sanction, the court struck without leave to refile plaintiffs’ summary judgment briefing; imposed a $15,500 fine plus attorney’s fees and expenses on the plaintiff’s counsel; vacated the order allowing former local counsel to withdraw and ordered him show cause why he should not be subject to sanctions; and dismissed the plaintiff’s claims with prejudice.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D.N.Y. – Magistrate Judge Gary Stein

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Matthew NG v. Amguard Ins. Co., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 260114 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 12, 2025), the court noted an inaccurate citation in the plaintiff’s letter motion that “provides no support” for the proposition asserted and assumed the filing “may have been drafted with the assistance of a [GenAI] tool.” The court expressly directed compliance with Judge Broderick’s Individual Rule 4(J) (which requires any party who uses GenAI in preparing a filing to disclose that use and to certify independent review and accuracy verification of all AI-drafted portions). While denying the plaintiff’s requests on service and a stay, the court set a deadline for the plaintiff to file either an opposition to the motion to dismiss or an amended complaint and emphasized future adherence to the court’s AI disclosure and verification rule.

judge: Senior Resident Superior Court Judge Martin B. McGee

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.C. Super. Ct. – Senior Resident Superior Court Judge Martin B. McGee

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary:
Judge McGee’s Revised Administrative Order on Artificial Intelligence for Judicial District 25 supersedes the court’s prior July 23, 2024 order, applies immediately to all civil and criminal proceedings in Cabarrus County, and lays out requirements for AI use and disclosure, concerning both court filings and evidentiary issues.

While the order does not bar the use of GenAI for litigation purposes, it reminds litigants that each submission to the court is the ultimate responsibility of the signing attorney or party, and requires "[h]uman-in-the-Loop (HITL) oversight” (i.e, having a human decision-maker “independently review, verify, and approve any AI output before it is relied on, acted on, or submitted in any court proceeding”). The order also reminds attorneys of their professional duties to “maintain technical competence regarding” the technologies used and to ensure they do not place confidential or privileged information into public AI tools. With respect to recording and transcription, the order provides that only official reporters or clerks may use AI tools to capture, record, transcribe, process, or alter audio or video without judicial authorization. Jurors are prohibited from using GenAI for any purpose, while litigants may use GenAI for non-disruptive legal research during hearings with appropriate attorney oversight. Finally, the order requires that parties must disclose no later than 90 days before trial if they have used GenAI to “generate[], offer[], materially alter[], enhance[] or analyze[]” evidence. After that window, disclosure must be immediate. By contrast, non-substantive AI use (such as “word processing, spell-check, grammar or “administrative tasks,” GenAI use in discovery, and use of GenAI to aid court reporters or transcriptionists) does not require disclosure. When there is reasonable basis for doubt as to the accuracy of a document, parties must use “best efforts” to verify the evidence’s validity and must meet and confer to resolve concerns about AI-generated evidence (and such concerns must be raised before trial absent extraordinary circumstances). Should parties fail to comply with this order, the court may impose sanctions, but the court will favorably consider “timely, good-faith disclosures” and taking “reasonable steps to verify authenticity and reliability.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: Pa. Sup. Ct. Interim Policy on the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence – Judicial Officers and Court Personnel

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Research
results.summary: Effective December 8, 2025, the Pennsylvania Supreme Court’s Interim Policy on the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence by Judicial Officers and Court Personnel governs court personnel’s use of GenAI tools and provides insight for parties on how to approach the use of GenAI. The policies define GenAI as an all-encompassing term for “algorithms and/or computer processes that use artificial intelligence to generate text, audio, or images based on user prompts.” Before any GenAI tool is used, it must first be approved by court leadership who ensure that the tool will keep information “confidential and privileged.” Court personnel must, however, presume that GenAI inputs will not be treated as confidential and privileged and remain aware of GenAI’s “capabilities and limitations,” including hallucinations, biases, and inaccuracies. The court welcomes GenAI use by its personnel for, among other things, document summaries, preliminary legal research, and drafts and edits of their own work. Ultimately, the GenAI user remains responsible for the output’s completeness and accuracy.

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Pa. – Judge Marilyn J. Horan

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Research
Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mullins v. Duquesne Univ. of the Holy Spirit, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 251137 (W.D. Pa. Dec. 5, 2025), the court granted the defendant’s motion to strike the pro se plaintiff’s reply brief where the plaintiff misused of GenAI by including multiple citation and quotation errors “that appear to be the result of Plaintiff using [GenAI] drafting tools,” including a “phantom quote.” The plaintiff “implict[ly]” admitted to using GenAI tools, which resulted in hallucinations, but the court criticized his “unwillingness to affirmatively admit” to such use. Emphasizing that neither the court nor opposing counsel is responsible for “fact‑checking” a party’s filings, the court held that filing AI‑generated content without thorough pre‑filing verification “wastes” judicial and party resources. The court therefore: (1) struck the reply but granted leave to refile a corrected version; and (2) ordered that all future filings must include a certification disclosing whether “he has utilized any [AI] drafting tools in the research and/or drafting of the document being filed,” identifying each AI tool, pinpointing the specific AI‑generated portions, and certifying the accuracy of all citations and authorities, warning that violations may trigger sanctions under Rule 11 or other authority.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Cal. Super. –  Judge Michael B. Wilson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Gibbs v. City of Compton, 2025 Cal. Super. LEXIS 73275 (Cal. Super. Dec. 4, 2025), the court found the plaintiff's opposition to the defendant’s motion to set aside default judgment “unreliable” on its face and that the plaintiff’s counsel drafted it using GenAI without properly reviewing it for accuracy. The court reached these conclusions because the plaintiff's filings (including a declaration by the plaintiff’s counsel) incorrectly asserted that defendant's memorandum violated the page limit, misquoted a court form, and included a nonexistent case quotation. The court also found GenAI output language at the end of the plaintiff’s opposition briefing that clearly demonstrated that “Plaintiff’s counsel utilized an AI tool to draft and submit the papers.” Judge Wilson therefore “disregard[ed] improper citations and arguments lacking proper legal citation” and “exercise[d] [the court’s] discretion to afford the declaration accompanying Plaintiff's Opposition less credence,” declining to consider the plaintiff’s arguments because of lack of legal support. The court ultimately granted the defendant’s motion to set aside the entry of default and default judgment.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Ill. – Judge Rebecca R. Pallmeyer

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Dorsey v. Ponce, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 250368 (N.D. Ill. Dec. 4, 2025), the court expressed concerns about generative AI-fabricated citations in the pro se plaintiff’s briefing. After discovering that several cited cases “do not exist,” the court expressed its suspicion that “many of these cases are the product of an artificial intelligence tool,” and ordered that all future filings must include a traditional reporter or database citation for any case cited. The court warned that any future “fabricated or hallucinated” citations will result in sanctions, including striking the filing and potential dismissal of the action. The underlying motion to dismiss was granted in part and denied in part on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: W.D. Okla. – Chief Judge Timothy D. DeGiusti

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hill v. Okla. Cnty. Crim. Just. Auth., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 250359 (W.D. Okla. Dec. 4, 2025), the court adopted a magistrate judge’s recommendation imposing sanctions on plaintiff’s counsel for citing non‑existent cases and using ChatGPT‑generated content in briefing, finding the conduct part of a broader pattern of missed deadlines, erroneous filings, and a “general lack of care.” At a show‑cause hearing, counsel “confirmed that he used Chat GPT” to draft a response and attempted to deflect responsibility to staff, which the magistrate judge deemed inconsistent with Rule 11 obligations and harmful to the integrity of the judicial process. The court required plaintiff’s counsel to pay attorney’s fees and costs associated with the show-cause hearing; disclose in all future pleadings whether GenAI was used, identify the specific tool was used, and certify that any AI‑drafted portions (including all citations and legal authorities) have been checked for accuracy; and complete (within 90 days) at least three hours of CLE in ethics or legal technology, including at least one hour on the use of GenAI in the legal context.

linkToCourtOrder.text: 10th Cir. – Judges Timothy M. Tymkovich, Bobby R. Baldock, Gregory A. Phillips

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Moore v. City of Del. City, 2025 U.S. App. LEXIS 31411 (10th Cir. Dec. 3, 2025), the court dismissed the pro se appellant’s case for citing eleven nonexistent cases and misrepresenting the holdings of at least two cases in her opening brief. The court “suspected” GenAI use and ordered the appellant to provide copies of the nonexistent cases or explain how the errors occurred in the brief, identify which GenAI tool she used in preparing the document, and show cause why she should not be sanctioned. The court was not convinced by the appellant’s response to the show cause order (and indeed found that she also “turned to a GenAI tool for assistance in drafting her response”), and concluded that the appellant’s “citation of nonexistent cases and her misrepresentation of at least one other case has impaired judicial efficiency and amounts to a failure to comply with Fed. R. App. P. 28(a)(8)(A)’s requirement that an appellant's brief cite supporting authority.” The court dismissed the appeal and ordered that the appellant disclose GenAI use and verify the accuracy of her citations in future filings. Finally, the court warned “future litigants, pro se and those represented by counsel alike, that outright dismissal—without a merits ruling—may be an appropriate sanction in a given case,” as may monetary or other sanctions (including awarding the opposing party’s attorney fees incurred from the “unscrutinized use of GenAI”).

The certification must be signed by all contributors to the filing.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

The order noted several examples where defendants’ citations either appeared fictitious or did not support the propositions asserted, highlighting counsel’s duty to verify authorities before filing. linkToCourtOrder.text: M.D. Pa. – Judge Julia K. Munley

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In United States v. Boehm, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 235285, the pro se defendant included inaccurate legal arguments and quotations “that appear to be generated by [GenAI] using legitimate case citations” in his request for an order clarifying the conditions of his supervised release. Although noting that the “improper use of [GenAI] wastes the time and resources of the court,” Judge Munley took into account the defendant’s status as a “formerly incarcerated individual attempting to advocate his position as he transitions from a prison setting to society” and many GenAI tools’ “tout[ing] [of] their ability to conduct in-depth research,” and therefore declined to award sanctions. The court admonished the defendant for the misrepresentations, however, and dismissed the motion without prejudice. Judge Munley further ordered that for any future filings, the defendant must file a separate affidavit disclosing the details of any GenAI use, and certifying that he has personally checked the accuracy of each citation.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Or. Ct. App. – Judge Erin Lagesen

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Charles Ringo v. Colquhoun Design Studio, LLC, 2025 Ore. App. LEXIS 1957 (Or. Ct. App. Dec. 3, 2025), the court directed respondents’ counsel to pay $2,000 for submitting a brief “littered with fabricated cases, a fabricated quotation, and fabricated substantive law.” Judge Ashby noted that the fabricated cases “likely” resulted from GenAI use and constitute a violation of ORCP 17C(3)’s certification that “the claims, defenses, and other legal positions taken in the pleading, motion or other document are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for the extension, modification or reversal of existing law or the establishment of new law,” as well as a violation of the attorney’s professional duties. The court described how the attorney’s conduct “strain[ed] [the court’s] limited judicial resources” and “jeopardizes the rule of law,” as “[t]he injection of false precedent into the practice of law shakes the foundation of our judicial system.” Judge Ashby thus ordered monetary sanctions of $500 for each false citation and $1,000 for each false quotation or statement of law, totaling to $2,000. The court allowed counsel to resubmit the stricken brief, but required that all submissions by this counsel going forward “contain a certification that (1) counsel drafted the brief and did not use [GenAI] to produce a draft of the brief; (2) counsel has read each case and each other source of law cited in the brief; and (3) counsel has verified that every source of law cited, quoted[,] or paraphrased exists.”

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Magee v. New Balance Athletics., Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234410 (E.D. Ark. Dec. 2, 2025), the pro se plaintiff used GenAI to prepare his response to the defendant's motion to dismiss, in which he allegedly “misstated the holdings of twenty-seven of the thirty cases cited, invented false quotations from twenty-three cases, and fabricated two cases.” In response, the plaintiff apologized and promised to do better in the future. Judge Marshall “accept[ed] [the plaintiff's] apology” but “admonish[ed] him,” warning that he must verify his sources in future filings and show cause why the court should not impose a monetary sanction for his “generative-AI-infected response.” The court ultimately granted the defendant’s motion to strike the plaintiff's response and denied pending motions to dismiss and for summary judgment.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Mich. –  Judge F. Kay Behm

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Jarrus v. Governor of Michigan, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234830, the pro se plaintiffs objected to the Magistrate Judge’s order for the plaintiffs to show cause for the use of GenAI in drafting their motion to amend their complaint. Judge Behm suspected that the plaintiffs again used GenAI in their objection. The court noted that the plaintiffs’ briefing appeared to be “nothing more than a copy-paste from a chatbot-style generative AI tool” with a “series of . . . false citations” of real cases with explanations that did not match the real case. Judge Behm noted that the use of GenAI for drafting without independent citation checking “unquestionably” violates Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. After the Magistrate Judge's warning (see Jarrus v. Governor of Michigan, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 221984 (E.D. Mich. Nov. 7, 2025)), the court ordered plaintiffs to show cause why they should not be sanctioned. Judge Behm highlighted that Rule 11 “imposes obligations on each and every signatory to a briefing, not just the person who was the primary drafter.” Thus, even though one of the plaintiffs did not draft the documents and only reviewed them at a “very general level” before signing, the court held her “equally responsible for the use of misrepresented or otherwise improper citations,” and warned that further misconduct could result in “a dismissal of her claims in their entirety.” Although the plaintiffs admitted to misusing GenAI (ChatGPT specifically) in their filings, the court found that sanctions were warranted. Judge Behmt adopted Magistrate Judge Patti's recommendation of $200 per GenAI citation and imposed a $600 fine ($300 per plaintiff), warning that failure to pay may result in dismissal. The court reasoned that one of the plaintiff's $20 per month spending on a ChatGPT subscription suggested an ability to pay sanctions. The court further warned that it will consider dismissal of the case if the plaintiffs file further briefings “with even a single misrepresented, misquoted, or fictitious case that is caused by the use of [GenAI].”

linkToCourtOrder.text: M.D. Fla. – Judge Paul G. Byron

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Ford v. James Koutoulas & Lgbcoin, Ltd., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234696 (M.D. Fla. Dec. 2, 2025), the defendants' motion for summary judgment contained several citations that the court and the plaintiffs suspected were GenAI hallucinations, where the court was “unable to locate” the cited authorities. The defendants did not deny plaintiffs’ allegations that their filings contained AI hallucinations. Judge Byron noted that citing to nonexistent cases is a “misrepresentation to the Court,” is sanctionable under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, and violates the Florida local rules and rules of professional conduct. The court warned that continued “unethical practices” may result in sanctions. The court denied the defendants' motion on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Ga. – Magistrate Judge Russell G. Vineyard

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Johnson v. Insight Glob., LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 253973 (N.D. Ga. Dec. 2, 2025), the court admonished the pro se plaintiff for submitting a motion to remand that contained “citations to non-existent caselaw, which may [have been] the result of [GenAI].” The court warned the plaintiff that his “pro se status does not shield him from the same duty of candor to the court as imposed on any other litigant,” and that further reliance on non-existent case law may result in sanctions. The court dismissed the motion on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Fla. – Magistrate Judge Panayotta Augustin-Birch

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Button v. McCawley, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234737 (S.D. Fla. Dec. 2, 2025), the pro se plaintiffs cited numerous GenAI-hallucinated cases in their response to the defendant's motion for attorney's fees. Magistrate Judge Augustin-Birch noted that the plaintiffs “falsely attribute[d] quotes” and “cite[d] non-existent cases.” The court ultimately decided not to recommend imposing sanctions because of the plaintiffs’ pro se status and the fact that it was their first infraction. However, the court warned that “going forward, Plaintiffs must ensure that all of their caselaw quotations and citations are accurate.” Magistrate Judge Augustin-Birch recommended granting the motion in part and denying it in part on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Cal. Ct. App. – Judge William F. Fahey

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Shayan v. Shakib, 2025 LEXIS 595242 (Cal. Ct. App. 2d. Dec. 1, 2025), the appellant's attorney filed a brief containing numerous fabricated case citations and a transcript from a hearing in a different matter, and the respondent asked the court to strike the brief and dismiss the appeal as a sanction. In response, the appellant’s attorney claimed that the errors were the result of his staff failing to replace placeholder language from the draft brief, not the result of GenAI use, that he checked each citation, and that the errors reflected only “minor” wording differences. The court disagreed with that the attorney’s characterization and noted that both the brief and the opposition contained “inaccurate representations of case holdings.” The court held that these errors violated local rules, the Ca. Code Civ. Proc., and the rules of professional conduct, regardless of whether the fabrications were the result of GenAI use. Furthermore, Judge Fahey observed that even if the attorney did review the accuracy of the citations, “the rules of this court require him to present accurate, truthful representations of the facts and law to this court,” which the attorney “did not do.” The court ordered that the attorney pay $7,500 in sanctions to the clerk of court (due to the time the court spent verifying the citations, the attorney's failure to accept responsibility, and the continued mistakes in the opposition motion) and also struck the appellant’s opening brief and required the filing of a corrected brief within 10 days. Judge Fahey served a copy of the order on the State Bar.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Colo. – Magistrate Judge Maritza Dominguez Braswell

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary:
In her guidance document entitled “Guidance on Responsible AI Use in Court Proceedings,” Magistrate Judge Braswell provides a framework for parties practicing before her court to address AI-related issues, particularly hallucinations in court filings. Notably, the guidance “is not an order and does not establish new rules or requirements” and “should [not] be interpreted to modify or supersede any applicable law, rule, professional obligation, or order issued by another judge.” The guidance acknowledges that AI “presents opportunities for innovation, growth, and increased access to justice” and encourages parties to “learn to leverage it responsibly,” while also noting the various “challenge[s]” that AI poses, only “one part” of which is hallucinations. The court’s guidance emphasizes that “all inaccurate statements made to a tribunal, regardless of how they
were generated, are a serious matter,” and that the “same rules apply” regardless of the source of the error. When hallucinations are found, the guidance encourages parties to confer to explore remedial measures, inform the court, and escalate only if the drafter refuses to engage in good faith. The guidance lists a variety of factors the court will consider when evaluating submission of AI hallucinations, including whether the drafter engaged in good faith, took responsibility, took immediate steps to mitigate harm, follows an AI Use Policy, took reasonable steps to verify accuracy before filing, and whether the drafter is a pro se party. The court will also consider the nature and extent of the hallucinations, whether they caused meaningful prejudice, and whether they resulted in a significant waste of judicial resources. Finally, the guidance encourages caution in the legal industry regarding AI-created bias, ensuring adequate data privacy safeguards, and ensuring human autonomy over important decisions.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Tex. – Chief Judge Amos L. Mazzant

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: Effective December 1, 2025, Judge Mazzant’s General Order 25-07 (E.D. Tex. Oct. 31, 2025) amends the Eastern District of Texas Local Rule CV-11(g) on GenAI use. Prior to the amendment, the rule warned only pro se litigants that they “remain responsible for the accuracy and quality” of their submissions to the court and cautioned that GenAI “may produce factually or legally inaccurate content.” As revised, all litigants are now “bound by the requirements of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11” and must “review and verify all content” (rather than just “computer-generated content,” as the Rule previously required) when using GenAI. Judge Mazzant’s Comment indicated that the amendments were prompted by an “increasing number of filings” where litigants “fail[ed] to review and verify the factual and legal accuracy” of content produced by GenAI. The parallel rule for attorneys, Local Rule AT-3(m), was also amended to conform to LR CV-11(g).

linkToCourtOrder.text: M.D. Fla. – Judge William F. Jung

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Bey v. Glass, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 233422 (M.D. Fla. Dec. 1, 2025), the pro se plaintiff cited a nonexistent case in his complaint. Judge Jung suspected that the hallucinated citation was the result of GenAI use in drafting after the court “cannot find . . . based on independent research” a case the plaintiff cited and warned the plaintiff that the court has the “inherent authority” under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 to impose sanctions for GenAI use, “including the complete dismissal and termination of the case.” The court dismissed the plaintiff's complaint without prejudice on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Colo. – Magistrate Judge Kathryn A. Starnella

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Research
Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Hanson v. Nest Home Lending, LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 233214 (D. Colo. Nov. 28, 2025), the court made absolute its prior order to show cause after finding that the pro se plaintiffs’ complaint, motions, and responses contained “fabricated legal authority” and “hallucinated citations” in violation of Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11. The plaintiffs argued they acted in good faith and that any errors stemmed from their “ignorance of the law,” but the court held that Rule 11’s objective‑reasonableness standard applies to all filers, including pro se litigants, and that good faith does not excuse submission of fake or inaccurate citations. The court emphasized the harms caused by such misuse, including wasted party and judicial resources, diversion from merits issues, and reputational harm to the legal system, and noted that plaintiffs had been on notice of problems in their complaint yet continued filing papers with fictitious or mischaracterized authority. The court further observed that plaintiffs’ response compounded the issue by misquoting precedent and citing out‑of‑jurisdiction law for irrelevant propositions. Magistrate Judge Starnella recommended sweeping non‑monetary remedies tailored to deterrence and docket integrity, including striking the tainted filings identified in the show‑cause order, granting the plaintiffs 45 days to file an amended complaint, and requiring that all future filings include a detailed certification, in table format, listing each citation, confirming its existence and that it supports the proposition asserted, and identifying the source where the authority was obtained. The court also directed plaintiffs to use “legitimate” research databases and to contact the Federal Pro Se Clinic regarding publicly available research resources. The court denied pending motions to dismiss as moot in light of its recommendation and warned that continued misconduct could lead to dismissal. While recognizing the increasing use of GenAI, the court underscored that parties must independently verify accuracy and that “using a fake opinion to support an argument is a violation of Rule 11(b)(2),” regardless of intent.

linkToCourtOrder.text: C.D. Cal. – Judge Stanley Blumenfeld, Jr.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Anaya v. Target Corp., No. 2:25-cv-09135-SB-RAO (Nov. 26, 2025), the court sanctioned plaintiff's counsel in the amount of $1,250 for filing a brief “containing a case that did not stand for the cited proposition” in support of the plaintiff's motion to remand. Judge Blumenfeld noted that the court had previously cautioned the plaintiff about the importance of abiding by court rules, warning that further violations could result in sanctions. In response to the order to show cause, the attorney explained that his associate used “AI to refine a small portion of the Reply" but he did not disclose the use because he did not realize the associate had used GenAI at the time. Judge Blumenfeld determined that this conduct violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court further “admonished” the firm's managing partner based on his “indirect responsibility” for “failing to take appropriate steps to ensure firmwide compliance with all applicable rules and orders.” Judge Blumenfeld determined that the despite his candor, the filing attorney’s “use of AI without proper disclosure and blatant misuse of case law in reliance on AI—coupled with his careless oversight of the case as lead counsel for Plaintiff—warrant appropriate sanctions.” The court ordered $1,250 in monetary sanctions and notification of the sanction to the State Bar of California.

linkToCourtOrder.text: M.D. Fla. – Judge Sheri Polster Chappell

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Doc App, Inc. v. Leafwell, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232188 (M.D. Fla. Nov. 26, 2025), the court granted the defendant’s motion for sanctions after the plaintiff’s counsel included various inaccurate citations (including to nonexistent and out-of-context cases) in its motion for a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction. The court found that the plaintiff’s attorney violated Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b) for including erroneous citations due to his “reckless use of AI.” The court concluded that sanctions were particularly warranted where counsel responded to the court’s order to show cause in a “colossal [] collection of excuses and projection” with a “defiant tone and condescending communications with and about his opposing counsel,” and where plaintiff’s counsel was not forthcoming about his use of AI. Judge Chappell held that the attorney failed to take responsibility for his actions and reminded him of his role as “an officer of the court” with “a duty of candor to the tribunal.” As sanctions, Judge Chappell ordered plaintiff’s counsel to: (i) pay the defendant’s attorneys’ fees and costs associated with their response to the motion; (ii) file a copy of this order to any complaint filed in the next 2 years in the Middle District of Florida; (iii) attend a Florida CLE class on legal ethics and AI by January 30, 2026 and file a certificate of completion by February 20, 2026; and (iv) send a copy of this order to his client’s CEO and file an affidavit confirming that the CEO has read the order. The court also referred plaintiff’s counsel to the Florida Bar for “appropriate discipline” and asked the clerk to send the entire case record to the Florida Bar.

court: New York - Federal District Court (Southern District)

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D.N.Y. – Judge Vernon S. Broderick

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Peak v. Maraj-Petty, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232924 (S.D.N.Y. Nov. 26, 2025), in an order continuing the stay of the case, Judge Broderick raised his concern that the pro se plaintiff was using GenAI throughout the litigation “because of his persistent misquotes and interpretation of cases.” Most recently, the plaintiff included two hallucinated quotations in an opposition letter. Though the court was sympathetic to the plaintiff’s status of representation, the plaintiff was reminded of Individual Rule of Practice in Civil Cases 4(J) that requires parties to disclose any use of AI and certify that all GenAI-drafted portions are verified and accurate.

linkToCourtOrder.text: D. Conn. – Judge Vernon D. Oliver

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Andre v. Warden, FCI Danbury, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232986 (D. Conn. Nov. 25, 2025), the pro se petitioner filed a motion for relief from judgment that, when considered in light of other pleadings, appeared “unusually well-written and formatted for a pro se litigant” and prompted the court to think they were drafted by an attorney. Upon further review, the court discovered that the motion contained “multiple significant hallucinations” and inaccurate citations. During the hearing on the court’s order to show cause, the petitioner admitted that he heavily relied on AI tools during the drafting process and even asked some of his “friends” to “ask AI certain questions” and then have “AI ‘draft up motions.’” Judge Oliver found that the petitioner acted in “subjective bad faith,” thereby violating Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, because he made “misrepresentations to the Court as to the origins of the filings” by directing “third parties to use generative AI and then concealed his actions from the Court when given the opportunity to accept responsibility.” As sanctions, the court ultimately struck the petitioner’s motion for relief with prejudice.

court: Illinois - Ill. App. Ct., Second Dist.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Ill. App. 2d Dist. – Judge Susan F. Hutchinson

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Research
Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Pletcher v. Vill. of Libertyville Police Pension Bd., No. 2-24-0416, 2025 Ill. App. Unpub. LEXIS 2115 (Nov. 24, 2025), the court granted defendant’s motion for sanctions against the pro se plaintiff pursuant to Illinois Supreme Court Rule 375(a). Specifically, the plaintiff filed a complaint for an administrative review of the Village of Libertyville Pension Board’s decision on plaintiff’s application for disability pension and a supporting brief. The court found that the plaintiff’s brief cited to multiple cases that do not exist, and also cited to “actual cases” but with “fictitious quotes and holdings.” The court concluded that these fictitious citations were “hallucinated by [GenAI]” and that the plaintiff violated Rule 341(h)(7). As a result, the court sanctioned the plaintiff by striking plaintiff’s brief and dismissing his appeal.

Nov. 24, 2025), the court adopted the magistrate judge’s report and recommendation, and warned the plaintiffs of sanctions, including “the striking of their briefs, fines, or other appropriate actions” if they misuse GenAI tools in future filings. In doing so, Judge Doughty referred to the preceding report and recommendation issued by Magistrate Judge Kayla Dye McClusky that highlighted the plaintiffs’ presumed use of GenAI given their citations to nonexistent cases, inaccurate citations, and “mischaracterizations of cases which do exist.” See Mills v. Rocket Mortg. LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232163 (W.D. La. Nov. 6, 2025).

linkToCourtOrder.text: C.D. Cal. – Judge Mark C. Scarsi

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Mangasarian v. City of Glendale, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 270943 (C.D. Cal. Nov. 21, 2025), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiffs “prepared certain filings with the assistance of a [GenAI] service.” The court warned the parties that any filings prepared using GenAI must include a declaration disclosing the GenAI use and certifying that the filer independently verified the accuracy of the contents as in compliance with Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. Judge Scarsi further warned that failure to follow this directive may result in sanctions. The court denied the plaintiffs’ application for a temporary restraining order on other grounds.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Del. Ch. – Senior Magistrate Selena E. Molina

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Gupta v. Safko et al., No. CV 2024-1296-SEM, 2025 WL 3269046, at *1 (Del. Ch. Nov. 21, 2025), the court denied defendants’ request that pro plaintiff should be sanctioned. Although the court concluded that the plaintiff’s filings “do appear to have been generated through GenAI,” because of his pro se status and the fact that there was no “clear showing” of hallucinations or “glaring” misrepresentations, the court ultimately declined to impose sanctions. Senior Magistrate Molina did, however, order that the plaintiff must not only “ensure every future filing” is accurate, but also “certify any future use of GenAI” to the court by identifying what GenAI tool was used and how, and that it was reviewed for accuracy.

judge: Senior Magistrate Judge Selena E. Molina

linkToCourtOrder.text: Del. Ch. Ct. – Senior Magistrate Judge Selena E. Molina

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Anuj Gupta v. Safko, 2025 Del. Ch. LEXIS 2041 (Del. Ch. Nov. 21, 2025), the defendants requested sanctions under Court of Chancery Rule 11, as the pro se plaintiff seemingly generated his filings with a GenAI tool. Judge Molina indicated that this conduct “raises several concerns about the Plaintiff’s ability to satisfy Rule 11’s dictates,” wastes parties’ and the court’s resources, and poses “reputational harms to the legal system.” However, given the plaintiff’s pro se status and lack of “any clear showing of hallucinations or glaring legal or factual misrepresentations in his filings,” the court declined to impose sanctions. Nevertheless, the court did order the plaintiff to certify any future use of GenAI and warned that “this leniency will not continue” should the plaintiff fail to comply with the court’s certification requirement.

Ohio Nov. 21, 2025), in denying the pro se plaintiff’s renewed motion for a temporary restraining order and emergency motion to expedite a ruling, the court discussed the plaintiff’s “false, likely hallucinated” citation from a previous motion and warned him that “continuing to flood the docket with frivolous motions, oppositions, and filings will subject him to sanctions,” including costs, attorney fees, and “a permanent injunction from filing new documents in this dispute or the dismissal of the dispute.”

court: Minnesota - Minn. Dist. Ct., Cnty. of Hennepin, Fourth Jud. Dist.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Minn. Dist. – Judge Laurie J. Miller

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Nuvola, LLC v. Wright, 2025 Minn. Dist. LEXIS 5940 (Minn. Dist. Nov. 21, 2025), the defendant’s counsel “cited multiple non-existent cases” in his motion to compel arbitration. The court set a hearing for the defendant’s attorney to explain the non-existent caselaw, and for the plaintiff’s counsel to explain why he did not bring the issue to the court’s attention. In response, the defendant’s counsel apologized to the court for submitting non-existent citations, and admitted to using GenAI but claimed that he expected the program to generate a brief only using the verified cases he had provided it. Judge Miller concluded that the attorney’s conduct violated Minn. R. Civ. P. 11.02(b)’s requirement that an attorney certifies, in presenting a document to the court, that “the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions there are warranted by existing law,” and Minn. Stat. § 549.211’s requirement that pleadings are grounded in law. As sanction, the court referred the defendant’s attorney to the Minnesota Lawyers Professional Responsibility Board; directed him to “conduct five (5) presentations to lawyers and/or law students on the dangers of using artificial intelligence in the legal profession”; and imposed a financial penalty of $1,000. The court also noted that the plaintiff’s counsel “offered little explanation for his failure to check the citations in Defendant’s brief.” While the court did not sanction the plaintiff’s attorney, Judge Miller “remind[ed] counsel that it is the obligation of counsel on both sides to respond to each other's arguments, including completing a basic cite-check of the cases cited by the other side.”

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Fla. – Judge Rodolfo A. Ruiz II

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Dubinin v. Papazian, No. 25-CV-23877-RAR, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 229362 (S.D. Fla. Nov. 21, 2025), following the court’s orders to show cause why plaintiff’s counsel should not be sanctioned for “cites to non-existent cases and quotations in a brief that appeared to be drafted using [GenAI],” plaintiff’s attorney denied using AI, but “admitted to filing a brief riddled with inaccuracies,” claiming the drafting had been delegated to another attorney. The court reviewed the two attorneys’ other filings in different matters and found that the two attorneys had previously been caught submitting filings that “may have been drafted using [GenAI].” Because of the filing attorney’s “misconduct in this case and others, including her misrepresentations to the Court,” and the fact that her “use of AI was ‘so egregious that this Court finds that [it was] done in bad faith,’” the court concluded that sanctions are appropriate under the court’s inherent powers, Local Rule 6(b)(2)(A) of the Southern District of Florida, and 28 U.S.C. § 1927. The court ultimately struck plaintiff’s complaint and dismissed the action without prejudice, ordered the filing attorney to pay defendant’s attorney fees, and referred both the filing and drafting attorneys to the Florida Bar and Grievance Committee.

linkToCourtOrder.text: S.D. Fla. – Magistrate Judge William Matthewman

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Clayman v. Bessant, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232878 (S.D. Fla. Nov. 21, 2025), the pro se plaintiff admitted that the only cited case in his motion to alter or amend an order was an “AI-generated hallucination.” The court noted that the plaintiff is permitted to use AI for drafting assistance but must ensure that all cases are accurate. Judge Matthewman also provided a “final warning” that the plaintiff could face sanctions and a restricted filing if he continues to misuse AI. The court ultimately denied plaintiff’s motion to alter or amend a judgment.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Supreme Court of N.Y.S., Richmond County – Judge Wayne M. Ozzi

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Grymes Dev. Co. v. Fodera, 2025 NYLJ LEXIS 3543, 2025 LX 552729, the court concluded that the third-party plaintiff’s counsel cited “a non-existent case citation and non-existent quotations . . . as a result of his reliance on [GenAI]” following the third-party plaintiff’s motion for summary judgment. Although the court acknowledged the potential benefits of GenAI, it held that the error “cannot be considered minimal” because attorneys have ethical duties to not make false statements to the court. The court therefore denied the summary judgment motion with prejudice, ordered the third-party plaintiff’s attorney to pay a $2,000 sanction, and ordered the counsel to reimburse the third-party defendants’ legal fees.

linkToCourtOrder.text: Bankr. M.D. Ala. – Judge Christopher L. Hawkins

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In In re Jackson Hosp. & Clinic, No. 25-30256-CLH, 2025 Bankr. LEXIS 3039 (Bankr. M.D. Ala. Nov. 20, 2025), the court issued an order for an attorney and her law firm (who were representing a creditor in a bankruptcy proceeding) to show cause why they should not be sanctioned for the repeated misuse of GenAI in court filings. Specifically, following the creditor’s filing of a related motion, the court observed that cited regulations “did not stand for the legal propositions” for which they were cited, noted that one attorney in particular had also previously cited caselaw that did not stand for the principle she cited it for, and pointed out that the party’s supporting briefs for a motion to reconsider the court’s decision also “continued to miscite authorities, and [the party’s supplemental brief] even recycled a fabricated quote” from another filing. In response to the court’s show cause order, the law firm conducted an investigation into the offending attorney’s other matters and concluded that she had also included a GenAI-created hallucination in a separate court filing. The attorney eventually admitted that she had “misled the Court” by previously denying the use of GenAI in connection with the filing, when in fact she “was aware [GenAI] was used by someone other than an associate at the Firm.” The court cited Alabama Ethics Rules 1.1, 3.1, and 3.3, 28 U.S.C. § 1927, Bankruptcy Rule 9011, and Bankruptcy Section 105(a), and the court’s inherent authority in concluding that while sanctions were not appropriate for the law firm, “the Court will impose a certification requirement on the Firm” to ensure that it takes further necessary steps to “prevent future abuse” of GenAI. The court did, however, find that sanctions were appropriate against the specific offending attorney, and publicly reprimanded her, ordered her to inform her clients, opposing counsel, and every court in which she is counsel of this opinion within 31 days, revoked the attorney’s pro hac vice status, and ordered the attorney must provide the clerk of court with a listing of jurisdictions in which she is licensed to practice law within 24 hours of the court’s order.

linkToCourtOrder.text: E.D. Wash. – Judge Stan Bastian

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Dam v. Waldron (In re Giga Watt Inc.), No. 2:25-CV-00077-SAB, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 228861 (E.D. Wash. Nov. 20, 2025), the court ruled on the parties’ various appellate motions in a bankruptcy proceeding. Appellee asked the court to issue an order for the pro se appellant to show cause why he should not be sanctioned on the grounds that the appellant made misrepresentations and “submitted fabricated legal theory generated by [GenAI].” The court concluded that in light of the appellant’s pro se status, because the court previously dismissed the action in appellee’s favor, and because there were no allegations that that “cases were completely fabricated or otherwise ‘hallucinated’ by AI,” sanctions were not warranted, and the court denied appellee’s motion.

linkToCourtOrder.text: N.D. Cal. – Magistrate Judge Nathanael M. Cousins

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Buchanan v. Vuori, Inc., Case No. 23-cv-01121-NC (N.D. Cal. Nov. 20, 2025), following the court’s order to show cause why plaintiff’s counsel should not be sanctioned for the inclusion of “false case quotations,” the attorney admitted to using “about six different AI tools to prepare his motion” (“ChatGPT-4, OpenAI, Claude and Claude Code, Clear Brief, Lexis Nexis, and Westlaw,” ECF 92 at 2), but admitted that “the AI tools failed to catch one another’s hallucinations.” The court opined that “[u]sing AI to check the work of AI was not a reasonable inquiry” into the facts and law, and that the attorney violated Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11, Civil Local Rule 11-4, and California Rule of Professional Conduct 3.3. The court made clear that it was not his “use of AI that violated Rule 11, but rather his failure to conduct a reasonably inquiry into the law cited in his motion.” The court therefore imposed the following sanctions: striking plaintiff’s second motion for preliminary approval of a class action settlement and corrected motion without leave to refile; ordered plaintiff’s counsel to pay $250 to the clerk of court; referred counsel to the Court’s Standing Committee on Professional Conduct; and found the attorney inadequate to serve as class counsel.

applicableTo: Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
results.summary: In Cojom v. Roblen, LLC., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 225325 (D. Conn. Nov. 19, 2025), the court imposed a $500 sanction on plaintiff's counsel for relying on citations generated by Descrybe.AI, a legal research tool (Opinion at *1), in his memorandum in opposition to defendant’s motion to set aside default. The court suspected GenAI use because counsel cited three authorities that the court “could not locate despite its best efforts.” In response to Judge Hall's order to show cause, counsel admitted to using GenAI for legal research and expressed “sincere and unreserved apology,” noting that the many familiar cases the tool generated provided him a “false sense of confidence in the AI tool’s accuracy.” This led him to the “deeply flawed” assumption that the rest of the citations were also valid. Counsel stated that he was “committed to ensuring that this type of error does not recur and to maintaining the highest standards of accuracy, candor, and professionalism in [his] future conduct before this Court and all others.” At the show cause hearing, plaintiff’s counsel explained that he cancelled his license for the tool, underwent training on the risks of GenAI use, and committed to cease all use of GenAI in his practice. In imposing sanctions, the court noted the harm flowing from GenAI use in this case, including that the plaintiff was “deprived of arguments based on legitimate judicial precedent and his pro se opponent was forced to contend with arguments based on phony law without the benefit of legal representation,” along with the court’s expended time and resources in investigating the citations. Judge Hall explained that “submitting fake citations is more than just sloppy lawyering: it imperils the integrity of our judicial process.” The court acknowledged that GenAI is increasingly used in legal research and did not intend a “Luddite attack on technology,” but rather a warned that the unsophisticated tool “must necessarily yield to a lawyer’s obligation of candor to the court.” Thus, Judge Hall found that failing to verify the citations violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, and concluded it must sanction such conduct “which, if left unchecked, poses serious risk to the integrity of the judicial system.” After weighing the seriousness of the misconduct against counsel’s apology and steps to prevent error in the future, the court ordered a $500 monetary sanction
